greek_head: Βούλει οὖν καὶ Σωκράτει τῷδε ἀνακοινωσώμεθα τὸν λόγον
greek_tokens: boulomai oun kai swkratew ode anakoinow o logos ei su dokew kratulos fhmi ode eimi swkraths onoma orqoths eimi ekastos o eimi fusis fuw kai ou outos eimi onoma o an tis suntiqhmi kalew kalew o eautou fwnew morion epifqeggomai alla orqoths tis o
english_head: Here is Socrates; shall we take him as a partner in our discussion? If you like. Cratylus, whom you see here, Socrates, says that everything has a right name of its own, which comes by nature, and that a name is not whatever people call a thing by agreement, just a piece of their own voice applied to the thing, but that there is a kind of inherent correctness in names, which is the same for all men,

greek_head: ὀνομάτων πεφυκέναι καὶ Ἕλλησι καὶ βαρβάροις τὴν αὐτὴν ἅπασιν ἐρωτῶ οὖν αὐτὸν ἐγὼ εἰ αὐτῷ Κρατύλος τῇ ἀληθείᾳ
greek_tokens: onoma fuw kai ellhn kai barbaros o autos apas erwtaw oun autos egw ei autos kratulos o alhqeia onoma eimi h ou o de omologew tis de swkratew fhmi swkratew eimi de os oukoun kai o allos anqrwpos pas osper kalew onoma ekastos outos eimi ekastos onoma o de oukoun sos ge eimi de os onoma ermogenhs oude an pas kalew anqrwpos kai egw erwtaw kai proqumeomai oida ostis pote
english_head: both Greeks and barbarians. So I ask him whether his name is in truth Cratylus, and he agrees that it is. And what is Socrates’ name? I said. Socrates, said he. Then that applies to all men, and the particular name by which we call each person is his name? And he said, Well, your name is not Hermogenes, even if all mankind call you so. Now, though I am asking him

greek_head: λέγει οὔτε ἀποσαφεῖ οὐδὲν εἰρωνεύεταί τε πρός με προςποιούμενός τι αὐτὸς ἐν ἑαυτῷ διανοεῖσθαι ὡς εἰδὼς περὶ
greek_tokens: legw oute aposafew oudeis eirwneuomai te pros egw prospoiew tis autos en eautou dianoeomai ws eidoi peri autos o ei boulomai safhs eipon poiew an kai egw omologew kai legw o autos legw ei oun ph exis sumballw o kratulos manteia hdews an akouw mallon de autos su oph dokew exw peri onoma orqoths eti an hdus puqw ei su boulomai eimi eimi pais ipponikos ermogenhs palaios paroimia ostis
english_head: and am exerting myself to find out what in the world he means, he does not explain himself at all; he meets me with dissimulation, claiming to have some special knowledge of his own about it which would, if he chose to speak it out clearly, make me agree entirely with him. Now if you could interpret Cratylus’s oracular speech, I should like to hear you; or rather, I should like still better to hear, if you please, what you yourself think about the correctness of names. Hermogenes, son of Hipponicus, there is an ancient saying

greek_head: χαλεπὰ τὰ καλά ἐστιν ὅπῃ ἔχει μαθεῖν καὶ δὴ καὶ τὸ περὶ τῶν ὀνομάτων οὐ σμικρὸν τυγχάνει ὂν μάθημα εἰ μὲν οὖν
greek_tokens: xalepos o kalos eimi oph exis manqanw kai dh kai o peri o onoma ou mikros tugxanw eimi maqhma ei men oun egw hdh akouw para prodikos o penthkontadraxmos epideicis os akouw uparxw peri outos paideuw os fhmi ekeinos oudeis an kwluw su autika mala oida o alhqeia peri onoma orqoths nun de ou akouw
english_head: that knowledge of high things is hard to gain; and surely knowledge of names is no small matter. Now if I had attended Prodicus’s fifty-drachma course of lectures, after which, as he himself says, a man has a complete education on this subject, there would be nothing to hinder your learning the truth about the correctness of names at once; but I have heard only the one-drachma course,

greek_head: ἀλλὰ τὴν δραχμιαίαν οὔκουν οἶδα πῇ ποτε τὸ ἀληθὲς ἔχει περὶ τῶν τοιούτων συζητεῖν μέντοι ἕτοιμός εἰμι καὶ
greek_tokens: alla o draxmiaios oukoun oida ph pote o alhqhs exis peri o toioutos suzhtew mentoi etoimos eimi kai sos kai kratulos koinh ostis de ou fhmi su ermogenhs onoma eimi o alhqeia wsper upopteuw autos skwptw oiomai gar isos su xrhma efihmi kthsis apotugxanw ekastote alla o nundi elegos oida men o toioutos xalepos eis o koinos de katatiqhmi xrh skopaw eite ws su legw exis eite ws kratulos kai meis egw eimi swkraths pollakis dh kai outos dialegw kai allos polus ou dunamai peiqw
english_head: and so I do not know what the truth is about such matters. However, I am ready to join you and Cratylus in looking for it. But as for his saying that Hermogenes is not truly your name, I suspect he is making fun of you; for perhaps he thinks that you want to make money and fail every time. But, as I said, it is difficult to know such things. We must join forces and try to find out whether you are right, or Cratylus. For my part, Socrates, I have often talked with Cratylus and many others,

greek_head: ὡς ἄλλη τις ὀρθότης ὀνόματος ἢ συνθήκη καὶ ὁμολογία ἐμοὶ γὰρ δοκεῖ ὅτι ἄν τίς τῳ θῆται ὄνομα τοῦτο εἶναι τὸ
greek_tokens: ws allos tis orqoths onoma h sunqhkh kai omologia egw gar dokew ostis an tis tis qaomai onoma outos eimi o orqos kai an auqis ge eteros metaqew ekeinos de mhketi kalew oudeis hsswn o usteron orqos exw o proteros wsper o oikeths egw metatiqhmi oudeis hsswn outos eimi orqos o metatiqhmi o proteros keimai ou gar fusis ekastos fuw onoma oudeis oudeis alla nomos kai eqos o eqizw te kai kalew ei de ph allh
english_head: and cannot come to the conclusion that there is any correctness of names other than convention and agreement. For it seems to me that whatever name you give to a thing is its right name; and if you give up that name and change it for another, the later name is no less correct than the earlier, just as we change the names of our servants; for I think no name belongs to any particular thing by nature, but only by the habit and custom of those who employ it and who established the usage.

greek_head: ἔχει ἕτοιμος ἔγωγε καὶ μανθάνειν καὶ ἀκούειν οὐ μόνον παρὰ Κρατύλου ἀλλὰ καὶ παρ' ἄλλου ὁτουοῦν
greek_tokens: exis etoimos egw kai manqanw kai akouw ou monos para kratulos alla kai para allos ostis
english_head: But if this is not the case, I am ready to hear and to learn from Cratylus or anyone else.

greek_head: Ἴσως μέντοι τὶ λέγεις ὦ Ἑρμόγενες σκεψώμεθα δέ ὃ ἂν φῂς καλῇ τις ἕκαστον τοῦθ' ἑκάστῳ ὄνομα
greek_tokens: isos mentoi tis legw eimi ermogenhs skeptomai de o an fhmi kalew tis ekastos outos ekastos onoma egw dokew kai ean idiwths kalew kai ean polis fhmi tis oun ean egw kalew otioun o eimi oios o nun kalew anqrwpos ean egw outos ippos prosagoreuw o de nun ippos anqrwpos eimi dhmosios men onoma anqrwpos o autos idios de ippos kai idios men au anqrwpos dhmosios de ippos outws legw
english_head: It may be that you are right, Hermogenes; but let us see. Whatever name we decide to give each particular thing is its name? Yes. Whether the giver be a private person or a state? Yes. Well, then, suppose I give a name to some thing or other, designating, for instance, that which we now call man as horse and that which we now call horse as man, will the real name of the same thing be man for the public and horse for me individually, and in the other case horse for the public and man for me individually? Is that your meaning?

greek_head: Ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ Φέρε δή μοι τόδε εἰπέ καλεῖς τι ἀληθῆ λέγειν καὶ
greek_tokens: egw dokew ferw dh egw ode eipon kalew tis alhqhs legw kai yeudhs egw oukoun eaw an logos alhqhs o de yeudhs panu ge ara oun outos os an o eimi legos ws eimi alhqhs os de an ws ou eimi yeudhs nai eimi ara outos logos legw o eimi te kai mh panu ge
english_head: Yes, that is my opinion. Now answer this question. Is there anything which you call speaking the truth and speaking falsehood? Yes. Then there would be true speech and false speech? Certainly. Then that speech which says things as they are is true, and that which says them as they are not is false? Yes. It is possible, then, to say in speech that which is and that which is not? Certainly. But is true speech true only as a whole,

greek_head: Ὁ λόγος δ' ἐστὶν ὁ ἀληθὴς πότερον μὲν ὅλος ἀληθής τὰ μόρια δ' αὐτοῦ οὐκ ἀληθῆ
greek_tokens: o logos de eimi o alhqhs poteros men oloc alhqhs o morion de autos ou alhqhs ou alla kai o morion poteros de o men megas morion alhqhs o de mikros ou h pas pas oimh egw eimi oun ostis legw logos mikros morion allos h onoma ou alla outos mikros kai outos onoma ara o o alhqhs logos legw nai alhqhs ge ws fhmi nai o de o yeudos morion ou yeudos fhmi eimi ara onoma yeudos kai alhqhs legw eiper kai logos
english_head: and are its parts untrue? No, its parts also are true. Are the large parts true, but not the small ones, or are all true? All, in my opinion. Is there, then, anything which you say is a smaller part of speech than a name? No, that is the smallest. And the name is spoken as a part of the true speech? Yes. Then it is, according to you, true. Yes. And a part of false speech is false, is it not? It is. Then it is possible to utter either a false or a true name, since one may utter speech that is either true or false?

greek_head: Πῶς γὰρ οὔ Ὃ ἂν ἄρα ἕκαστος φῇ τῳ ὄνομα εἶναι τοῦτό ἐστιν
greek_tokens: pws gar ou o an ara ekastos fhmi tis onoma eimi outos eimi ekastos onoma nai eimi kai oposos an fhmi tis ekastos onoma eimi tosoutos eimi kai tote opotan fhmi ou gar exw egw eimi swkraths onoma allos orqoths h outos egw men eteros eimi kalew ekastos onoma o egw tiqhmi sos de eteros o au su outws de kai
english_head: Yes, of course. Then whatever each particular person says is the name of anything, that is its name for that person? Yes. And whatever the number of names anyone says a thing has, it will really have that number at the time when he says it? Yes, Socrates, for I cannot conceive of any other kind of correctness in names than this; I may call a thing by one name, which I gave, and you by another, which you gave. And in the same way, I see that states have their own different names for the same things,

greek_head: ταῖς πόλεσιν ὁρῶ ἰδίᾳ ἑκάσταις ἐνίοις ἐπὶ τοῖς αὐτοῖς κείμενα ὀνόματα καὶ Ἕλλησι παρὰ τοὺς ἄλλους Ἕλληνας
greek_tokens: o polis oraw idios ekastos eneimi epi o autos keimai onoma kai ellhn para o allos ellhn kai ellhn para barbaros ferw dh eidon eimi ermogenhs poteros kai o eimi outws exw su fainw idios autos o ousia eimi ekastos wsper prwtagoras legw legos pas xrhma
english_head: and Greeks differ from other Greeks and from barbarians in their use of names. Now, Hermogenes, let us see. Do you think this is true of the real things, that their reality is a separate one for each person, as Protagoras said with his doctrine

greek_head: μέτρον εἶναι ἄνθρωπον ὡς ἄρα οἷα μὲν ἂν ἐμοὶ φαίνηται τὰ πράγματα εἶναι τοιαῦτα μὲν ἔστιν ἐμοί οἷα δ' ἂν
greek_tokens: metron eimi anqrwpos ws ara oios men an egw fainw o pragma eimi toioutos men eimi egw oios de an sos toioutos de sos h exw dokew su autos eautou tis bebaioths o ousia hdh pote egw eimi swkraths aforaw kai entauqa ekferw eis o prwtagoras legw ou panu tis mentoi egw dokew outws exw tis de eis ode hdh ekferw wste mh panu su
english_head: that man is the measure of all things—that things are to me such as they seem to me, and to you such as they seem to you—or do you think things have some fixed reality of their own? It has sometimes happened to me, Socrates, to be so perplexed that I have been carried away even into this doctrine of Protagoras; but I do not at all believe he is right. Well, have you ever been carried away so far

greek_head: δοκεῖν εἶναί τινα ἄνθρωπον πονηρόν Οὐ μὰ τὸν Δία ἀλλὰ πολλάκις δὴ αὐτὸ πέπονθα
greek_tokens: dokew eimi tis anqrwpos ponhros ou ma o dios alla pollakis dh autos pasxw wste egw dokew panu ponhros eimi tis anqrwpos kai mala suxnos tis de panu xrhstos oupw su dokew eimi anqrwpos kai mala oligos dokew de oun egw pws oun outos tiqhmi ara wde o men panu xrhstos panu fronimos o de panu ponhros panu afrwn
english_head: as not to believe at all that any man is bad? Lord, no; but I have often been carried away into the belief that certain men, and a good many of them, are very bad. Well, did you never think any were very good? Very few. But you did think them so? Yes. And what is your idea about that? Are the very good very wise and the very bad very foolish?

greek_head: Ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ οὕτως Οἷόν τε οὖν ἐστιν εἰ Πρωταγόρας ἀληθῆ ἔλεγεν
greek_tokens: egw dokew outws oios te oun eimi ei prwtagoras alhqhs legw kai eimi outos o alhqeia o oios an dokazw ekastos toioutos kai eimi o men egw fronimos eimi o de afrwn ou dhta kai outos ge ws oimh sos panu dokew fronhsis eimi kai afrosunh mh panu dunatos eimi prwtagoras alhqhs legw oudeis gar an pou o alhqeia o eteros o eteros fronimos eaw eiper o an ekastos
english_head: Yes, that is my opinion. Now if Protagoras is right and the truth is as he says, that all things are to each person as they seem to him, is it possible for some of us to be wise and some foolish? No, it is not. And you are, I imagine, strongly of the opinion that if wisdom and folly exist, it is quite impossible that Protagoras is right, for one man would not in reality be at all wiser than another

greek_head: δοκῇ ἑκάστῳ ἀληθῆ ἔσται Ἔστι ταῦτα
greek_tokens: dokazw ekastos alhqhs eimi eimi outos alla meis oude kata euqudhmos ge oimh sos dokew pas pas omoios eimi ama kai aei oude gar an outws eien e men xrhstos e de ponhros ei omoios apas kai aei areth te kai kakia eaw alhqhs legw oukoun ei mhte pas pas eimi omoios ama kai aei mhte ekastos idios ekastos o eimi eimi dhlos dh
english_head: if whatever seems to each person is really true to him. Quite right. But neither do you believe with Euthydemus that all things belong equally to all men at the same time and perpetually, for on this assumption also some could not be good and others bad, if virtue and its opposite were always equally possessed by all. True. Then if neither all things belong equally to all men at the same time and perpetually nor each thing to each man individually, it is clear that things have some fixed reality of their own,

greek_head: ὅτι αὐτὰ αὑτῶν οὐσίαν ἔχοντά τινα βέβαιόν ἐστι τὰ πράγματα οὐ πρὸς ἡμᾶς οὐδὲ ὑφ' ἡμῶν ἑλκόμενα ἄνω καὶ κάτω
greek_tokens: ostis autos eautou ousia exw tis bebaios eimi o pragma ou pros egw oude ufh egw elkw anoos kai katos o hmeteros fantasma alla kaqa eautou pros o eautou ousia exw hper fuw dokew egw eimi swkraths outws poteros oun autos men an eaw outws fuw o de prassw autos ou kata o autos tropos h ou kai outos eis tis eidos o eimi eimi o prassw panu ge kai outos
english_head: not in relation to us nor caused by us; they do not vary, swaying one way and another in accordance with our fancy, but exist of themselves in relation to their own reality imposed by nature. I think, Socrates, that is the case. Can things themselves, then, possess such a nature as this, and that of their actions be different? Or are not actions also a class of realities? Certainly they are.

greek_head: Κατὰ τὴν αὑτῶν ἄρα φύσιν καὶ αἱ πράξεις πράττονται οὐ κατὰ τὴν ἡμετέραν δόξαν οἷον ἐάν τι ἐπιχειρήσωμεν
greek_tokens: kata o eautou ara fus kai o prassw prassw ou kata o hmeteros doca oios ean tis epixeirew egw o eimi temnw poteros egw tmhteon eimi ekastos ws an egw boulomai kai os an boulomai h ean men kata o fus boulomai ekastos temnw o temnw te kai temnw kai os fuw temnw te kai pleos tis egw eimi kai orqos prassw outos ean de para fus ecamartanw te kai oudeis prassw
english_head: Then actions also are performed according to their own nature, not according to our opinion. For instance, if we undertake to cut anything, ought we to cut it as we wish, and with whatever instrument we wish, or shall we, if we are willing to cut each thing in accordance with the nature of cutting and being cut, and with the natural instrument, succeed in cutting it, and do it rightly, whereas if we try to do it contrary to nature we shall fail and accomplish nothing?

greek_head: Ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ οὕτω Οὐκοῦν καὶ ἐὰν κάειν τι ἐπιχειρήσωμεν οὐ κατὰ
greek_tokens: egw dokew outws oukoun kai ean kaiw tis epixeirew ou kata pas doca deomai kaiw alla kata o orqos outos de eimi h fuw ekastos kaiw te kai kaiw kai os fuw eimi outos oukoun kai allos outws panu ge ara oun ou kai o legw eis tis o pracis eimi nai poteros oun h an tis dokazw lekteos eimi outos
english_head: I think the way is as you suggest. Then, too, if we undertake to burn anything, we must burn not according to every opinion, but according to the right one? And that is as each thing naturally burns or is burned and with the natural instrument? True. And all other actions are to be performed In like manner? Certainly. And speaking is an action, is it not? Yes. Then if a man speaks as he fancies he ought to speak,

greek_head: λέγων ὀρθῶς λέξει ἢ ἐὰν μὲν ᾗ πέφυκε τὰ πράγματα λέγειν τε καὶ λέγεσθαι καὶ ᾧ ταύτῃ καὶ τούτῳ λέγῃ πλέον τέ τι
greek_tokens: legos orqos lecis h ean men h fuw o pragma legw te kai legw kai os outos kai outos legos pleos su tis poihsis kai eraomai an de mh ecamartanw te kai oudeis poihsis outws egw dokew ws legw oukoun o legw morion o onomazw dionomazw gar pou legw o logos panu ge oukoun kai o onomazw pracis tis eimi eiper kai o legw pracis tis eimi peri o pragma nai
english_head: will he speak rightly, or will he succeed in speaking if he speaks in the way and with the instrument in which and with which it is natural for us to speak and for things to be spoken, whereas otherwise he will fail and accomplish nothing? I think the way you suggest is the right one. Now naming is a part of speaking, for in naming I suppose people utter speech. Certainly. Then is not naming also a kind of action, if speaking is a kind of action concerned with things? Yes.

greek_head: Αἱ δὲ πράξεις ἐφάνησαν ἡμῖν οὐ πρὸς ἡμᾶς οὖσαι ἀλλ' αὑτῶν τινα ἰδίαν φύσιν ἔχουσαι
greek_tokens: o de prassw fainw egw ou pros egw eimi alla eautou tis idios fus exw eimi outos oukoun kai onomasteon eimi h fuw o pragma onomazw te kai onomazw kai os alla ou h an egw boulomai eiper tis o emprosqen mellw omologew eimi kai outws men an pleos tis poiew kai onomazw allos de ou fainw egw ferw dh o dew temnw dew tis fhmi temnw nai
english_head: But we saw that actions are not merely relative to us, but possess a separate nature of their own? True. Then in naming also, if we are to be consistent with our previous conclusions, we cannot follow our own will, but the way and the instrument which the nature of things prescribes must be employed, must they not? And if we pursue this course we shall be successful in our naming, but otherwise we shall fail. I think you are right. And again, what has to be cut, we said, has to be cut with something. Certainly.

greek_head: Καὶ ὃ ἔδει κερκίζειν ἔδει τῳ κερκίζειν καὶ ὃ ἔδει τρυπᾶν ἔδει τῳ τρυπᾶν
greek_tokens: kai o dew kerkizw dew tis kerkizw kai o dew truph dew tis truph panu ge kai o dew dh onomazw dew tis onomazw
english_head: And what has to be woven, has to be woven with something, and what has to be bored, has to be bored with something? Certainly. And then what has to be named, has to be named with something?

greek_head: Ἔστι ταῦτα Τί δὲ ἦν ἐκεῖνο ᾧ ἔδει τρυπᾶν
greek_tokens: eimi outos tis de eimi ekeinos os dew truph trupanon tis de os kerkizw kerkis tis de os onomazw onoma eu legw organon ara tis eimi kai o onoma panu ge ei oun egw eromai tis eimi organon o kerkis ou os kerkizw nai
english_head: True. And what is that with which we have to bore? A borer. And that with which we weave? A shuttle. And that with which we must name? A name. Right. A name also, then, is a kind of instrument. Certainly. Then if I were to ask What instrument is the shuttle? Is it not that with which we weave? Yes.

greek_head: Κερκίζοντες δὲ τί δρῶμεν οὐ τὴν κρόκην καὶ τοὺς στήμονας συγκεχυμένους διακρίνομεν
greek_tokens: kerkizw de tis draw ou o krokh kai o sthmwn sugxew diakrinw nai oukoun kai peri trupanon ecis outws eipon kai peri o allos panu ge exis dh kai peri onoma outws eipon organon eimi o onoma onomazw tis poiew ou exw legw ara ou didaskw tis allhlwn kai o pragma diakrinw h exis panu ge onoma ara didaskalikos tis eimi organon kai
english_head: And what do we do when we weave? Do we not separate the mingled threads of warp and woof? Yes. And you could give a similar answer about the borer and the rest, could you not? Certainly. And can you say something of the same kind about a name? The name being an instrument, what do we do with it when we name? I cannot tell. Do we not teach one another something, and separate things according to their natures? Certainly. A name is, then, an instrument of teaching and of separating reality,

greek_head: διακριτικὸν τῆς οὐσίας ὥσπερ κερκὶς ὑφάσματος Ναί
greek_tokens: diakritikos o ousia wsper kerkis ufasma nai ufantikos de ge o kerkis pws de ou ufantikos men ara kerkis kalos xraw kalos de eimi ufantikos didaskalikos de onoma kalos de eimi didaskalikos nai o tis oun ergon o ufanths kalos xraw otan o kerkis xraomai o o tektwn pas de tektwn h o o texnh exw o o texnh
english_head: as a shuttle is an instrument of separating the web? Yes. But the shuttle is an instrument of weaving? Of course. The weaver, then, will use the shuttle well, and well means like a weaver; and a teacher will use a name well, and well means like a teacher. Yes. Whose work will the weaver use well when he uses the shuttle? The carpenter’s. Is every one a carpenter, or he who has the skill? He who has the skill.

greek_head: Τῷ τίνος δὲ ἔργῳ ὁ τρυπητὴς καλῶς χρήσεται ὅταν τῷ τρυπάνῳ χρῆται
greek_tokens: o tis de ergon o truphths kalos xraw otan o trupanon xraomai o o xalkeos ara oun pas xalkeus h o o texnh exw o o texnh eien o de tis ergon o didaskalikos xraw otan o onoma xraomai oude outos exw oude outos ge exis eipon tis paradidwmi egw o onoma os xraomai ou dhta ara ou o nomos dokew su eimi o paradidwmi autos eoika
english_head: And whose work will the hole-maker use when he uses the borer? The smith’s. And is every one a smith, or he who has the skill? He who has the skill. And whose work will the teacher use when he uses the name? I cannot tell that, either. And can you not tell this, either, who gives us the names we use? No. Do you not think it is the law that gives them to us? Very likely.

greek_head: Νομοθέτου ἄρα ἔργῳ χρήσεται ὁ διδασκαλικὸς ὅταν ὀνόματι χρῆται
greek_tokens: nomoqeths ara ergon xraw o didaskalikos otan onoma xraomai dokew egw nomoqeths de su dokew pas eimi anhr h o o texnh exw o o texnh ou ara pas anhr eimi ermogenhs onoma tiqhmi
english_head: Then the teacher, when he uses a name, will be using the work of a lawgiver? I think so. Do you think every man is a lawgiver, or only he who has the skill? He who has the skill. Then it is not for every man, Hermogenes,

greek_head: ἐστὶν ἀλλά τινος ὀνοματουργοῦ οὗτος δ' ἐστίν ὡς ἔοικεν ὁ νομοθέτης ὃς δὴ τῶν δημιουργῶν σπανιώτατος ἐν ἀνθρώποις
greek_tokens: eimi alla tis onomatourgew outos de eimi ws eoika o nomoqeths os dh o dhmiourgew spanios en anqrwpos gignomai eoika eimi dh episkeptomai poi blepw o nomoqeths o onoma tiqhmi ek o emprosqen de anaskeptomai poi blepw o tektwn o kerkis poiew ara ou pros toioutos tis o fuw kerkizw panu ge
english_head: to give names, but for him who may be called the name-maker; and he, it appears, is the lawgiver, who is of all the artisans among men the rarest. So it appears. See now what the lawgiver has in view in giving names. Look at it in the light of what has gone before. What has the carpenter in view when he makes a shuttle? Is it not something the nature of which is to weave? Certainly. Well, then, if the shuttle breaks while he making it,

greek_head: Τί δέ ἂν καταγῇ αὐτῷ ἡ κερκὶς ποιοῦντι πότερον πάλιν ποιήσει ἄλλην πρὸς τὴν κατεαγυῖαν βλέπων ἢ πρὸς
greek_tokens: tis de an kaqhgeomai autos o kerkis poiew poteros palin poihsis allos pros o katagnumi blepw h pros ekeinos o eidos pros osper kai os katagnumi poiew pros ekeinos egw dokew oukoun ekeinos dikaios an autos o eimi kerkis kalew egw dokew oukoun epeidan deomai lepton imation h paxus h lineos h ereous h opoios tis kerkis poiew passw men deomai o o kerkis exw eidos oios de ekastos kalos fuw
english_head: will he make another with his mind fixed on that which is broken, or on that form with reference to which he was making the one which he broke? On that form, in my opinion. Then we should very properly call that the absolute or real shuttle? Yes, I think so. Then whenever he has to make a shuttle for a light or a thick garment, or for one of linen or of wool or of any kind whatsoever, all of them must contain the form or ideal of shuttle,

greek_head: ταύτην ἀποδιδόναι τὴν φύσιν εἰς τὸ ἔργον ἕκαστον Ναί
greek_tokens: outos apodidwmi o fus eis o ergnumi ekastos nai kai peri o allos dh organon o autos tropos o fusis ekastos fuw organon eceuriskw deomai apodidwmi eis ekeinos ek e an poiew o ergnumi ou oios an autos boulomai alla oios fuw o fusis gar ekastos ws eoika trupanon fuw eis o sidhron deomai efisthmi tiqhmi panu ge kai o fusis kerkis ekastos fuw eis culon eimi outos
english_head: and in each of his products he must embody the nature which is naturally best for each? Yes. And the same applies to all other instruments. The artisan must discover the instrument naturally fitted for each purpose and must embody that in the material of which he makes the instrument, not in accordance with his own will, but in accordance with its nature. He must, it appears, know how to embody in the iron the borer fitted by nature for each special use. Certainly. And he must embody in the wood the shuttle fitted by nature for each kind of weaving. True.

greek_head: Φύσει γὰρ ἦν ἑκάστῳ εἴδει ὑφάσματος ὡς ἔοικεν ἑκάστη κερκίς καὶ τἆλλα οὕτως
greek_tokens: fusis gar eimi ekastos eidomai ufasma ws eoika ekastos kerkis kai allos outws nai ara oun eimi beltistos kai o ekastos fusis fuw onoma o nomoqeths ekeinos eis o fqoggos kai o sullabh deomai efisthmi tiqhmi kai blepw pros autos ekeinos o eimi onoma pas o onoma poiew te kai tiqhmi ei mellw kurios eimi onoma qeths ei de mh eis o autos sullabh ekastos o nomoqeths tiqhmi oudeis
english_head: For each kind of shuttle is, it appears, fitted by nature for its particular kind of weaving, and the like is true of other instruments. Yes. Then, my dear friend, must not the law-giver also know how to embody in the sounds and syllables that name which is fitted by nature for each object? Must he not make and give all his names with his eye fixed upon the absolute or ideal name, if he is to be an authoritative giver of names? And if different lawgivers do not embody it in the same syllables, we must not forget this ideal name on that account; for different smiths do not embody the form in the same iron,

greek_head: δεῖ τοῦτο ἀμφιγνοεῖν οὐδὲ γὰρ εἰς τὸν αὐτὸν σίδηρον ἅπας χαλκεὺς τίθησιν τοῦ αὐτοῦ ἕνεκα ποιῶν τὸ αὐτὸ
greek_tokens: deomai outos amfignoew oude gar eis o autos sidhron apas xalkeus tiqhmi o autos eneka poa o autos organon alla omws ews an o autos idea apodidwmi ean
english_head: though making the same instrument for the same purpose, but so long as they reproduce the same ideal,

greek_head: ἐν ἄλλῳ σιδήρῳ ὅμως ὀρθῶς ἔχει τὸ ὄργανον ἐάντε ἐνθάδε ἐάντε ἐν βαρβάροις τις ποιῇ ἦ γάρ
greek_tokens: en allos sidhron omws orqos exis o organon ean enqade ean en barbaros tis poiew eimi gar panu ge oukoun outws aciwsis kai o nomoqeths o te enqade kai o en o barbaros ews an o o onoma eidos apodidwmi o proshkw ekastos en opoios sullabh oudeis xeirwn nomoqeths eimi o enqade h o opou alloqi panu ge
english_head: though it be in different iron, still the instrument is as it should be, whether it be made here or in foreign lands, is it not? Certainly. On this basis, then, you will judge the law-giver, whether he be here or in a foreign land, so long as he gives to each thing the proper form of the name, in whatsoever syllables, to be no worse lawgiver, whether here or anywhere else, will you not? Certainly.

greek_head: Τίς οὖν ὁ γνωσόμενος εἰ τὸ προσῆκον εἶδος κερκίδος ἐν ὁποιῳοῦν ξύλῳ κεῖται ὁ ποιήσας ὁ τέκτων ἢ ὁ χρησόμενος
greek_tokens: tis oun o gignwskw ei o proshkw eidos kerkis en opoios culon kew o poiew o tektwn h o xraw o ufanths eoika men mallon eimi swkraths o xraw tis oun o o o luropoios ergon xraw ara ou outos os efisthmi an ergazomai kallista epistatew kai ergazomai gignwskw eite eu ergazomai eite mh panu ge tis o kiqarisths tis de o o o nauphgew
english_head: Now who is likely to know whether the proper form of shuttle is embodied in any piece of wood? The carpenter who made it, or the weaver who is to use it ? Probably the one who is to use it, Socrates. Then who is to use the work of the lyre-maker? Is not he the man who would know best how to superintend the making of the lyre and would also know whether it is well made or not when it is finished? Certainly. Who is he? The lyre-player. And who would know best about the work of the ship-builder?

greek_head: Κυβερνήτης Τίς δὲ τῷ τοῦ νομοθέτου ἔργῳ ἐπιστατήσειέ τ' ἂν
greek_tokens: kubernhths tis de o o nomoqeths ergon epistatew su an kallista kai ergazomai krinw kai enqade kai en o barbaros ara ou os xraw nai ara oun ou o erwtaw efisthmi outos eimi panu ge o de autos kai apokrinw nai o de erwtaw kai apokrinw efisthmi allos tis su kalew h dialektikos ou alla outos
english_head: The navigator. And who can best superintend the work of the lawgiver and judge of it when it is finished, both here and in foreign countries? The user, is it not? Yes. And is not this he who knows how to ask questions? Certainly. And the same one knows also how to make replies? Yes. And the man who knows how to ask and answer questions you call a dialectician? Yes, that is what I call him.

greek_head: Τέκτονος μὲν ἄρα ἔργον ἐστὶν ποιῆσαι πηδάλιον ἐπιστατοῦντος κυβερνήτου εἰ μέλλει καλὸν εἶναι τὸ πηδάλιον
greek_tokens: tektwn men ara ergnumi eimi poiew phdalion epistatew kubernhths ei mellw kalos eimi o phdalion fainw nomoqeths de ge ws eoika onoma efisthmi exw dialektikos anhr ei mellw kalos onoma tiqhmi eimi outos kinduneuw ara eimi ermogenhs eimi ou faulos ws su oiomai o o onoma qesis oude faulos androw oude o epitugxanw kai kratulos alhqhs legw legos fusis
english_head: The work of the carpenter, then, is to make a rudder under the supervision of the steersman, if he rudder is to be a good one. Evidently. And the work of the lawgiver, as it seems, is to make a name, with the dialectician as his supervisor, if names are to be well given. True. Then, Hermogenes, the giving of names can hardly be, as you imagine, a trifling matter, or a task for trifling or casual persons: and Cratylus is right in saying that names belong to things by nature

greek_head: τὰ ὀνόματα εἶναι τοῖς πράγμασι καὶ οὐ πάντα δημιουργὸν ὀνομάτων εἶναι ἀλλὰ μόνον ἐκεῖνον τὸν ἀποβλέποντα εἰς τὸ
greek_tokens: o onoma eimi o pragma kai ou pas dhmiourgos onoma eimi alla monos ekeinos o apoblepw eis o o fusis onoma eimi ekastos kai dunamai autos o eidos tiqhmi eis te o gramma kai o sullabh ou exw eimi swkraths opws xrh pros o legw
english_head: and that not every one is an artisan of names, but only he who keeps in view the name which belongs by nature to each particular thing and is able to embody its form in the letters and syllables. I do not know how to answer you, Socrates; nevertheless it is not easy to change my conviction suddenly.

greek_head: ἐναντιοῦσθαι ἴσως μέντοι οὐ ῥᾴδιόν ἐστιν οὕτως ἐξαίφνης πεισθῆναι ἀλλὰ δοκῶ μοι ὧδε ἂν μᾶλλον πιθέσθαι σοι εἴ
greek_tokens: enantioomai isos mentoi ou radios eimi outws ecaifnhs peiqw alla dokazw egw wde an mallon peiqw su ei egw deiknumi ostis fhmi eimi o fusis orqoths onoma egw men eimi makarios ermogenhs oudeis legos alla epilanqanomai ge os oligos proteros elegos ostis ou oida alla skeptomai meta sew nun de skopaw egw egw te kai sos tosoutos men hdh fainw para o proteros fusis su tis orqoths exw eimi o onoma kai ou
english_head: I think you would be more likely to convince me, if you were to show me just what it is that you say is the natural correctness of names. I, my dear Hermogenes, do not say that there is any. You forget what I said a while ago, that I did not know, but would join you in looking for the truth. And now, as we are looking, you and I, we already see one thing we did not know before, that names do possess a certain natural correctness, and that not every man knows

greek_head: παντὸς ἀνδρὸς ἐπίστασθαι καλῶς αὐτὸ πράγματι ὁτῳοῦν θέσθαι ἢ οὔ
greek_tokens: pas anhr efisthmi kalos autos pragma ostis tiqhmi h ou panu ge oukoun o meta outos xrh zhtew eiper epiqumew oida ostis pote au eimi autos o orqoths alla meis epiqumew ge oida skopaw toinun pws oun xrh skopaw orqos men o skeyis eimi etairos meta o efisthmi xrhma ekeinos tellw kai xaris katatiqhmi eimi de outos e sofisths os kai o adelfos
english_head: how to give a name well to anything whatsoever. Is not that true? Certainly. Then our next task is to try to find out, if you care to know about it, what kind of correctness that is which belongs to names. To be sure I care to know. Then investigate. How shall I investigate? The best way to investigate, my friend, is with the help of those who know; and you make sure of their favour by paying them money. They are the sophists,

greek_head: σου Καλλίας πολλὰ τελέσας χρήματα σοφὸς δοκεῖ εἶναι ἐπειδὴ δὲ οὐκ ἐγκρατὴς εἶ τῶν πατρῴων λιπαρεῖν χρὴ τὸν
greek_tokens: su kallia polus telew xrhma sofos dokew eimi epei de ou egkraths ei o patrwos liparew xrh o adelfos kai deomai autos didaskw su o orqoths peri o toioutos os manqanw para prwtagoras atopos mentoi eaw egw eimi swkraths o dehsis ei o men alhqeia o prwtagoras oloc ou apodexomai o de o toioutos alhqeia erw agapaw os tis acios alla ei mh au su outos areskw para omhros xrh
english_head: of Protagoras altogether, should desire what is said in such a Truth, as if it were of any value. Then if you do not like that,

greek_head: μανθάνειν καὶ παρὰ τῶν ἄλλων ποιητῶν Καὶ τί λέγει ὦ Σώκρατες Ὅμηρος περὶ ὀνομάτων
greek_tokens: manqanw kai para o allos poihths kai tis legw eimi swkraths omhros peri onoma kai pou pollaxou megas de kai kallista en os diorizw epi o autos o te e anqrwpos onoma kalew kai e qeos h ou oiomai autos megas tis kai qaumasios legw en outos peri onoma orqoths dhlos gar dh ostis e ge qeos autos kalew pros orqoths o eimi fusis onoma
english_head: you ought to learn from Homer and the other poets. Why, Socrates, what does Homer say about names, and where? In many passages; but chiefly and most admirably in those in which he distinguishes between the names by which gods and men call the same things. Do you not think he gives in those passages great and wonderful information about the correctness of names? For clearly the gods call things

greek_head: ἢ σὺ οὐκ οἴει Εὖ οἶδα μὲν οὖν ἔγωγε εἴπερ καλοῦσιν ὅτι ὀρθῶς
greek_tokens: h su ou oiomai eu oida men oun egw eiper kalew ostis orqos kalew alla poios outos legw ou oida ostis peri o potamos o en o troia os monomaxew o hfaistos os canqos fhmi kalew qeos anhr de skamandros egw
english_head: by the names that are naturally right. Do you not think so? Of course I know that if they call things, they call them rightly. But what are these instances to which you refer? Do you not know that he says about the river in Troy which had the single combat with Hephaestus,whom the gods call Xanthus, but men call ScamanderHom. Il. 20.74? Oh yes.

greek_head: Τί οὖν δή οὐκ οἴει τοῦτο σεμνόν τι εἶναι γνῶναι ὅπῃ ποτὲ ὀρθῶς ἔχει ἐκεῖνον τὸν ποταμὸν Ξάνθον καλεῖν
greek_tokens: tis oun dh ou oiomai outos semnon tis eimi gignwskw oph pote orqos exis ekeinos o potamos canqos kalew mallon h skamandros ei de boulomai peri o ornis os legw ostis xalkis kiklhskw qeos anhr de kumindis faulos hgeomai o maqhma osos orqos eimi kalew xalkis kumindis o autos orneon h o batieia te kai murinhs
english_head: Well, do you not think this is a grand thing to know, that the name of that river is rightly Xanthus, rather than Scamander? Or, if you like, do you think it is a slight thing to learn about the bird which he says gods call chalcis, but men call cymindis,Hom. Il. 14.291 that it is much more correct for the same bird to be called chalcis than cymindis? Or to learn that the hill men call Batieia is called by the gods Myrina’s tomb, and many other such statements by Homer and other poets?

greek_head: καὶ ἄλλα πολλὰ καὶ τούτου τοῦ ποιητοῦ καὶ ἄλλων ἀλλὰ ταῦτα μὲν ἴσως μείζω ἐστὶν ἢ κατ' ἐμὲ καὶ σὲ ἐξευρεῖν
greek_tokens: kai allos polus kai outos o poihths kai allos alla outos men isos megas eimi h kata egw kai sos eceuriskw o de skamandrios te kai o astuanac anqrwpinos diaskeptomai ws egw dokew kai raios o fhmi onoma eimi o o ektwr uios tis pote legw o orqoths autos oida gar dhpou outos o epos en os eneimi o egw legos panu ge poteros oun oiomai omhros orqos hgeomai o onoma kew o pais o astuanac h o skamandrios
english_head: But perhaps these matters are too high for us to understand; it is, I think, more within human power to investigate the names Scamandrius and Astyanax, and understand what kind of correctness he ascribes to these, which he says are the names of Hector’s son. You recall, of course: the lines which contain the words to which I refer. Certainly. Which of the names of the boy do you imagine Homer thought was more correct, Astyanax or Scamandrius?

greek_head: Οὐκ ἔχω λέγειν Ὧδε δὴ σκόπει εἴ τις ἔροιτό σε πότερον οἴει
greek_tokens: ou exw legw wde dh skopaw ei tis eromai su poteros oiomai orqos kalew o onoma o fronimos h o afrwn dhlos dh ostis o fronimos fhmi an poteros oun o gunh en o polis fronimos su dokew eimi h e anhr ws o oloc eipon genos e anhr oukoun oida ostis omhros o paidion o o ektwr
english_head: I cannot say. Look at it in this way: suppose you were asked, Do the wise or the unwise give names more correctly? The wise, obviously, I should say. And do you think the women or the men of a city, regarded as a class in general, are the wiser? The men. And do you not know that Homer says the child of Hector was called Astyanax by the men of Troy;

greek_head: ὑπὸ τῶν Τρώων φησὶν καλεῖσθαι Ἀστυάνακτα Σκαμάνδριον δὲ δῆλον ὅτι ὑπὸ τῶν γυναικῶν ἐπειδὴ οἵ γε ἄνδρες αὐτὸν
greek_tokens: upo o trws fhmi kalew astuanac skamandrios de dhlos ostis upo o gunaikow epei e ge anhr autos astuanac kalew eoika ge oukoun kai omhros o trws sofos ageomai h o gunh autos oimh egw o astuanac ara orqos oiomai kew o pais h o skamandrios fainw skopaw dh dia tis pote h autos egw kallista ufhgeomai o dioti fhmi gar
english_head: so he must have been called Scamandrius by the women, since the men called him Astyanax? Yes, probably. And Homer too thought the Trojan men were wiser than the women? I suppose he did. Then he thought Astyanax was more rightly the boy’s name than Scamandrius? So it appears. Let us, then, consider the reason for this. Does he not himself indicate the reason most admirably? For he says—

greek_head: οἶος γάρ σφιν ἔρυτο πόλιν καὶ τείχεα μακρά διὰ ταῦτα δή ὡς ἔοικεν ὀρθῶς ἔχει καλεῖν τὸν τοῦ σωτῆρος
greek_tokens: oios gar sfeis ruomai polis kai teixos makros dia outos dh ws eoika orqos exis kalew o o swthr uios astuanac outos o swzw o pathr autos os fhmi omhros fainw egw tis dh pote ou gar pw oude autos egw manqanw eimi ermogenhs su de manqanw ma diw ou egw
english_head: He alone defended their city and long walls.Hom. Il. 22.507 Therefore, as it seems, it is right to call the son of the defender Astyanax (Lord of the city), ruler of that which his father, as Homer says, defended. That is clear to me. Indeed? I do not yet understand about it myself, Hermogenes. Do you? No, by Zeus, I do not.

greek_head: Ἀλλ' ἆρα ὠγαθέ καὶ τῷ Ἕκτορι αὐτὸς ἔθετο τὸ ὄνομα Ὅμηρος
greek_tokens: alla ara agaqos kai o ektwr autos eqw o onoma omhros tis dh ostis egw dokew kai outos paraplhsios tis eimi o astuanac kai eoika ellhnikos outos o onoma o gar anac kai o ektwr sxedon tis tautos shmainw basilikos amfoteros eimi o onoma e gar an tis anac eimi kai ektwr dhpou eimi outos dhlos gar ostis kratew
english_head: But, my good friend, did not Homer himself also give Hector his name? Why do you ask that? Because that name seems to me similar to Astyanax, and both names seem to be Greek. For lord (ἄναξ) and holder (ἕκτωρ) mean nearly the same thing, indicating that they are names of a king; for surely a man is holder of that of which he is lord; for it is clear that he rules it and possesses it and holds it.

greek_head: τε αὐτοῦ καὶ κέκτηται καὶ ἔχει αὐτό ἢ οὐδέν σοι δοκῶ λέγειν ἀλλὰ λανθάνω καὶ ἐμαυτὸν οἰόμενός τινος ὥσπερ
greek_tokens: te autos kai ktaomai kai exis autos h oudeis su dokazw legw alla lanqanw kai emautou oiomai tis wsper ixnos efaptw o omhros doca peri onoma orqoths ma diw ou su ge ws egw dokew alla isos o efaptw dikaios ge o eimi ws egw fainw o lewn ekgonos lewn kalew kai o ippos ekgonos ippos ou tis legos ean wsper teras gignomai ek ippos allos tis h ippos
english_head: Or does it seem to you that there is nothing in what I am saying, and am I wrong in imagining that I have found a clue to Homer’s opinion about the correctness of names? No, by Zeus, you are not wrong, in my opinion; I think perhaps you have found a clue. It is right, I think, to call a lion’s offspring a lion and a horse’s offspring a horse. I am not speaking of prodigies, such as the birth of some other kind of creature from a horse,

greek_head: ἀλλ' οὗ ἂν ᾖ τοῦ γένους ἔκγονον τὴν φύσιν τοῦτο λέγω ἐὰν βοὸς ἔκγονον φύσει ἵππος παρὰ φύσιν τέκῃ μόσχον οὐ
greek_tokens: alla e an eimi o genos ekgonos o fus outos legos ean bous ekgonos fusis ippos para fus tiktw mosxos ou pwlos klhteos alla mosxos oude an ek anqrwpos oimh mh o anqrwpos ekgonos gignomai alla o an o ekgonos anqrwpos klhteos kai o dendron wsautws kai allos apas h ou sundokew sundokew kalos legw fulassw gar egw mh ph parakrouw su kata gar o autos logos kan ek basileus gignomai tis
english_head: but of the natural offspring of each species after its kind. If a horse, contrary to nature, should bring forth a calf, the natural offspring of a cow, it should be called a calf, not a colt, nor if any offspring that is not human should be born from a human being, should that other offspring be called a human being; and the same applies to trees and all the rest. Do you not agree? Yes. Good; but keep watch of me, and do not let me trick you; for by the same argument any offspring of a king should be called a king;

greek_head: ἔκγονον βασιλεὺς κλητέος εἰ δὲ ἐν ἑτέραις συλλαβαῖς ἢ ἐν ἑτέραις τὸ αὐτὸ σημαίνει οὐδὲν πρᾶγμα οὐδ' εἰ πρόσκειταί
greek_tokens: ekgonos basileus klhteos ei de en eteros sullabh h en eteros o autos shmainw oudeis pragma oude ei proskeimai tis gramma h afairew oudeis oude outos ews an egkraths eimi o ousia o pragma dhleomai en o onoma pws outos legw oudeis poikilos alla wsper o stoixeion oida ostis onoma legw alla ou autos o stoixeion plhn tessares o e kai o u kai o o kai o w o de
english_head: and whether the same meaning is expressed in one set of syllables or another makes no difference; and if a letter is added or subtracted, that does not matter either, so long as the essence of the thing named remains in force and is made plain in the name. What do you mean? Something quite simple. For instance, when we speak of the letters of the alphabet, you know, we speak their names, not merely the letters themselves, except in the case of four, ε, υ, ο, ω.

greek_head: ἄλλοις φωνήεσί τε καὶ ἀφώνοις οἶσθα ὅτι περιτιθέντες ἄλλα γράμματα λέγομεν ὀνόματα ποιοῦντες ἀλλ' ἕως ἂν αὐτοῦ
greek_tokens: allos fwnhesi te kai afwnos oida ostis peritiqhmi allos gramma legw onoma poiew alla ews an autos dhleomai o dunamis entiqhmi orqos exis ekeinos o onoma kalew o autos egw dhlwsis oios o bhta oraw ostis o hta kai o tau kai o alfa prostiqhmi oudeis lupew wste mh ou o ekeinos o stoixeion fus dhlow oloc o onoma e boulomai o nomoqeths outws apistew kalos tiqhmi o gramma o onoma alhqhs egw dokew legw
english_head: We make names for all the other vowels and consonants by adding other letters to them; and so long as we include the letter in question and make its force plain, we may properly call it by that name, and that will designate it for us. Take beta, for instance, The addition of e(η), t(τ), a(α) does no harm and does not prevent the whole name from making clear the nature of that letter which the lawgiver wished to designate; he knew so well how to give names to letters. I think you are right. Does not the same reasoning apply to a king?

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν καὶ περὶ βασιλέως ὁ αὐτὸς λόγος ἔσται γάρ ποτε ἐκ βασιλέως βασιλεύς καὶ ἐξ ἀγαθοῦ ἀγαθός καὶ
greek_tokens: oukoun kai peri basileus o autos logos eimi gar pote ek basileus basileus kai ek agaqos agaqos kai ek kalew kalos kai allos pas outws ek ekastos genos eteros toioutos ekgonos ean mh teras gignomai klhteos dh tautos onoma poikillw de ecesti o sullabh wste doca an o idiwtikos exw eteros eimi allhlwn o autos eimi wsper egw o o iatros farmakon xrwma kai osmh poikillw allos fainw o autos eimi o de ge
english_head: A king’s son will probably be a king, a good man’s good, a handsome man’s handsome, and so forth; the offspring of each class will be of the same class, unless some unnatural birth takes place; so they should be called by the same names. But variety in the syllables is admissible, so that names which are the same appear different to the uninitiated, just as the physicians’ drugs, when prepared with various colors and perfumes, seem different to us, though they are the same, but to the physician,

greek_head: ἰατρῷ ἅτε τὴν δύναμιν τῶν φαρμάκων σκοπουμένῳ τὰ αὐτὰ φαίνεται καὶ οὐκ ἐκπλήττεται ὑπὸ τῶν προσόντων οὕτω
greek_tokens: iatros ate o dunamis o farmakon skopaw o autos fainw kai ou ekplhssw upo o proseimi outws de isos kai o efisthmi peri onoma o dunamis autos skopaw kai ou ekplhssw ei tis proskeimai gramma h metakeimai h afairew h kai en allos pantapasi gramma eimi o o onoma dunamis wsper o nundi legw astuanac te kai ektwr oudeis o autos
english_head: who considers only their medicinal value, they seem the same, and he is not confused by the additions. So perhaps the man who knows about names considers their value and is not confused if some letter is added, transposed, or subtracted, or even if the force of the name is expressed in entirely different letters. So, for instance, in the names we were just discussing, Astyanax and Hector, none of the letters is the same, except T,

greek_head: γραμμάτων ἔχει πλὴν τοῦ ταῦ ἀλλ' ὅμως ταὐτὸν σημαίνει καὶ Ἀρχέπολίς γε τῶν μὲν γραμμάτων τί ἐπικοινωνεῖ
greek_tokens: gramma exis plhn o tau alla omws tautos shmainw kai arxepolis ge o men gramma tis epikoinwnew dhlow de omws o autos kai allos polus eimi o oudeis alla h basileus shmainw kai allos ge au strathgos oios agis kai polemarxos kai eupolemos kai iatrikos ge eteros iatroklehs kai akesimbrotos kai eteros an isos suxnos euriskw o men sullabh kai o gramma diafwnew o de dunamis tautos fqeggomai fainw outws h ou
english_head: but nevertheless they have the same meaning. And what letters has Archepolis (ruler of the city) in common with them? Yet it means the same thing; and there are many other names which mean simply king. Others again mean general, such as Agis (leader), Polemarchus (war-lord), and Eupolemus (good warrior); and others indicate physicians, as Iatrocles (famous physician) and Acesimbrotus (healer of mortals); and we might perhaps find many others which differ in syllables and letters, but express the same meaning. Do you think that is true, or not?

greek_head: Πάνυ μὲν οὖν Τοῖς μὲν δὴ κατὰ φύσιν γιγνομένοις τὰ αὐτὰ
greek_tokens: panu men oun o men dh kata fus gignomai o autos apodoteos onoma panu ge tis de o para fus e an en teras eidomai gignomai oios otan ek anhr agaqos kai qeosebhs asebhs gignomai ara ou wsper en o emprosqen kan ippos bous ekgonos tiktw ou o tiktw dhpou dew o epwnumios exw alla o genos e eaw panu ge
english_head: Certainly. To those, then, who are born in accordance with nature the same names should be given. Yes. And how about those who are born contrary to nature as prodigies? For instance, when an impious son is born to a good and pious man, ought he not, as in our former example when a mare brought forth a calf, to have the designation of the class to which he belongs, instead of that of his parent? Certainly.

greek_head: Καὶ τῷ ἐκ τοῦ εὐσεβοῦς ἄρα γενομένῳ ἀσεβεῖ τὸ τοῦ γένους ὄνομα ἀποδοτέον
greek_tokens: kai o ek o eusebhs ara gignomai asebew o o genos onoma apodoteos eimi outos ou qeofilos ws eoika oude mnhsiqeos oude o toioutos oudeis alla ostis enantios outos shmainw ean o orqoths tugxanw o onoma pas ge mallon eimi swkraths wsper ge kai o oresths eimi ermogenhs kinduneuw orqos exw eite tis tuxh eqw autos o onoma eite kai poihths tis o qhriwdhs o fusis kai o agrios autos kai o oreinos endeiknumi o onoma
english_head: Then the impious son of a pious father ought to receive the name of his class. True. Not Theophilus (beloved of God) or Mnesitheus (mindful of God) or anything of that sort; but something of opposite meaning, if names are correct. Most assuredly, Socrates. As the name of Orestes (mountain man) is undoubtedly correct, Hermogenes, whether it was given him by chance or by some poet who indicated by the name the fierceness, rudeness, and mountain-wildness of his nature.

greek_head: Φαίνεται οὕτως ὦ Σώκρατες Ἔοικεν δέ γε καὶ τῷ πατρὶ αὐτοῦ κατὰ φύσιν τὸ
greek_tokens: fainw outws eimi swkraths eoika de ge kai o pathr autos kata fus o onoma eimi fainw kinduneuw gar toioutos tis eimi o agamemnwn oios o dokew autos diaponew kai karterew telos epitiqhmi o docasis dia areth shmeion de autos o en troia monh o paqos te kai karteria ostis oun agastos kata
english_head: So it seems, Socrates. And his father’s name also appears to be in accordance with nature. It seems so. Yes, for Agamemnon (admirable for remaining) is one who would resolve to toil to the end and to endure, putting the finish upon his resolution by virtue. And a proof of this is his long retention of the host at Troy and his endurance. So the name Agamemnon denotes that this man is admirable for remaining.

greek_head: τὴν ἐπιμονὴν οὗτος ὁ ἀνὴρ ἐνσημαίνει τὸ ὄνομα ὁ Ἀγαμέμνων ἴσως δὲ καὶ ὁ Ἀτρεὺς ὀρθῶς ἔχει ὅ τε
greek_tokens: o epimonh outos o anhr enshmainomai o onoma o agamemnwn isos de kai o atreus orqos exis o te gar o xrusippos autos fonos kai o pros o quesths ws wmos diaprassw pas outos zhmiwdhs kai athros pros areth o oun o onoma epwnumios mikros paraklinw kai epikaluptw wste mh pas dhlow o fus o anhr o de epaiw peri onoma ikanos dhlow o boulomai o atreus kai gar kata o ateirhs kai
english_head: And so, too, the name of Atreus is likely to be correct; for his murder of Chrysippus and the cruelty of his acts to Thyestes are all damaging and ruinous (ἀτηρά) to his virtue. Now the form of his name is slightly deflected and hidden, so that it does not make the man’s nature plain to every one; but to those who understand about names it makes the meaning of Atreus plain enough; for indeed

greek_head: κατὰ τὸ ἄτρεστον καὶ κατὰ τὸ ἀτηρὸν πανταχῇ ὀρθῶς αὐτῷ τὸ ὄνομα κεῖται δοκεῖ δέ μοι καὶ τῷ Πέλοπι
greek_tokens: kata o atrestos kai kata o athros pantaxh orqos autos o onoma kew dokew de egw kai o peloy o onoma emmetros kew shmainw gar outos o onoma o o eggus oraw acios eimi outos o epwnumios pws dh oios pou kai kata ekeinos legw o anhr en o o murtilos fonos oudeis oios te gignomai pronoew oude proeidon o prosw o eis o pas genos osos autos
english_head: in view of his stubbornness (ἀτειρές) and fearlessness (ἄτρεστον) and ruinous acts (ἀτηρά) the name is correctly given to him on every ground. And I think Pelops also has a fitting name; for this name means that he who sees only what is near deserves this designation. How is that? Why it is said of him that in murdering Myrtilus he was quite unable to forecast or foresee the ultimate effects upon his whole race, and all the misery with which it was overwhelmed,

greek_head: δυστυχίας ἐνεπίμπλη τὸ ἐγγὺς μόνον ὁρῶν καὶ τὸ παραχρῆμα τοῦτο δ' ἐστὶ πέλας ἡνίκα προεθυμεῖτο λαβεῖν
greek_tokens: dustuxia empiplhmi o eggus monos oraw kai o paraxrhma outos de eimi pelas hnika proqumeomai lambanw pas tropos o o ippodameia gamos o de tantalos kai pas an hgeomai onoma orqos kai kata fus tiqhmi ei alhqhs o peri autos legw o poios outos o su pou eti zaw dustuxhma gignomai polus kai deinos os kai telos o patris autos oloc anatrepw kai teleutaw en adhs o uper o kefalh o liqos
english_head: because he saw only the near at hand and the immediate— that is to say, πέλας (near)—in his eagerness to win by all means the hand of Hippodameia. And any one would think the name of Tantalus was given rightly and in accordance with nature, if the stories about him are true. What are the stories? The many terrible misfortunes that happened to him both in his life, the last of which was the utter overthrow of his country, and in Hades, after his death,

greek_head: ταλαντεία θαυμαστὴ ὡς σύμφωνος τῷ ὀνόματι καὶ ἀτεχνῶς ἔοικεν ὥσπερ ἂν εἴ τις βουλόμενος ταλάντατον ὀνομάσαι
greek_tokens: talanteia qaumastos ws sumfwnos o onoma kai atexnos eoika wsper an ei tis boulomai talas onomazw apokruptw onomazw kai eipon anti ekeinos tantalos toioutos tis kai outos o onoma eoika ekporizw o tuxh o fhmh fainw de kai o pathr autos legw o zeus
english_head: the balancing (ταλαντεία) of the stone above his head, in wonderful agreement with his name; and it seems exactly as if someone who wished to call him most wretched (ταλάντατον) disguised the name and said Tantalus instead; in some such way as that chance seems to have affected his name in the legend. And his father also, who is said to be Zeus, appears to have a very excellent name, but it is not easy to understand;

greek_head: παγκάλως τὸ ὄνομα κεῖσθαι ἔστι δὲ οὐ ῥᾴδιον κατανοῆσαι ἀτεχνῶς γάρ ἐστιν οἷον λόγος τὸ τοῦ Διὸς ὄνομα διελόντες
greek_tokens: pagkalos o onoma kew eimi de ou radios katanoew atexnos gar eimi oios logos o o zeus onoma diairew de autos dixaw e men o eteros meros e de o eteros xraomai e men gar zhnos e de dios kalew suntiqhmi de eis eis dhlow o fus o qeaomai o dh proshkw fhmi onoma oios te eimi apergazomai ou gar eimi egw kai o allos pas ostis eimi aitios mallon o zaw h o arxw te kai basileus o pas sumbainw oun orqos
english_head: for the name of Zeus is exactly like a sentence; we divide it into two parts, and some of us use one part, others the other; for some call him Zena (Ζῆνα), and others Dia (Δία); but the two in combination express the nature of the god, which is just what we said a name should be able to do. For certainly no one is so much the author of life (ζῆν) for us and all others as the ruler and king of all.

greek_head: ὀνομάζεσθαι οὗτος ὁ θεὸς εἶναι δι' ὃν ζῆν ἀεὶ πᾶσι τοῖς ζῶσιν ὑπάρχει διείληπται δὲ δίχα ὥσπερ λέγω ἓν ὂν τὸ
greek_tokens: onomazw outos o qeos eimi dia os zaw aei pas o zaw uparxw dialambanw de dixa wsper legos eis eimi o onoma o zeus kai o zeus outos de kronos uios ubristikos men an tis dokew eimi akouw ecaifnhs eulogos de megas tis dianoia ekgonos eimi o dios koros gar shmainw ou pais alla o kaqaros autos kai akhratos o neomai eimi de outos ouranos us ws logos o de au eis o anoos oyis kalos exis outos o onoma kalew
english_head: Thus this god is correctly named, through whom (δι’ ὅν) all living beings have the gift of life (ζῆν). But, as I say, the name is divided, though it is one name, into the two parts, Dia and Zena. And it might seem, at first hearing, highly irreverent to call him the son of Cronus and reasonable to say that Zeus is the offspring of some great intellect; and so he is, for κόρος (for Κρόνος) signifies not child, but the purity (καθαρόν) and unblemished nature of his mind. And Cronus, according to tradition, is the son of Uranus; but the upward gaze is rightly called by the name urania (οὐρανία),

greek_head: οὐρανία ὁρῶσα τὰ ἄνω ὅθεν δὴ καί φασιν ὦ Ἑρμόγενες τὸν καθαρὸν νοῦν παραγίγνεσθαι οἱ μετεωρολόγοι
greek_tokens: ouranios oraw o anoos oqen dh kai fhmi eimi ermogenhs o kaqaros new paragignomai e metewrologos kai o ouranos orqos o onoma kew ei de mimnhskw o hsiodos genealogia tis eti o anw progonos legw outos ou an epauw dieceimi ws orqos autos o onoma kew ews apopeiraomai o sofia outos tis poihsis ei ara aperaw h ou o egw ecaifnhs nun outwsi
english_head: looking at the things above (ὁρῶ τὰ ἄνω), and the astronomers say, Hermogenes, that from this looking people acquire a pure mind, and Uranus is correctly named. If I remembered the genealogy of Hesiod and the still earlier ancestors of the gods he mentions, I would have gone on examining the correctness of their names until I had made a complete trial whether this wisdom which has suddenly come to me, I know not whence,

greek_head: προσπέπτωκεν ἄρτι οὐκ οἶδ' ὁπόθεν Καὶ μὲν δή ὦ Σώκρατες ἀτεχνῶς γέ μοι δοκεῖς
greek_tokens: prospitnw arti ou oida opoqen kai men dh eimi swkraths atexnos ge egw dokew wsper e enqousiaw ecaifnhs xrhsmwdew kai aitiaomai ge eimi ermogenhs malista autos apo euqufrwn o prospaltiou prospitnw egw ewqen gar polus autos suneimi kai parexw o ous kinduneuw oun enqousia ou monos o ous egw empiplhmi o daimonios sofia alla kai o yuxazw epilambanw dokew oun egw
english_head: will fail or not. Indeed, Socrates, you do seem to me to be uttering oracles, exactly like an inspired prophet. Yes, Hermogenes, and I am convinced that the inspiration came to me from Euthyphro the Prospaltian. For I was with him and listening to him a long time early this morning. So he must have been inspired, and he not only filled my ears but took possession of my soul with his superhuman wisdom. So I think this is our duty:

greek_head: χρῆναι οὑτωσὶ ἡμᾶς ποιῆσαι τὸ μὲν τήμερον εἶναι χρήσασθαι αὐτῇ καὶ τὰ λοιπὰ περὶ τῶν ὀνομάτων ἐπισκέψασθαι
greek_tokens: xrh outwsi egw poiew o men shmeron eimi xraw autos kai o loipas peri o onoma episkeptomai aurion de an kai su sundokew apodiopompeomai te autos kai kaqairw eceuriskw ostis o toioutos deinos
english_head: we ought today to make use of this wisdom and finish the investigation of names, but tomorrow, if the rest of you agree, we will conjure it away and purify ourselves, when we have found some one, whether priest or sophist,

greek_head: καθαίρειν εἴτε τῶν ἱερέων τις εἴτε τῶν σοφιστῶν Ἀλλ' ἐγὼ μὲν συγχωρῶ πάνυ γὰρ ἂν ἡδέως τὰ
greek_tokens: kaqairw eite o iera tis eite o sofisths alla egw men sugxwrew panu gar an hdews o epiloipos peri o onoma akouw alla xrh outws poiew pinw oun boulomai arxw diaskopew epei eis tupon tis embainw ina oida ei ara egw epimarturhsis autos o onoma mh panu apo o automatos outws ekastos kew alla exw
english_head: who is skilled in that kind of purifying. I agree, for I should be very glad to hear the rest of the talk about names. Very well. Then since we have outlined a general plan of investigation, where shall we begin, that we may discover whether the names themselves will bear witness that they are not at all distributed at haphazard, but have a certain correctness?

greek_head: τινὰ ὀρθότητα τὰ μὲν οὖν τῶν ἡρώων καὶ ἀνθρώπων λεγόμενα ὀνόματα ἴσως ἂν ἡμᾶς ἐξαπατήσειεν πολλὰ μὲν γὰρ
greek_tokens: tis orqoths o men oun o hrws kai anqrwpos legw onoma isos an egw ecapataw polus men gar autos kew kata progonos epwnumios oudeis proshkw eneimi wsper kata arxh legw polus de wsper euxomai tiqhmi oios eutuxidhs kai swsihs kai qeofilos kai allos polus o men oun toioutos dokew egw xrh eaw eoika de malista egw euriskw o orqos keimai peri o aei eimi kai fuw spoudazw gar entauqa
english_head: Now the names of heroes and men might perhaps prove deceptive; for they are often given because they were names of ancestors, and in some cases, as we said in the beginning, they are quite inappropriate; many, too, are given as the expression of a prayer, such as Eutychides (fortunate), Sosias (saviour), Theophilus (beloved of God), and many others. I think we had better disregard such as these; but we are most likely to find the correct names in the nature of the eternal and absolute; for there the names ought to have been given with the greatest care,

greek_head: μάλιστα πρέπει τὴν θέσιν τῶν ὀνομάτων ἴσως δ' ἔνια αὐτῶν καὶ ὑπὸ θειοτέρας δυνάμεως ἢ τῆς τῶν ἀνθρώπων ἐτέθη
greek_tokens: malista prepw o qesis o onoma isos de enioi autos kai upo qeios dunamis h o o anqrwpos tiqhmi dokew egw kalos legw eimi swkraths ara oun ou dikaios apo o qea arxw skopaw ph pote autos outos o onoma e qeos orqos kalew eoika ge toiosde toinun egw upopteuw fainw egw e proteros o anqrwpos o peri o ellas outos monos
english_head: and perhaps some of them were given by a power more divine than is that of men. I think you are right, Socrates. Then is it not proper to begin with the gods and see how the gods are rightly called by that name? That is reasonable. Something of this sort, then, is what I suspect: I think the earliest men in Greece believed only in those gods in whom many foreigners believe today—

greek_head: τοὺς θεοὺς ἡγεῖσθαι οὕσπερ νῦν πολλοὶ τῶν βαρβάρων ἥλιον καὶ σελήνην καὶ γῆν καὶ ἄστρα καὶ οὐρανόν ἅτε οὖν
greek_tokens: o qeos hgeomai os nun polus o barbaros hlios kai selhnh kai gaia kai astron kai ouranos ate oun autos oraw pas aei eimi dromos kai qew apo outos o fusis o o qew qeos autos eponomazw usteron de katanoew o allos pas hdh outos o onoma prosagoreuw eoika tis o legos o alhqhs h oudeis panu men oun eoika tis oun an meta outos skopaw dhlos dh ostis daimwn te kai hrws kai anqrwpos
english_head: sun, moon, earth, stars, and sky. They saw that all these were always moving in their courses and running, and so they called them gods (θεούς) from this running (θεῖν) nature; then afterwards, when they gained knowledge of the other gods, they called them all by the same name. Is that likely to be true, or not? Yes, very likely. What shall we consider next?

greek_head: δαίμονας Καὶ ὡς ἀληθῶς ὦ Ἑρμόγενες τί ἄν ποτε νοοῖ τὸ
greek_tokens: daimwn kai ws alhqhs eimi ermogenhs tis an pote noew o onoma e daimwn skeptomai an tis su docow eipon legos monos oida oun tis fhmi hsiodos eimi o daimwn ou ennoew oude ostis xruseos genos o proteros fhmi gignomai o anqrwpos oida outos ge legw toinun peri autos atar epei outos genos kata moira kaluptw
english_head: Spirits, obviously. Hermogenes, what does the name spirits really mean? See if you think there is anything in what I am going to say. Go on and say it. Do you remember who Hesiod says the spirits are? I do not recall it. Nor that he says a golden race was the first race of men to be born? Yes, I do know that. Well, he says of it:But since Fate has covered up this race,

greek_head: οἱ μὲν δαίμονες ἁγνοὶ ὑποχθόνιοι καλέονται ἐσθλοί ἀλεξίκακοι φύλακες θνητῶν ἀνθρώπων
greek_tokens: e men daimwn agnos upoxqonios kalew esqlos alecikakos fulac qnhtos anqrwpos tis oun dh ostis oimh egw legw autos o xruseos genos ou ek xruseos fuw alla agaqos te kai kalos tekmhrion de egw eimi ostis kai egw fhmi sidhreos eimi genos alhqhs legw oukoun kai o nun oiomai an fainw autos ei tis
english_head: They are called holy spirits under the earth,Noble, averters of evil, guardians of mortal men.Hes. WD 122 ff What of that? Why, I think he means that the golden race was not made of gold, but was good and beautiful. And I regard it as a proof of this that he further says we are the iron race. True. Don’t you suppose that if anyone of our day is good,

greek_head: ἀγαθός ἐστιν ἐκείνου τοῦ χρυσοῦ γένους εἶναι Εἰκός γε
greek_tokens: agaqos eimi ekeinos o xruseos genos eimi eoika ge e de agaqos allos tis h fronimos fronimos outos toinun pas mallon legw ws egw dokew o daimwn ostis fronimos kai dahmwn eimi daimwn autos onomazw kai en ge o arxaios o hmeteros fwnew autos sumbainw o onoma legw oun kalos kai outos kai allos poihths polus osos legw ws epeidan tis agaqos eimi teleutaw megas moira kai timh exis kai gignomai
english_head: Hesiod would say he was of that golden race? Quite likely. But the good are the wise, are they not? Yes, they are the wise. This, then, I think, is what he certainly means to say of the spirits: because they were wise and knowing (δαήμονες) he called them spirits (δαίμονες) and in the old form of our language the two words are the same. Now he and all the other poets are right, who say that when a good man dies

greek_head: δαίμων κατὰ τὴν τῆς φρονήσεως ἐπωνυμίαν ταύτῃ οὖν τίθεμαι καὶ ἐγὼ τὸν δαήμονα πάντ' ἄνδρα ὃς ἂν ἀγαθὸς ᾖ
greek_tokens: daimwn kata o o fronhsis epwnumios outos oun tiqhmi kai egw o dahmwn panth anhr os an agaqos eimi daimonion eimi kai zaw kai teleutaw kai orqos daimwn kalew kai egw egw dokazw eimi swkraths outos panu su sumyhfos eimi o de dh hrws tis an eaw outos de ou panu xalepos ennoew mikros gar paragw autos o onoma dhlow o ek o erws genesis pws legw ou oida ostis hmiqeos e hrws tis oun
english_head: he has a great portion and honor among the dead, and becomes a spirit, a name which is in accordance with the other name of wisdom. And so I assert that every good man, whether living or dead, is of spiritual nature, and is rightly called a spirit. And I, Socrates, believe I quite agree with you in that. But what is the word hero? That is easy to understand; for the name has been but slightly changed, and indicates their origin from love (ἔρως). What do you mean?

greek_head: Πάντες δήπου γεγόνασιν ἐρασθέντος ἢ θεοῦ θνητῆς ἢ θνητοῦ θεᾶς ἐὰν οὖν σκοπῇς καὶ τοῦτο κατὰ τὴν Ἀττικὴν
greek_tokens: pas dhpou gignomai eramai h qeaomai qnhtos h qnhtos qea ean oun skopaw kai outos kata o attikos o palaios fwnh mallon eidomai dhlwsis gar su ostis para o o erws onoma oqen gignomai e hrws mikros paragw eimi onoma xaris kai htoi outos legw o hrws h ostis sofos eimi kai rhtwr kai deinos kai dialektikos erwtaw ikanos eimi o gar eirw legw eimi osper oun arti legw en o attikos fwnew legw
english_head: Why, they were all born because a god fell in love with a mortal woman, or a mortal man with a goddess. Now if you consider the word hero also in the old Attic pronunciation, you will understand better; for that will show you that it has been only slightly altered from the name of love (Eros), the source from which the heroes spring, to make a name for them. And either this is the reason why they are called heroes, or it is because they were wise and clever orators and dialecticians, able to ask questions (ἐρωτᾶν), for εἴρειν is the same as λέγειν (speak). Therefore, when their name is spoken in the Attic dialect, which I was mentioning just now, the heroes turn out to be orators and askers of questions,

greek_head: οἱ ἥρωες ῥήτορές τινες καὶ ἐρωτητικοὶ συμβαίνουσιν ὥστε ῥητόρων καὶ σοφιστῶν γένος γίγνεται τὸ ἡρωικὸν
greek_tokens: e hrws rhtwr tis kai erwthtikos sumbainw wste rhtwr kai sofisths genos gignomai o hrwikos fulon alla ou outos xalepos eimi ennoew alla mallon o o anqrwpos dia tis pote anqrwpos kalew h su exis eipon pinw agaqos exw oude ei tis oios su an eimi euriskw ou sunteinw dia o hgeomai sos mallon euriskw h emautou
english_head: so that the heroic race proves to be a race of orators and sophists. That is easy to understand, but the case of men, and the reason why they are called men (ἄνθρωποι), is more difficult. Can you tell me what it is? No, my friend, I cannot; and even if I might perhaps find out, I shall not try, because I think you are more likely to find out than I am.

greek_head: Τῇ τοῦ Εὐθύφρονος ἐπιπνοίᾳ πιστεύεις ὡς ἔοικας Δῆλα δή
greek_tokens: o o euqufrwn epipnoia pisteuw ws eoika dhlos dh orqos ge su pisteuw ws kai nun ge egw fainw komyos ennoew kai kinduneuw ean mh eulabeomai eti shmeron sofos o deon gignomai skopaw dh o legos proteros men gar o toiosde deomai ennoew peri onoma ostis pollakis epemballw gramma o de ecairew para o boulomai onomazw kai o ocuths metaballw oios zeus filos outos ina
english_head: You have faith in the inspiration of Euthyphro, it seems. Evidently. And you are right in having it; for just at this very moment I think I have had a clever thought, and if I am not careful, before the day is over I am likely to be wiser than I ought to be. So pay attention. First we must remember in regard to names that we often put in or take out letters, making the names different from the meaning we intend, and we change the accent.

greek_head: ἀντὶ ῥήματος ὄνομα ἡμῖν γένηται τό τε ἕτερον αὐτόθεν ἰῶτα ἐξείλομεν καὶ ἀντὶ ὀξείας τῆς μέσης συλλαβῆς βαρεῖαν
greek_tokens: anti rhma onoma egw gignomai o te eteros autoqen iwta ecairew kai anti ocus o mesh sullabh barus fqeggomai allos de enantion emballw gramma o de barus ocus fqeggomai alhqhs legw outos toinun eis kai o o anqrwpos onoma pasxw ws egw dokew ek gar rhma onoma gignomai eis gramma o alfa ecairew kai barus o teleutaw gignomai pws legw
english_head: Take, for instance, Διὶ φίλος; to change this from a phrase to a name, we took out the second iota and pronounced the middle syllable with the grave instead of the acute accent (Diphilus). In other instances, on the contrary, we insert letters and pronounce grave accents as acute. True. Now it appears to me that the name of men (ἄνθρωπος) underwent a change of that sort. It was a phrase, but became a noun when one letter, alpha, was removed and the accent of the last syllable was dropped. What do you mean?

greek_head: Ὧδε σημαίνει τοῦτο τὸ ὄνομα ὁ ἄνθρωπος ὅτι τὰ μὲν ἄλλα θηρία ὧν ὁρᾷ οὐδὲν ἐπισκοπεῖ οὐδὲ ἀναλογίζεται
greek_tokens: wde shmainw outos o onoma o anqrwpos ostis o men allos qhrion os oraw oudeis episkopew oude analogizomai oude anaqrew o de anqrwpos ama oraw outos de eimi o oraw kai anaqrew kai logizomai outos o oraw enteuqen dh monos o qhraw orqos o anqrwpos anqrwpos onomazw anaqrew o oraw tis oun o meta outos eromai su o hdews an puqw panu ge
english_head: I will tell you. The name man (ἄνθρωπος) indicates that the other animals do not examine, or consider, or look up at (ἀναθρεῖ) any of the things that they see, but man has no sooner seen—that is, ὄπωπε—than he looks up at and considers that which he has seen. Therefore of all the animals man alone is rightly called man (ἄνθρωπος), because he looks up at (ἀναθρεῖ) what he has seen (ὄπωπε). Of course. May I ask you about the next word I should like to have explained? Certainly.

greek_head: Ὥσπερ τοίνυν μοι δοκεῖ τούτοις ἑξῆς εἶναί τι χρῆμα ψυχὴν γάρ που καὶ σῶμα καλοῦμεν τοῦ
greek_tokens: wsper toinun egw dokew outos echs eimi tis xrhma yuxh gar pou kai swma kalew o anqrwpos pws gar ou peirw dh kai outos diairew wsper o emprosqen yuxh legw episkeptomai ws eoikotws outos o onoma tugxanw epeimi au o swma nai ws men toinun ek o paraxrhma legw oimh tis toioutos noew o o yuxh onomazw ws outos ara otan pareimi o swma aitios eimi o zaw autos o o
english_head: It seems to me to come naturally next after those you have discussed. We speak of man’s soul and body. Yes, of course. Let us try to analyze these, as we did the previous words. You mean consider soul (ψυχή) and see why it is properly called by that name, and likewise body (σῶμα)? Yes. To speak on the spur of the moment, I think those who gave the soul its name had something of this sort in mind: they thought when it was present in the body it was the cause of its living,

greek_head: ἀναπνεῖν δύναμιν παρέχον καὶ ἀναψῦχον ἅμα δὲ ἐκλείποντος τοῦ ἀναψύχοντος τὸ σῶμα ἀπόλλυταί τε καὶ τελευτᾷ
greek_tokens: anapnew dunamis parexw kai anayuxw ama de ekleipw o anayuxw o swma apollumi te kai teleutaw oqen dh egw dokew autos yuxh kalew ei de boulomai exw hrema dokazw gar egw tis kaqoraw piqanos outos
english_head: giving it the power to breathe and reviving it (ἀναψῦχον), and when this revivifying force fails, the body perishes and comes to an end therefore, I think, they called it ψυχή. But—please keep still a moment. I fancy I see something which will carry more conviction

greek_head: τοῖς ἀμφὶ Εὐθύφρονα τούτου μὲν γάρ ὡς ἐμοὶ δοκεῖ καταφρονήσαιεν ἂν καὶ ἡγήσαιντο φορτικὸν εἶναι τόδε δὲ
greek_tokens: o amfi euqufrwn outos men gar ws egw dokew katafronew an kai hgeomai fortikos eimi ode de skopaw ean ara kai sos aresis legos monos o fus pas o swma wste kai zaw kai perieimi tis su dokew exw te kai oxew allos h yuxh oudeis allos tis de kai o o allos apas fus ou pisteuw anacagorhs new kai yuxh eimi o diakosmew kai exw egw
english_head: to Euthyphro and his followers; for I think they would despise this attempt and would consider it cheap talk. Now see if you like the new one. I am listening. Do you think there is anything which holds and carries the whole nature of the body, so that it lives and moves, except the soul? No; nothing. Well, and do you not believe the doctrine of Anaxagoras, that it is mind or soul which orders and holds the nature of all things? I do.

greek_head: Καλῶς ἄρα ἂν τὸ ὄνομα τοῦτο ἔχοι τῇ δυνάμει ταύτῃ ἣ φύσιν ὀχεῖ καὶ ἔχει φυσέχην ἐπονομάζειν
greek_tokens: kalos ara an o onoma outos exw o dunamis outos o fus oxaomai kai exis fusexh eponomazw ecesti de kai yuxh komyeuw legw panu men oun kai dokew ge egw outos ekeinos texnikos eimi kai gar eimi geloios mentoi fainw ws alhqhs onomazw ws tiqhmi alla dh o meta outos pws fhmi exw o swma legw nai pollaxh egw dokew outos ge an men kai mikros
english_head: Then there would be an admirable fitness in calling that power which carries and holds (ἔχει) nature (φύσιν) φυσέχη and this may be refined and pronounced ψυχή. Certainly; and I think this is a more scientific explanation than the other. Yes, it is. But it seems actually absurd that the name was given with such truth. Now what shall we say about the next word? You mean body (σῶμα)? Yes. I think this admits of many explanations, if a little, even very little, change is made; for some say it is the tomb (σῆμα) of the soul,

greek_head: τις παρακλίνῃ καὶ πάνυ καὶ γὰρ σῆμά τινές φασιν αὐτὸ εἶναι τῆς ψυχῆς ὡς τεθαμμένης ἐν τῷ νῦν παρόντι καὶ
greek_tokens: tis paraklinw kai panu kai gar shma tis fhmi autos eimi o yuxazw ws qaptw en o nun pareimi kai dioti au outos shmainw o an shmainw o yuxh kai outos shma orqos kalew dokew mentoi egw malista tiqhmi e amfi orfeus outos o onoma ws dikh didwmi o yuxazw os dh eneka didwmi outos de peribolos exw ina swzw desmwthrion eikwn eimi oun o yuxazw outos wsper autos onomazw ews an ekteisis o ofeilw o swma kai oudeis dew paragw oude eis gramma
english_head: their notion being that the soul is buried in the present life; and again, because by its means the soul gives any signs which it gives, it is for this reason also properly called sign (σῆμα). But I think it most likely that the Orphic poets gave this name, with the idea that the soul is undergoing punishment for something; they think it has the body as an enclosure to keep it safe, like a prison, and this is, as the name itself denotes, the safe (σῶμα) for the soul, until the penalty is paid, and not even a letter needs to be changed.

greek_head: Ταῦτα μέν μοι δοκεῖ ἱκανῶς ὦ Σώκρατες εἰρῆσθαι περὶ δὲ τῶν θεῶν τῶν ὀνομάτων οἷον καὶ περὶ τοῦ Διὸς
greek_tokens: outos men egw dokew ikanos eimi swkraths erw peri de o qea o onoma oios kai peri o zeus nundi legw exw an pou kata o autos tropos episkeptomai kata tis pote orqoths autos o onoma kew nai ma dios egw ge eimi ermogenhs eiper ge new exw enos men o kalos tropos ostis peri qea oudeis oida oute peri autos oute peri o onoma ostis pote eautou kalew dhlos gar ostis ekeinos ge alhqhs kalew
english_head: I think, Socrates, enough has been said about these words; but might we not consider the names of the gods in the same way in which you were speaking about that of Zeus a few minutes ago, and see what kind of correctness there is in them? By Zeus, Hermogenes, we, if we are sensible, must recognize that there is one most excellent kind, since of the gods we know nothing, neither of them nor of their names, whatever they may be, by which they call themselves, for it is clear that they use the true names. But there is a second kind of correctness,

greek_head: δεύτερος δ' αὖ τρόπος ὀρθότητος ὥσπερ ἐν ταῖς εὐχαῖς νόμος ἐστὶν ἡμῖν εὔχεσθαι οἵτινές τε καὶ ὁπόθεν χαίρουσιν
greek_tokens: deuteros de au tropos orqoths wsper en o euxh nomos eimi egw euxomai ostis te kai opoqen xairw onomazw outos kai egw autos kalew ws allos mhdeis
english_head: that we call them, as is customary in prayers, by whatever names and patronymics are pleasing to them, since we know no other.

greek_head: εἰδότας καλῶς γὰρ δὴ ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ νενομίσθαι εἰ οὖν βούλει σκοπῶμεν ὥσπερ προειπόντες τοῖς θεοῖς ὅτι περὶ
greek_tokens: oida kalos gar dh egw dokew nomizw ei oun boulomai skopaw wsper proeipon o qeaw ostis peri autos oudeis egw skeptomai ou gar aciow oios su an eimi skopaw alla peri o anqrwpos os pote tis doca exw tiqhmi autos o onoma outos gar anemeshtos alla egw dokew eimi swkraths metrios legw kai outws poiew
english_head: Now I think that is an excellent custom. So, if you like, let us first make a kind of announcement to the gods, saying that we are not going to investigate about them—for we do not claim to be able to do that—but about men, and let us inquire what thought men had in giving them their names; for in that there is no impiety. I think, Socrates, you are right; let us do as you say.

greek_head: Ἄλλο τι οὖν ἀφ' Ἑστίας ἀρχώμεθα κατὰ τὸν νόμον Δίκαιον γοῦν
greek_tokens: allos tis oun apo estios arxw kata o nomos dikaios goun tis oun an tis fhmi dianoeomai o onomazw estios onomazw ou ma o dios oude outos oimh radios eimi kinduneuw goun agaqos ermogenhs e proteros o onoma tiqhmi ou faulos eimi alla metewrologos kai adolesxhs tis tis dh katafainw egw o qesis o onoma toioutos
english_head: Shall we, then, begin with Hestia, according to custom? That is the proper thing. Then what would you say the man had in mind who gave Hestia her name? By Zeus, I think that is no more easy question than the other. At any rate, my dear Hermogenes, the first men who gave names were no ordinary persons, but high thinkers and great talkers. What then? I am sure the names were given by men of that kind; and if foreign names are examined,

greek_head: τινῶν ἀνθρώπων καὶ ἐάν τις τὰ ξενικὰ ὀνόματα ἀνασκοπῇ οὐχ ἧττον ἀνευρίσκεται ὃ ἕκαστον βούλεται οἷον καὶ ἐν
greek_tokens: tis anqrwpos kai ean tis o cenikos onoma anaskopew ou hsswn aneuriskw o ekastos boulomai oios kai en outos o egw ousia kalew eimi e essia kalew e de au asios proteros men oun kata o eteros onoma outos o o pragma ousia estios kalew exis logos kai ostis ge au egw o o ousia metexw eimi fhmi kai kata outos orqos an kalew estios eoika gar kai egw o palaios essia kalew o ousia eti de kai kata o qusia an tis
english_head: the meaning of each of them is equally evident. Take, for instance, that which we call οὐσία (reality, essence); some people call it ἐσσία, and still others ὠσία. First, then, in connection with the second of these forms, it is reasonable that the essence of things be called Hestia; and moreover, because we ourselves say of that which partakes of reality it is, (ἔστιν), the name Hestia would be correct in this connection also; for apparently we also called οὐσία (reality) ἐσσία in ancient times. And besides, if you consider it in connection with sacrifices,

greek_head: ἐννοήσας ἡγήσαιτο οὕτω νοεῖν ταῦτα τοὺς τιθεμένους τὸ γὰρ πρὸ πάντων θεῶν τῇ Ἑστίᾳ πρώτῃ προθύειν εἰκὸς ἐκείνους
greek_tokens: ennoew hgeomai outws noew outos o tiqhmi o gar pro pas qea o estios proteros proquw eoika ekeinos ostis o pas ousia essia eponomazw osos de au asios sxedon tis au outos kaqa hrakleitos an hgeomai o eimi eimi te pas kai menw oudeis o oun aitios kai o arxhgos autos eimi o wqew oqen dh kalos exw autos asios onomazw kai outos
english_head: you would come to the conclusion that those who established them understood the name in that way; for those who called the essence of things ἐσσία would naturally sacrifice to Hestia first of all the gods. Those on the other hand, who say ὠσία would agree, well enough with Heracleitus that all things move and nothing remains still. So they would say the cause and ruler of things was the pushing power (ὠθοῦν), wherefore it had been rightly named ὠσία. But enough of this, considering that we know nothing.

greek_head: μὲν δὴ ταύτῃ ὡς παρὰ μηδὲν εἰδότων εἰρήσθω μετὰ δ' Ἑστίαν δίκαιον Ῥέαν καὶ Κρόνον ἐπισκέψασθαι καίτοι
