greek_tokens: men dh outos ws para mhdeis oida erw meta de estios dikaios rean kai kronos episkeptomai kaitoi o ge o kronos onoma hdh dierxomai isos mentoi oudeis legos tis dh eimi swkraths agaqos ennoew tis smhnos sofia poios dh outos
english_head: After Hestia it is right to consider Rhea and Cronus. The name of Cronus, however, has already been discussed. But perhaps I am talking nonsense. Why, Socrates? My friend, I have thought of a swarm of wisdom. What is it?

greek_head: Γελοῖον μὲν πάνυ εἰπεῖν οἶμαι μέντοι τινὰ πιθανότητα ἔχον
greek_tokens: geloios men panu eipon oimh mentoi tis piqanoths exw tis outos o hrakleitos egw dokazw kaqoraw palaios atta sofos legw atexnos o epi kronos kai reas o kai omhros legw pws outos legw legw pou hrakleitos ostis pas xwrew kai oudeis menos kai potamos roh apeikazw o eimi legw ws dis eis o autos potamos ou an embainw eimi outos
english_head: It sounds absurd, but I think there is some probability in it. What is this probability? I seem to have a vision of Heracleitus saying some ancient words of wisdom as old as the reign of Cronus and Rhea, which Homer said too. What do you mean by that? Heracleitus says, you know, that all things move and nothing remains still, and he likens the universe to the current of a river, saying that you cannot step twice into the same stream. True.

greek_head: Τί οὖν δοκεῖ σοι ἀλλοιότερον Ἡρακλείτου νοεῖν ὁ τιθέμενος τοῖς τῶν ἄλλων θεῶν προγόνοις Ῥέαν τε καὶ
greek_tokens: tis oun dokew su alloios hrakleitos noew o tiqhmi o o allos qea progonos rean te kai kronos ara oiomai apo o automatos autos amfoteros reuma onoma tiqhmi wsper au omhros wkeanos te qea genesis fhmi kai mhthr thqus oimh de kai hsiodos legw de pou kai orfeus ostis wkeanos proteros kallirroos arxw gamos
english_head: Well, don’t you think he who gave to the ancestors of the other gods the names Rhea and Cronus had the same thought as Heracleitus? Do you think he gave both of them the names of streams merely by chance? Just so Homer, too, says—Ocean the origin of the gods, and their mother Tethys;Hom. Il. 14.201, 302 and I believe Hesiod says that also. Orpheus, too, says—Fair-flowing Ocean was the first to marry,

greek_head: ὅς ῥα κασιγνήτην ὁμομήτορα Τηθὺν ὄπυιεν ταῦτ' οὖν σκόπει ὅτι καὶ ἀλλήλοις συμφωνεῖ καὶ πρὸς τὰ τοῦ
greek_tokens: os ara kasignhth omomhtwr thqus opuiw outos oun skopaw ostis kai allhlwn sumfwnew kai pros o o hrakleitos pas teinw fainw tis egw legw eimi swkraths o mentoi o thqus ou ennoew onoma tis boulomai alla meis outos ge oligos autos legw ostis phgazw onoma epikruptw eimi o gar diattaw kai
english_head: and he wedded his sister Tethys, daughter of his mother.Orpheus FrSee how they agree with each other and all tend towards the doctrine of Heracleitus. I think there is something in what you say, Socrates; but I do not know what the name of Tethys means. Why, the name itself almost tells that it is the name of a spring somewhat disguised; for that which is strained (διαττώμενον)

greek_head: τὸ ἠθούμενον πηγῆς ἀπείκασμά ἐστιν ἐκ δὲ τούτων ἀμφοτέρων τῶν ὀνομάτων ἡ Τηθὺς τὸ ὄνομα σύγκειται
greek_tokens: o hqew phgazw apeikasma eimi ek de outos amfoteros o onoma o thqus o onoma sugkeimai outos men eimi swkraths komyos tis de ou mellw alla tis o meta outos o men dios eipon nai o adelfos dh autos legw o te poseidwn kai o ploutwn kai o eteros onoma o onomazw autos panu ge o men toinun o poseidwn egw fainw onomazw
english_head: and filtered (ἠθούμενον) represents a spring, and the name Tethys is compounded of those two words. That is very neat, Socrates. Of course it is. But what comes next? Zeus we discussed before. Yes. Let us, then, speak of his brothers, Poseidon and Pluto, including also the other name of the latter. By all means. I think Poseidon’s name was given by him who first applied it,

greek_head: τοῦ πρώτου ὀνομάσαντος ὅτι αὐτὸν βαδίζοντα ἐπέσχεν ἡ τῆς θαλάττης φύσις καὶ οὐκέτι εἴασεν προελθεῖν
greek_tokens: o proteros onomazw ostis autos badizw epwxato o o qalassa fusis kai ouketi eaw proerxomai alla wsper desmos o podow autos gignomai o oun arxw o dunamis outos qeos onomazw poseidwn ws posidesmos eimi o de e egkeimai isos euprepeia eneka taxa de ou an outos legos alla anti o sigma
english_head: because the power the sea restrained him as he was walking and hindered his advance; it acted as a bond (δεσμός) of his feet (ποδῶν). So he called the lord of this power Poseidon, regarding him as a foot-bond (ποσί-δεσμον). The e is inserted perhaps for euphony. But possibly that may not be right; possibly two lambdas were originally pronounced instead of the sigma, because the god knew (εἰδότος) many (πολλά) things.

greek_head: δύο λάβδα τὸ πρῶτον ἐλέγετο ὡς πολλὰ εἰδότος τοῦ θεοῦ ἴσως δὲ ἀπὸ τοῦ σείειν ὁ σείων ὠνόμασται
greek_tokens: duo labda o proteros legw ws polus oida o qeaomai isos de apo o seiw o qeios onomazw proskeimai de o pei kai o delta o de ploutwn outos men kata o o ploutos dosis ostis ek o gaia katwqen anihmi o ploutos eponomazw o de adhs e polus men egw dokew upolambanw o aidhs proserew o onoma outos kai fobew o onoma ploutwn kalew autos
english_head: Or it may be that from his shaking he was called the Shaker (ὁ σείων), and the pi and delta are additions. As for Pluto, he was so named as the giver of wealth (πλοῦτος), because wealth comes up from below out of the earth. And Hades—I fancy most people think that this is a name of the Invisible (ἀειδής), so they are afraid and call him Pluto.

greek_head: Σοὶ δὲ πῶς φαίνεται ὦ Σώκρατες Πολλαχῇ ἔμοιγε δοκοῦσιν ἅνθρωποι διημαρτηκέναι
greek_tokens: sos de pws fainw eimi swkraths pollaxh egw dokew anqrwpos diamartanw peri outos o qeaomai o dunamis kai fobew autos ou acios eimi ostis te gar epeidan apac tis egw apoqnhskw aei ekei eimi fobew kai ostis o yuxh gumnos o swma para ekeinos aperxomai kai outos fobew o de egw dokew pas eis tautos tis sunteinw kai o arxh o qeaomai kai o onoma pws dh
english_head: And what do you think yourself, Socrates? I think people have many false notions about the power of this god, and are unduly afraid of him. They are afraid because when we are once dead we remain in his realm for ever, and they are also terrified because the soul goes to him without the covering of the body. But I think all these facts, and the office and the name of the god, point in the same direction. How so?

greek_head: Ἐγώ σοι ἐρῶ ἅ γέ μοι φαίνεται εἰπὲ γάρ μοι δεσμὸς ζῴῳ ὁτῳοῦν ὥστε μένειν ὁπουοῦν πότερος
greek_tokens: egw su eraomai o ge egw fainw eipon gar egw desmos zwion ostis wste menw opou poteros isxuros eimi anagkh h epiqumia polus diaferw eimi swkraths o epiqumia oiomai oun o adhs ou an polus ekfeugw ei mh o isxuros desmos dew o ekeise eimi dhlos dh epiqumia ara tis autos ws eoika deomai eiper o megas desmos deomai kai ou anagkh fainw oukoun epiqumia au polus eimi nai
english_head: I will tell you my own view. Please answer this question: Which is the stronger bond upon any living being to keep him in any one place, desire, or compulsion? Desire, Socrates, is much stronger. Then do you not believe there would be many fugitives from Hades, if he did not bind with the strongest bond those who go to him there? Of course there would. Apparently, then, if he binds them with the strongest bond, he binds them by some kind of desire, not by compulsion. Yes, that is plain. There are many desires, are there not? Yes.

greek_head: Τῇ μεγίστῃ ἄρα ἐπιθυμίᾳ τῶν ἐπιθυμιῶν δεῖ αὐτούς εἴπερ μέλλει τῷ μεγίστῳ δεσμῷ κατέχειν
greek_tokens: o megas ara epiqumia o epiqumia deomai autos eiper mellw o megas desmos katexw nai eimi oun tis megas epiqumia h otan tis tis suneimi oiomai dia ekeinos eimi ameinwn anhr ma diw oude opwsoun eimi swkraths dia outos ara fhmi eimi ermogenhs oudeis deuro eqelw aperxomai o ekeiqen oude autos o seirhn
english_head: Then he binds with the desire which is the strongest of all, if he is to restrain them with the strongest bond. Yes. And is there any desire stronger than the thought of being made a better man by association with some one? No, by Zeus, Socrates, there certainly is not. Then, Hermogenes, we must believe that this is the reason why no one has been willing to come away from that other world, not even the Sirens, but they and all others have been overcome by his enchantments,

greek_head: ἀλλὰ κατακεκηλῆσθαι ἐκείνας τε καὶ τοὺς ἄλλους πάντας οὕτω καλούς τινας ὡς ἔοικεν ἐπίσταται λόγους λέγειν
greek_tokens: alla katakhlew ekeinos te kai o allos pas outws kalos tis ws eoika efisthmi logos legw o adhs kai eimi os ge ek o logos outos o qeos outos teleios sofisths te kai megas euergeths o para autos os ge kai o enqade tosoutos agaqos aneimi outws polus autos o perieimi ekei eimi kai o ploutwn apo outos exw o onoma kai o au mh eqelw suneimi o anqrwpos exw o swma alla tote suggignomai
english_head: so beautiful, as it appears, are the words which Hades has the power to speak; and from this point of view this god is a perfect sophist and a great benefactor of those in his realm, he who also bestows such great blessings upon us who are on earth; such abundance surrounds him there below, and for this reason he is called Pluto. Then, too, he refuses to consort with men while they have bodies, but only accepts their society

greek_head: ἐπειδὰν ἡ ψυχὴ καθαρὰ ᾖ πάντων τῶν περὶ τὸ σῶμα κακῶν καὶ ἐπιθυμιῶν οὐ φιλοσόφου δοκεῖ σοι εἶναι καὶ εὖ ἐντεθυμημένου

english_head: when the soul is pure of all the evils and desires of the body. Do you not think this shows him to be a philosopher and to understand perfectly that under these conditions he could restrain them by binding them with the desire of virtue, but that so long as they are infected with the unrest and madness of the body, not even his father Cronus could hold them to himself, though he bound them with his famous chains? There seems to be something in that, Socrates.

english_head: And the name Hades is not in the least derived from the invisible (ἀειδές), but far more probably from knowing (εἰδέναι) all noble things, and for that reason he was called Hades by the lawgiver. Very well; what shall we say of Demeter, Hera, Apollo, Athena, Hephaestus, Ares, and the other gods Demeter appears to have been called Demeter, because like a mother (μήτηρ) she gives the gift of food,

english_head: and Hera is a lovely one (ἐρατή), as indeed, Zeus is said to have married her for love. But perhaps the lawgiver had natural phenomena in mind, and called her Hera (Ἥρα) as a disguise for ἀήρ (air), putting the beginning at the end. You would understand, if you were to repeat the name Hera over and over. And Pherephatta!—How many people fear this name, and also Apollo! I imagine it is because they do not know about correctness of names. You see they change the name to Phersephone and its aspect frightens them. But really the name indicates that the goddess is wise;

greek_head: σοφὴν εἶναι τὴν θεόν ἅτε γὰρ φερομένων τῶν πραγμάτων τὸ ἐφαπτόμενον καὶ ἐπαφῶν καὶ δυνάμενον ἐπακολουθεῖν

english_head: for since things are in motion (φερόμενα), that which grasps (ἐφαπτόμενον) and touches (ἐπαφῶν) and is able to follow them is wisdom. Pherepapha, or something of that sort, would therefore be the correct name of the goddess, because she is wise and touches that which is in motion (ἐπαφὴ τοῦ φερομένου)—and this is the reason why Hades, who is wise, consorts with her, because she is wise—but people have altered her name, attaching more importance to euphony than to truth, and they call her Pherephatta. Likewise in the case of Apollo,

english_head: as I say, many people are afraid because of the name of the god, thinking that it has some terrible meaning. Have you not noticed that? Certainly; what you say is true. But really the name is admirably appropriate to the power of the god. How is that? I will try to tell you what I think about it;

Go on; you seem to imply that it is a remarkable name. His name and nature are in harmony; you see he is a musical god. For in the first place, purification and purgations used in medicine and in soothsaying, and fumigations with medicinal and magic drugs,

english_head: and the baths and sprinklings connected with that sort of thing all have the single function of making a man pure in body and soul, do they not? Certainly. But this is the god who purifies and washes away (ἀπαλοούων) and delivers (ἀπολύων) from such evils, is he not? Certainly. With reference, then, to his acts of delivering and his washings,

english_head: as being the physician of such diseases, he might properly be called Apoluon (ἀπαλούων, the washer), and with reference to soothsaying and truth and simplicity—for the two are identical—he might most properly be called by the name the Thessalians use; for all Thessalians call the god Aplun. And because he is always by his archery controller of darts (βολῶν) he is ever darting (ἀεὶ βάλλων). And with reference to music we have to understand that alpha often signifies together, and here it denotes moving together in the heavens about the poles, as we call them, and harmony in song,

greek_head: τὴν ἐν τῇ ᾠδῇ ἁρμονίαν ἣ δὴ συμφωνία καλεῖται ὅτι ταῦτα πάντα ὥς φασιν οἱ κομψοὶ περὶ μουσικὴν καὶ ἀστρονομίαν

english_head: which is called concord; for, as the ingenious musicians and astronomers tell us, all these things move together by a kind of harmony. And this god directs the harmony, making them all move together, among both gods and men; and so, just as we call the ὁμοκέλευθον (him who accompanies), and ὁμόκοιτιν (bedfellow), by changing the ὁμο to alpha, ἀκόλουθον and ἄκοιτιν, so also we called him Apollo who was Homopolo,

But in fact, as was said,

english_head: the name touches upon all the qualities of the god, as simple, ever-darting, purifying, and accompanying. The Muses and music in general are named, apparently, from μῶσθαι, searching, and philosophy; and Leto from her gentleness, because whatever is asked of her, she is willing (ἐθελήμων). But perhaps her name is Letho, as she is called by many foreigners; and those who call her by that name seem to do so

english_head: on account of the mild and gentle (λεῖον, Ληθώ) kindness of her character. Artemis appears to get her name from her healthy (ἀρτεμές) and well-ordered nature, and her love of virginity; or perhaps he who named her meant that she is learned in virtue (ἀρετή), or possibly, too, that she hates sexual intercourse (ἄροτον μισεῖ) of man and woman; or he who gave the goddess her name may have given it for any or all of these reasons. What of Dionysus and Aphrodite? You ask great things of me, son of Hipponicus. You see there is both a serious and a facetious account of the form

english_head: of the name of these deities. You will have to ask others for the serious one; but there is nothing to hinder my giving you the facetious account, for the gods also have a sense of humor. Dionysus, the giver (διδούς) of wine (οἶνος), might be called in jest Didoinysus, and wine, because it makes most drinkers think (οἴεσθαι) they have wit (νοῦς) when they have not, might very justly be called Oeonus (οἰόνους). As for Aphrodite, we need not oppose Hesiod; we can accept his derivation of the name

No. It is easy to tell the reason of one of her two names. What name? We call her Pallas, you know. Yes, of course. Those of us are right, I fancy,

But what can you say of her other name? You mean Athena? Yes. That is a weightier matter, my friend. The ancients seem to have had the same belief about Athena as the interpreters of Homer have now;

english_head: for most of these, in commenting on the poet, say that he represents Athena as mind (νοῦς) and intellect (διάνοια); and the maker of her name seems to have had a similar conception of her, but he gives her the still grander title of mind of God ἡ θεοῦ νόησις, seeming to say that she is a ἁ θεονόα; here he used the alpha in foreign fashion instead of eta, and dropped out the iota and sigma. But perhaps that was not his reason; he may have called her Theonoe because she has unequalled knowledge of divine things (τὰ θεῖα νοοῦσα). Perhaps, too, he may have wished to identify the goddess with wisdom of character (ἐν ἤθει νόησις)

english_head: by calling her Ethonoe; and then he himself or others afterwards improved the name, as they thought, and called her Athenaa. And how do you explain Hephaestus? You ask about the noble master of light? To be sure. Hephaestus is Phaestus, with the eta added by attraction; anyone could see that, I should think. Very likely, unless some other explanation occurs to you, as it probably will. To prevent that, ask about Ares. I do ask.

english_head: Ares, then, if you like, would be named for his virility and courage, or for his hard and unbending nature, which is called ἄρρατον; so Ares would be in every way a fitting name for the god of war. Certainly. For God’s sake, let us leave the gods, as I am afraid to talk about them; but ask me about any others you please, that you may see what Euthyphro’s horses are.

english_head: I will do so, but first one more god. I want to ask you about Hermes, since Cratylus says I am not Hermogenes (son of Hermes). Let us investigate the name of Hermes, to find out whether there is anything in what he says. Well then, this name Hermes seems to me to have to do with speech; he is an interpreter (ἡρμηνεύς) and a messenger,

english_head: is wily and deceptive in speech, and is oratorical. All this activity is concerned with the power of speech. Now, as I said before, εἴρειν denotes the use of speech; moreover, Homer often uses the word ἐμήσατο, which means contrive. From these two words, then, the lawgiver imposes upon us the name of this god who contrived speech and the use of speech—εἴρεινmeans speak—

greek_head: ἡμῖν ὁ νομοθέτης Ὦ ἄνθρωποι ὃς τὸ εἴρειν ἐμήσατο δικαίως ἂν καλοῖτο ὑπὸ ὑμῶν Εἰρέμης νῦν δὲ ἡμεῖς
greek_tokens: egw o nomoqeths eimi anqrwpos os o eirw mhdomai dikaios an kalew upo su eiremhs nun de egw ws oiomai kallwpizw o onoma ermhs kalew kai o ge iris apo o eirw eoika kalew ostis aggelos eimi nh o dios eu ara egw dokew kratulos legw o egw mh eimi ermogenhs oukoun eumhxanos ge eimi logos kai o ge o pan o ermhs eimi uios difuhs exis o eoika eimi etairos
english_head: and tells us: Ye human beings, he who contrived speech (εἴρειν ἐμήσατο) ought to be called Eiremes by you. We, however, have beautified the name, as we imagine, and call him Hermes. Iris also seems to have got her name from εἴρειν, because she is a messenger. By Zeus, I believe Cratylus was right in saying I was not Hermogenes; I certainly am no good contriver of speech. And it is reasonable, my friend, that Pan is the double-natured son of Hermes.

english_head: How is that? You know that speech makes all things (πᾶν) known and always makes them circulate and move about, and is twofold, true and false. Certainly. Well, the true part is smooth and divine and dwells aloft among the gods, but falsehood dwells below among common men, is rough and like the tragic goat; for tales and falsehoods are most at home there, in the tragic life. Certainly. Then Pan, who declares and always moves (ἀεὶ πολῶν) all, is rightly called goat-herd (αἰπόλος),

greek_head: Πὰν αἰπόλος εἴη διφυὴς Ἑρμοῦ ὑός τὰ μὲν ἄνωθεν λεῖος τὰ δὲ κάτωθεν τραχὺς καὶ τραγοειδής καὶ ἔστιν
greek_tokens: pas aipolos eaw difuhs ermhs us o men anwqen leios o de katwqen traxus kai tragoeidhs kai eimi htoi logos h logos adelfos o pas eiper ermhs us eimi adelfos de eoika adelfos oudeis qaumastos alla osper egw elegos eimi makarios apallassw ek o qea o ge toioutos eimi swkraths ei boulomai peri o toiosde de tis su kwluw dierxomai oios hlios te kai selhnh kai astron kai gaia kai aiqhr kai ahr kai pur
english_head: being the double-natured son of Hermes, smooth in his upper parts, rough and goat-like in his lower parts. And Pan, if he is the son of Hermes, is either speech or the brother of speech, and that brother resembles brother is not at all surprising. But, as I said, my friend, let us get away from the gods. From such gods as those, if you like, Socrates; but why should you not tell of another kind of gods, such as sun, moon, stars, earth,

greek_head: καὶ ὕδατος καὶ ὡρῶν καὶ ἐνιαυτοῦ Συχνὰ μέν μοι προστάττεις ὅμως δέ εἴπερ σοι
greek_tokens: kai udwr kai wra kai eniautos suxnos men egw prostassw omws de eiper su xarizomai eimi eqelw kai meis xarizomai tis dh oun proteros boulomai h wsper eipon o hlios dierxomai panu ge eoika toinun katadhlos gignomai an mallon ei
english_head: ether, air, fire, water, the seasons, and the year? You are imposing a good many tasks upon me; however, if it will give you pleasure, I am willing. It will give me pleasure. What, then, do you wish first? Shall we discuss the sun (Ἥλιος), as you mentioned it first? By all means. I think it would be clearer

greek_head: τῷ Δωρικῷ τις ὀνόματι χρῷτο ἅλιον γὰρ καλοῦσιν οἱ Δωριῆς ἅλιος οὖν εἴη μὲν ἂν κατὰ τὸ ἁλίζειν εἰς
greek_tokens: o dwrikos tis onoma xraomai alios gar kalew e dwriazw alios oun eaw men an kata o alizw eis tautos o anqrwpos epeidan anatellw eaw de an kai o peri o gaia aei eilew eimi eoika de an kai ostis poikillw eimi o gignomai ek o gaia o de poikillw kai aiolew tautos tis de o selhnh outos de o onoma fainw o anacagorhs piezw tis dh eoika dhlow palaios o ekeinos newsti legw
english_head: if we were to use the Doric form of the name. The Dorians call it Ἅλιος. Now ἅλιος might be derived from collecting (ἁλίζειν) men when he rises, or because he always turns (ἀεὶ εἱλεῖν) about the earth in his course, or because he variegates the products of the earth, for variegate is identical with αἰολλεῖν. And what of the moon, Selene? That name appears to put Anaxagoras in an uncomfortable position. How so? Why, it seems to have anticipated by many years the recent doctrine of Anaxagoras,

greek_head: ὅτι ἡ σελήνη ἀπὸ τοῦ ἡλίου ἔχει τὸ φῶς Πῶς δή
greek_tokens: ostis o selhnh apo o hlios exis o faos pws dh o men pou selas kai o faos tautos nai neos de pou kai enos aei eimi peri o selhnh outos o faos eiper alhqhs e anacagoreios legw kuklos gar pou aei autos perieimi neos aei epiballw enos de uparxw o o proteros meis panu ge selhnaih de ge kalew autos polus panu ge ostis de selas neos kai enos exis aei selaenoneoaeia
english_head: that the moon receives its light from the sun. How is that? Σέλας (gleam) and φῶς (light) are the same thing. Yes. Now the light is always new and old about the moon, if the Anaxagoreans are right; for they say the sun, in its continuous course about the moon, always sheds new light upon it, and the light of the previous month persists. Certainly. The moon is often called Σελαναία. Certainly. Because it has always a new and old gleam (σέλα νέον τε καὶ ἕνον)

greek_head: μὲν δικαιότατ' ἂν τῶν ὀνομάτων καλοῖτο συγκεκροτημένον δὲ Σελαναία κέκληται
greek_tokens: men dikaios an o onoma kalew sugkrotew de selhnaih kalew diqurambwdhs ge outos onoma eimi swkraths alla o mainomai kai o astron pws legw o men meis apo o meiow eaw an meihs orqos kalew o de astron eoika o astraph epwnumios exw o de astraph ostis o wy anastrefw anastrwph an eaw nun de astraph kallwpizw kalew tis de o pur kai o udwr
english_head: the very most fitting name for it would be Σελαενονεοάεια, which has been compressed into Σελαναία. That is a regular opera bouffe name, Socrates. But what have you to say of the month (μήν) and the stars? The word month (μείς) would be properly pronounced μείης, from μειοῦσθαι, to grow less, and I think the stars (ἄστερα) get their name from ἀστραπή (lightning). But ἀστραπή, because it turns our eyes upwards (τὰ ὦπα ἀναστρέθει), would be called ἀναστρωπή, which is now pronounced more prettily ἀστραπή. And what of πῦρ (fire) and ὕδωρ (water)?

greek_head: Τὸ πῦρ ἀπορῶ καὶ κινδυνεύει ἤτοι ἡ τοῦ Εὐθύφρονός με μοῦσα ἐπιλελοιπέναι ἢ τοῦτό τι παγχάλεπον εἶναι
greek_tokens: o pur aforaw kai kinduneuw htoi o o euqufrwn egw mousa epileipw h outos tis pagxalepos eimi skeptomai oun os eisagw mhxanh epi pas o toioutos o an aforaw tis dh egw su eraomai apokrinw gar egw exw an eipon pur kata tis tropos kalew ma diw ou egw skeptomai dh o egw upopteuw peri autos ennoew gar
english_head: Πῦρ is too much for me. It must be that either the muse of Euthyphro has deserted me or this is a very difficult word. Now just note the contrivance I introduce in all cases like this which are too much for me. What contrivance? I will tell you. Answer me; can you tell the reason of the word πῦρ? Not I, by Zeus. See what I suspect about it. I know that many Greeks,

greek_head: ὅτι πολλὰ οἱ Ἕλληνες ὀνόματα ἄλλως τε καὶ οἱ ὑπὸ τοῖς βαρβάροις οἰκοῦντες παρὰ τῶν βαρβάρων εἰλήφασιν
greek_tokens: ostis polus e ellhn onoma allos te kai e upo o barbaros oikew para o barbaros lambanw tis oun dh ei tis zhtew outos kata o ellhnikos fwnh ws eoikotws kew alla mh kata ekeinos ek eis o onoma tugxanw eimi oida ostis aforaw an eoikotws ge
english_head: especially those who are subject to the barbarians, have adopted many foreign words. What of that? If we should try to demonstrate the fitness of those words in accordance with the Greek language, and not in accordance with the language from which they are derived, you know we should get into trouble. Naturally.

greek_head: Ὅρα τοίνυν καὶ τοῦτο τὸ ὄνομα τὸ πῦρ μή τι βαρβαρικὸν ᾖ τοῦτο γὰρ οὔτε ῥᾴδιον προσάψαι ἐστὶν
greek_tokens: oraw toinun kai outos o onoma o pur mh tis barbarikos eimi outos gar oute radios prosaptw eimi ellhnikos fwnew faneros su eimi outws autos kalew frugw mikros tis paraklinw kai o ge udwr kai o kuwn kai allos polus eimi outos ou toinun deomai outos prosbiazomai epei exw ge an tis eipon peri autos o men oun pur kai o udwr outos
english_head: Well, this word πῦρ is probably foreign; for it is difficult to connect it with the Greek language, and besides, the Phrygians have the same word, only slightly altered. The same is the case with ὕδωρ (water), κύων (dog), and many other words. Yes, that is true. So we must not propose forced explanations of these words, though something might be said about them. I therefore set aside πῦρ and ὕδωρ in this way.

greek_head: ἀπωθοῦμαι ὁ δὲ δὴ ἀὴρ ἆρά γε ὦ Ἑρμόγενες ὅτι αἴρει τὰ ἀπὸ τῆς γῆς ἀὴρ κέκληται ἢ ὅτι ἀεὶ ῥεῖ ἢ ὅτι
greek_tokens: apwqew o de dh ahr ara ge eimi ermogenhs ostis airw o apo o gaia ahr kalew h ostis aei reomai h ostis pneuma ek autos gignomai rew e gar poihths pou o pneuma ahth kalew isos oun legw wsper an ei eipon pneumatorrous ahtorrous oqen dh boulomai autos outws eipon ostis eimi ahr o de aiqhr ode ph upolambanw ostis aei qew peri o ahr reon aeiqehr dikaios an kalew gaia de mallon shmainw o boulomai ean tis
english_head: But is air called ἀήρ because it raises (αἴρει) things from the earth, or because it is always flowing (ἀεὶ ῥεῖ), or because wind arises from its flow? The poets call the winds ἀήτας, blasts. Perhaps the poet means to say air-flow (ἀητόρρουν), as he might say wind-flow (πνευματόρρουν). The word αἴθηρ (ether) I understand in this way: because it always runs and flows about the air (ἀεὶ θεῖ περὶ τὸν ἀέρα ῥέον), it may properly be called ἀειθεήρα. The word γῆ (earth) shows the meaning better

greek_head: γαῖαν ὀνομάσῃ γαῖα γὰρ γεννήτειρα ἂν εἴη ὀρθῶς κεκλημένη ὥς φησιν Ὅμηρος τὸ γὰρ γεγάασιν γεγεννῆσθαι
greek_tokens: gaia onomazw gaia gar gennhteira an eaw orqos kalew os fhmi omhros o gar gignomai gennaw legw eien tis oun egw eimi o meta outos wra eimi swkraths kai eniautos kai etos o men dh wra attikisti ws o palaios rhteos eiper boulomai o eoika oida horai gar eimi dia o orizw xeimwn te kai qeros kai pneuma kai o karpos o ek o gaia orizw de dikaios an orai kalew
english_head: in the form γαῖα; for γαῖα is a correct word for mother, as Homer says, for he uses γεγάασιν to mean γεγενῆσθαι (be born). Well, now what came next? The seasons, Socrates, and the two words for year. The word ὧραι (seasons) should be pronounced in the old Attic fashion, ὅραι, if you wish to know the probable meaning; ΗΟΡΑΙ exist to divide winters and summers and winds and the fruits of the earth; and since they divide (ὁρίζουσι), they would rightly be called ὅραι.

greek_head: ἐνιαυτὸς δὲ καὶ ἔτος κινδυνεύει ἕν τι εἶναι τὸ γὰρ τὰ φυόμενα καὶ τὰ γιγνόμενα ἐν μέρει ἕκαστον προάγον εἰς
greek_tokens: eniautos de kai etos kinduneuw eis tis eimi o gar o fuw kai o gignomai en meros ekastos proagw eis faos kai autos en eautou ecetazw outos wsper en o prosqen o o zeus onoma dixa diairew e men zhnos e de dios kalew outws kai entauqa e men eniautos ostis en eautou e de etos ostis etazw o de oloc logos eimi o en eautou etazw outos prosagoreuw eis eimi dixa wste duo onoma gignomai eniautos te kai
english_head: The two words for year, ἐνιαυτός and ἔτος, are really one. For that which brings to light within itself the plants and animals, each in its turn, and examines them, is called by some ἐνιαυτός, because of its activity within itself (ἐν ἑαυτῷ), and by others ἔτος, because it examines (ἐτάζει), just as we saw before that the name of Zeus was divided and some said Δία and others Ζῆνα. The whole phrase is that which examines within itself (τὸ ἐν ἁυτῷ ἐτάζον), and this one phrase is divided in speech so that the two words ἐνιαυτός and ἔτος

greek_head: ἔτος ἐξ ἑνὸς λόγου Ἀλλὰ δῆτα ὦ Σώκρατες πολὺ ἐπιδίδως
greek_tokens: etos ek eis logos alla dhta eimi swkraths polus epididwmi prosw hdh oimh fainw sofia elaunw panu men oun taxa mallon fhmi
english_head: are formed from one phrase. Truly, Socrates, you are going ahead at a great rate. Yes, I fancy I am already far along on the road of wisdom. I am sure you are. You will be surer presently.

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μετὰ τοῦτο τὸ εἶδος ἔγωγε ἡδέως ἂν θεασαίμην ταῦτα τὰ καλὰ ὀνόματα τίνι ποτὲ ὀρθότητι
greek_tokens: alla meta outos o eidos egw hdews an qeaomai outos o kalos onoma tis pote orqoths kew o peri o areth oios fronhsis te kai sunesis kai dikaiosunh kai allos o toioutos pas egeirw men eimi etairos ou faulos genos onoma omws de epei o leonteh enduw ou apodeiliateon alla episkepteos ws eoika fronhsis kai sunesis kai gnwmh kai episthmh kai allos dh o fhmi pas outos o
english_head: Now after the class of words you, have explained, I should like to examine the correctness of the noble words that relate to virtue, such as wisdom, intelligence, justice, and all the others of that sort. You are stirring up a mighty tribe of words, my friend; however, since I have put on the lion helmet, I must not play the coward, but must, it seems, examine wisdom, intelligence, thought, knowledge,

greek_head: καλὰ ὀνόματα Πάνυ μὲν οὖν οὐ δεῖ ἡμᾶς προαποστῆναι
greek_tokens: kalos onoma panu men oun ou deomai egw proafistamai kai meis nh o kuwn dokazw ge egw ou kakos manteuomai o kai nundi ennoew ostis e panu palaios anqrwpos e tiqhmi o onoma pas mallon wsper kai o nun e polus o sofos upo o puknos peristrefw zhtew oph exis o eimi eiliggiaw epeita autos fainw periferw o pragma kai pantws
english_head: and all the other noble words of which you speak. Certainly we must not stop until that is done. By dog, I believe I have a fine intuition which has just come to me, that the very ancient men who invented names were quite like most of the present philosophers who always get dizzy as they turn round and round in their search for the nature of things, and then the things seem to them to turn round and round and be in motion.

greek_head: φέρεσθαι αἰτιῶνται δὴ οὐ τὸ ἔνδον τὸ παρὰ σφίσιν πάθος αἴτιον εἶναι ταύτης τῆς δόξης ἀλλὰ αὐτὰ τὰ πράγματα
greek_tokens: ferw aitiaomai dh ou o endon o para sfeis paqos aitios eimi outos o doca alla autos o pragma outws fuw oudeis autos monimos eimi oude bebaios alla rew kai ferw kai mestos eimi pas fora kai genesis aei legos dh ennoew pros pas o nundi onoma pws dh outos eimi swkraths ou katanoew isos o arti legw ostis pantapasi ws ferw te kai rew kai gignomai o pragma o onoma epikeimai ou panu enqumeomai
english_head: They think the cause of this belief is not an affection within themselves, but that the nature of things really is such that nothing is at rest or stable, but everything is flowing and moving and always full of constant motion and generation. I say this because I thought of it with reference to all these words we are now considering. How is that, Socrates? Perhaps you did not observe that the names we just mentioned are given under the assumption that the things named are moving and flowing and being generated. No, I did not notice that at all.

greek_head: Καὶ μὴν πρῶτον μὲν τοῦτο ὃ πρῶτον εἴπομεν παντάπασιν ὡς ἐπὶ τοιούτων ἐστίν
greek_tokens: kai meis proteros men outos o proteros eipon pantapasi ws epi toioutos eimi o poios o fronhsis fora gar eimi kai reomai nohsis eaw de an kai onhsis upolambanw fora alla oun peri ge o ferw eimi ei de boulomai o gnwmh pantapasi dhlow gonaw skeyis kai nwmhsis o gar nwmaw kai o skopaw tautos ei de boulomai autos o nohsis o neos eimi esis o de naus eimi o eimi shmainw
english_head: Surely the first one we mentioned is subject to such assumptions. What is the word ? Wisdom (φρόνησις); for it is perception (νόησις) of motion (φορᾶς) and flowing (ῥοῦ); or it might be understood as benefit (ὄνησις) of motion (φορᾶς); in either case it has to do with motion. And γνώμη (thought), if you please, certainly denotes contemplation and consideration of generation (γονῆς νώμησις); for to consider is the same as to contemplate. Or, if you please, νόησις (intelligence) is merely ἕσις (desire) τοῦ νεοῦ (of the new); but that things are new shows that they are always being generated;

greek_head: γιγνόμενα ἀεὶ εἶναι τούτου οὖν ἐφίεσθαι τὴν ψυχὴν μηνύει τὸ ὄνομα ὁ θέμενος τὴν νεόεσιν οὐ γὰρ νόησις τὸ
greek_tokens: gignomai aei eimi outos oun efihmi o yuxh mhnuw o onoma o tiqhmi o neoesin ou gar nohsis o arxaios kalew alla anti o hta ei dew legw duo noeesin swfrosunos de swthria e nundi skeptomai
english_head: therefore the soul’s desire for generation is declared by the giver of the name νεόεσις; for in antiquity the name was not νόησις, but two epsilons had to be spoken instead of the eta. Σωφροσύνη (self-restraint) is σωτηρία (salvation) of φρόνησις (wisdom), which we have just been discussing.

greek_head: φρονήσεως καὶ μὴν ἥ γε ἐπιστήμη μηνύει ὡς φερομένοις τοῖς πράγμασιν ἑπομένης τῆς ψυχῆς τῆς ἀξίας
greek_tokens: fronhsis kai meis o ge episthmh mhnuw ws ferw o pragma epomai o yuxazw o acios logos kai oute apoleipw oute proqew dio dh emballw deomai o ei epeisthmhn autos onomazw sunesis de au outws men dokew an wsper sullogismos eimi otan de suneimi legos tautos pantapasi o efisthmi sumbainw legw sumporeuomai gar legw
english_head: And ἐπιστήμη (knowledge) indicates that the soul which is of any account accompanies (ἕπεται) things in their motion, neither falling behind them nor running in front of them; therefore we ought to insert an epsilon and call it ἐπεϊστήμη. Σύνεσις (intelligence) in its turn is a kind of reckoning together; when one says συνιέναι (understand), the same thing as ἐπίστασθαι is said;

greek_head: τὴν ψυχὴν τοῖς πράγμασι τὸ συνιέναι ἀλλὰ μὴν ἥ γε σοφία φορᾶς ἐφάπτεσθαι σημαίνει σκοτωδέστερον δὲ
greek_tokens: o yuxh o pragma o suneimi alla meis o ge sofia fora efaptw shmainw skotwdhs de outos kai cenikos alla deomai ek o poihths anamimnhskw ostis pollaxou legw peri ostis an tugxanw o arxw taxus proeimi seuw fhmi lakwnikos de andris o eudokimos kai onoma eimi shs o gar taxus ormh e lakedaimonios outos kalew outos oun o fora epafh shmainw o sofia ws ferw o eimi
english_head: for συνιέναι means that the soul goes with things. Certainly σοφία (wisdom) denotes the touching of motion. This word is very obscure and of foreign origin; but we must remember that the poets often say of something which begins to advance ἐσύθη (it rushed). There was a famous Laconian whose name was Σοῦς (Rush), for this is the Laconian word for rapid motion. Now σοφία signifies the touching (ἐπαφή) of this rapid motion, the assumption being that things are in motion. And the word ἀγαθόν (good)

greek_head: καὶ μὴν τό γε ἀγαθόν τοῦτο τῆς φύσεως πάσης τῷ ἀγαστῷ βούλεται τὸ ὄνομα ἐπικεῖσθαι ἐπειδὴ γὰρ πορεύεται
greek_tokens: kai meis o ge agaqos outos o fusis pas o agastos boulomai o onoma epikeimai epei gar poreuw o eimi en men ara autos taxos en de braduths eimi oun ou pas o taxus alla tis autos agastos o qoos dh o agastos outos o epwnumios eimi agaqos dikaiosunh de ostis men epi o o dikaios suneimi outos kew o onoma radios sumballw autos de o dikaios xalepos kai gar dh kai eoika mexri men tis omologew
english_head: is intended to denote the admirable (ἀγαστόν) in all nature. For since all things are in motion, they possess quickness and slowness; now not all that is swift, but only part of it, is admirable; this name ἀγαθόν is therefore given to the admirable (ἀγαστόν) part of the swift (θοοῦ). It is easy to conjecture that the word δικαιασύνη applies to the understanding (σύνεσις) of the just (τοῦ διαίον) but the word δίκαιον (just) is itself difficult. Up to a certain point, you see, many men seem to agree about it, but beyond that they differ.

greek_head: παρὰ πολλῶν ἔπειτα δὲ ἀμφισβητεῖσθαι ὅσοι γὰρ ἡγοῦνται τὸ πᾶν εἶναι ἐν πορείᾳ τὸ μὲν πολὺ αὐτοῦ ὑπολαμβάνουσιν
greek_tokens: para polus epeita de amfisbhtew osos gar hgeomai o pas eimi en poreia o men polus autos upolambanw toioutos tis eimi oios oudeis allos h xwrew dia de outos pas eimi tis dieceimi dia e pas o gignomai gignomai eimi de taxistos outos kai leptos ou gar an dunamai allos dia o eimi eimi pas ei mh leptos te eimi wste autos mhdeis stegw kai taxistos wste xraomai wsper isthmi o allos epei de oun epitropeuw o
english_head: For those who think the universe is in motion believe that the greater part of it is of such a nature as to be a mere receptacle, and that there is some element which passes through all this, by means of which all created things are generated. And this element must be very rapid and very subtle; for it could not pass through all the universe unless it were very subtle, so that nothing could keep it out, and it must be very swift, so that all other things are relatively at rest. Since, then, it superintends and passes through (διαϊόν) all other things,

greek_head: ἄλλα πάντα διαϊόν τοῦτο τὸ ὄνομα ἐκλήθη ὀρθῶς δίκαιον εὐστομίας ἕνεκα τὴν τοῦ κάππα δύναμιν προσλαβόν μέχρι
greek_tokens: allos pas dieimi outos o onoma kalew orqos dikaios eustomia eneka o o kappa dunamis proslambanw mexri men oun entauqa o nundi legw para polus omologew
english_head: this is rightly called by the name δίκαιον, the sound of the kappa being added merely for the sake of euphony. Up to this point, as I said just now, many men agree about justice (δίκαιον);

greek_head: τοῦτο εἶναι τὸ δίκαιον ἐγὼ δέ ὦ Ἑρμόγενες ἅτε λιπαρὴς ὢν περὶ αὐτοῦ ταῦτα μὲν πάντα διαπέπυσμαι ἐν
greek_tokens: outos eimi o dikaios egw de eimi ermogenhs ate liparhs eimi peri autos outos men pas diapunqanomai en aporrhtos ostis outos eimi o dikaios kai o aitios dia o gar gignomai outos eimi o aitios kai dios kalew fhmi tis outos orqos exw dia outos epeidan de hrema autos epanerwtaw aekwn outos mhdeis hsswn tis oun pote eimi eimi aristos dikaios ei outos outws exis dokazw te hdh makros o proshkw erwtaw kai uper o skaptw
english_head: and I, Hermogenes, being very much in earnest about it, have persistently asked questions and have been told in secret teachings that this is justice, or the cause—for that through which creation takes place is a cause—and some one told me that it was for this reason rightly called Zeus (Δία). But when, after hearing this, I nevertheless ask them quietly, What then, my most excellent friend, if this is true, is justice? they think I am asking too many questions and am leaping over the trenches.

greek_head: ἅλλεσθαι ἱκανῶς γάρ μέ φασι πεπύσθαι ἀκηκοέναι καὶ ἐπιχειροῦσιν βουλόμενοι ἀποπιμπλάναι με ἄλλος ἄλλα ἤδη
greek_tokens: allomai ikanos gar egw fhmi punqanomai akouw kai epixeirew boulomai apopimplhmi egw allos allos hdh legw kai ouketi sumfwnew o men gar tis fhmi outos eimi dikaios o hlios outos gar monos dieimi kai kaiw epitropeuw o eimi epeidan oun tis legos autos asmenos ws kalos tis akouw katagelaw egw outos aekwn kai erwtaw ei oudeis dikaios oimh eimi en o anqrwpos epeidan
english_head: They say I have been told enough; they try to satisfy me by saying all sorts of different things, and they no longer agree. For one says the sun is justice, for the sun alone superintends all things, passing through and burning (διαϊόντα καὶ καίοντα) them. Then when I am pleased and tell this to some one, thinking it is a fine answer, he laughs at me and asks if I think there is no justice among men when the sun has set. So I beg him to tell me what he thinks it is,

greek_head: ὁ ἥλιος δύῃ λιπαροῦντος οὖν ἐμοῦ ὅτι αὖ ἐκεῖνος λέγει αὐτό τὸ πῦρ φησιν τοῦτο δὲ οὐ ῥᾴδιόν ἐστιν εἰδέναι ὁ δὲ
greek_tokens: o hlios duh liparew oun egw ostis au ekeinos legw autos o pur fhmi outos de ou radios eimi oida o de ou autos o pur fhmi alla autos o qermos o en o pur eneimi o de outos men pas katagelaw fhmi eimi de o dikaios o legw anacagorhs new eimi outos autokratwr gar autos eimi kai oudeis mignumi pas fhmi autos kosmew o pragma dia pas eimi entauqa dh egw eimi filos polus en pleiwn aporia eimi h prin epixeirew manqanw
english_head: and he says Fire. But this is not easy to understand. He says it is not actual fire, but heat in the abstract that is in the fire. Another man says he laughs at all these notions, and that justice is what Anaxagoras says it is, mind; for mind, he says, is ruled only by itself, is mixed with nothing, orders all things, and passes through them. Then, my friend, I am far more perplexed than before I undertook to learn about the nature of justice.

greek_head: περὶ τοῦ δικαίου ὅτι ποτ' ἔστιν ἀλλ' οὖν οὗπερ ἕνεκα ἐσκοποῦμεν τό γε ὄνομα τοῦτο φαίνεται αὐτῷ διὰ ταῦτα κεῖσθαι
greek_tokens: peri o dikaios ostis pote eimi alla oun ouper eneka skopaw o ge onoma outos fainw autos dia outos kew fainw egw eimi swkraths outos men akouw tis kai ou autosxediazw tis de allos ou panu akouw dh isos gar an su kai o epiloipos ecapataw ws ou akouw legos meta gar dikaiosunh tis egw leipw andreia oimh oupw dierxomai adikia men gar
english_head: But I think the name—and that was the subject of our investigation—was given for the reasons I have mentioned. I think, Socrates, you must have heard this from some one and are not inventing it yourself. And how about the rest of my talk? I do not at all think you had heard that. Listen then; perhaps I may deceive you into thinking that all I am going to say is my own. What remains to consider after justice? I think we have not yet discussed courage.

greek_head: δῆλον ὅτι ἐστὶν ὄντος ἐμπόδισμα τοῦ διαϊόντος ἀνδρεία δὲ σημαίνει ὡς ἐν μάχῃ ἐπονομαζομένης τῆς ἀνδρείας μάχην
greek_tokens: dhlos ostis eimi eimi empodisma o dieimi andreia de shmainw ws en maxh eponomazw o andreia maxh de eimi en o eimi eiper reomai ou allos tis h o enantios roh ean oun tis ecairew o delta o onoma o andreia autos mhnuw o ergnumi o onoma o andreia dhlos oun ostis ou paomai roh o enantios roh andreia eimi alla o para
english_head: It is plain enough that injustice (ἀδικία) is really a mere hindrance of that which passes through (τοῦ διαϊόντος, but the word ἀδρεία (courage) implies that courage got its name in battle, and if the universe is flowing, a battle in the universe can be nothing else than an opposite current or flow (ῥοή). Now if we remove the delta from the word ἀνδρεία, the word ἀνρεία signifies exactly that activity. Of course it is clear that not the current opposed to every current is courage, but only that opposed to the current which is contrary to justice;

greek_head: τὸ δίκαιον ῥεούσῃ οὐ γὰρ ἂν ἐπῃνεῖτο ἡ ἀνδρεία καὶ τὸ ἄρρεν καὶ ὁ ἀνὴρ ἐπὶ παραπλησίῳ τινὶ τούτῳ ἐστί
greek_tokens: o dikaios rew ou gar an epainew o andreia kai o arshn kai o anhr epi paraplhsios tis outos eimi o anoos roh gunh de gonh egw fainw boulomai eimi o de qhlus apo o qhlazw tis fainw eponomazw o de qhlh ara ge eimi ermogenhs ostis qallw poiew wsper o ardw eoika ge eimi swkraths kai meis autos ge o qallw o auch egw dokew apeikazw o o neos ostis taxus kai ecaifnidios gignomai
english_head: for otherwise courage would not be praised. The words ἄρρεν (male) and ἀνήρ (man) refer, like ἀνδρεία, to the upward (ἄνω) current or flow. The word γυνή (woman) seems to me to be much the same as γονή (birth). I think θῆλυ (female) is derived from θηλή (teat); and is not θηλή, Hermogenes, so called because it makes things flourish (τεθηλέναι), like plants wet with showers? Very likely, Socrates. And again, the word θάλλειν (flourish) seems to me to figure the rapid and sudden growth of the young.

greek_head: οἷόνπερ οὖν μεμίμηται τῷ ὀνόματι συναρμόσας ἀπὸ τοῦ θεῖν καὶ ἅλλεσθαι τὸ ὄνομα ἀλλ' οὐ γὰρ ἐπισκοπεῖς με
greek_tokens: oionper oun mimeomai o onoma sunarmozw apo o qew kai allomai o onoma alla ou gar episkopew egw wsper ektos dromos ferw epeidan leios epilambanw epiloipos de egw eti suxnos o dokew spoudaios eimi alhqhs legw os ge eimi eis kai texnh eidon ostis pote boulomai eimi panu men oun oukoun outos ge ecis neomai shmainw o men tau
english_head: Something of that sort the namegiver has reproduced in the name, which he compounded of θεῖν (run) and ἅλλεσθαι (jump). You do not seem to notice how I rush along outside of the race-course, when I get on smooth ground. But we still have plenty of subjects left which seem to be serious. True. One of which is to see what the word τέχνη (art, science) means. Certainly. Does not this denote possession of mind, if you remove the tau and insert omicron between the chi and the nu

greek_head: ἀφελόντι ἐμβαλόντι δὲ οὖ μεταξὺ τοῦ χεῖ καὶ τοῦ νῦ καὶ τοῦ νῦ καὶ τοῦ ἦτα
greek_tokens: afairew emballw de ou metacu o xaw kai o nun kai o nun kai o hta kai mala ge glisxros eimi swkraths eimi makarios ou oistos ostis o proteros onoma tiqhmi kataxwnnumi hdh upo o boulomai tragwdew autos peritiqhmi gramma kai ecairew eustomia eneka kai pantaxh strefw kai upo kallwpismos kai upo xronos epei en o katoptron ou dokew su atopos eimi o emballw o razw alla toioutos oimh poiew e o men
english_head: and the nu and the eta (making ἐχονόη)? It does it very poorly, Socrates. My friend, you do not bear in mind that the original words have before now been completely buried by those who wished to dress them up, for they have added and subtracted letters for the sake of euphony and have distorted the words in every way for ornamentation or merely in the lapse of time. Do you not, for instance, think it absurd that the letter rho is inserted in the word κάαπτρον (mirror)?

greek_head: ἀληθείας οὐδὲν φροντίζοντες τὸ δὲ στόμα πλάττοντες ὥστ' ἐπεμβάλλοντες πολλὰ ἐπὶ τὰ πρῶτα ὀνόματα τελευτῶντες
greek_tokens: alhqeia oudeis frontizw o de stoma plassw wste epemballw polus epi o proteros onoma teleutaw poiew mhde an enos anqrwpos suneimi ostis pote boulomai o onoma wsper kai o sfigc anti fikos sfigc kalew kai allos polus outos men eimi outws eimi swkraths ei de au tis eaw kai entiqhmi kai ecairew ostis an boulomai tis eis o onoma polus euporia eimi kai pas an pas tis onoma pragma prosarmozw
english_head: I think that sort of thing is the work of people who care nothing for truth, but only for the shape of their mouths; so they keep adding to the original words until finally no human being can understand what in the world the word means. So the sphinx, for instance, is called sphinx, instead of phix, and there are many other examples. Yes, that is true, Socrates. And if we are permitted to insert and remove any letters we please in words, it will be perfectly easy to fit any name to anything.

greek_head: Ἀληθῆ λέγεις Ἀληθῆ μέντοι ἀλλὰ τὸ μέτριον οἶμαι δεῖ φυλάττειν
greek_tokens: alhqhs legw alhqhs mentoi alla o metrios oimh deomai fulassw kai o eoika sos o sofos efisthmi boulomai an kai egw su sumboulomai eimi ermogenhs alla mh
english_head: True. Yes, quite true. But I think you, as a wise director, must observe the rule of moderation and probability. I should like to do so. And I, too, Hermogenes.

greek_head: λίαν ὦ δαιμόνιε ἀκριβολογοῦ μή μ' ἀπογυιώσῃς μένεος
greek_tokens: lian eimi daimonios akribologeomai mh egw apoguiow menos erxomai gar epi o korufh os erw epeidan meta texnh mhxanh episkeptomai mhxanh gar egw dokew o anw epi polus shmeion eimi o gar mhkos pws o polus shmainw ek amfw oun outos sugkeimai mhkos te kai o anw o onoma o mhxanh alla osper nundi eipon epi o korufh deomai o erw erxomai areth gar kai kakia ostis boulomai o onoma
english_head: But do not, my friend, demand too much precision, lest you enfeeble me of my sight.Hom. Il. 6.265 For now that τέχνη (art) is disposed of, I am nearing the loftiest height of my subject, when once we have investigated μηχανή(contrivance). For I think μηχανή signifies ἄνειν ἐπὶ πολύ (much accomplishment); for μῆκος (length) has about the same meaning as τὸ πολύ (much), and the name μηχανή is composed of these two, μῆκος and ἄνειν. But, as I was just saying, we must go on to the loftiest height of our subject; we must search for the meaning of the words ἀρετή (virtue) and κακία (wickedness). Now one of them I cannot yet see;

greek_head: ζητητέα τὸ μὲν οὖν ἕτερον οὔπω καθορῶ τὸ δ' ἕτερον δοκεῖ μοι κατάδηλον εἶναι συμφωνεῖ γὰρ τοῖς ἔμπροσθεν
greek_tokens: zhthteos o men oun eteros oupw kaqoraw o de eteros dokew egw katadhlos eimi sumfwnew gar o emprosqen pas ate gar eimi o pragma pas o kakos eimi kakia an eaw outos de otan en yuxw eimi o kakos eimi epi o pragma malista o o oloc epwnumios exis o kakia o de kakos eimi ostis pote eimi dokew egw dhlow kai en o deilia o oupw dierxomai alla
english_head: but the other seems to be quite clear, since it agrees with everything we have said before. For inasmuch as all things are in motion, everything that moves badly (κακῶς ἰόν) would be evil (κακία); and when this evil motion in relation to its environment exists in the soul, it receives the general name κακία (evil) in the special sense of wickedness. But the nature of evil motion (κακῶς ἰέναι) is made clear, I think, also in the word δειλία (cowardice), which we have not yet discussed. We passed it by,

greek_head: ὑπερέβημεν δέον αὐτὸ μετὰ τὴν ἀνδρείαν σκέψασθαι δοκοῦμεν δέ μοι καὶ ἄλλα πολλὰ ὑπερβεβηκέναι ἡ δ' οὖν
greek_tokens: uperbainw deon autos meta o andreia skeptomai dokew de egw kai allos polus uperbainw o de oun deilia o yuxazw shmainw desmos eimi isxuros o gar lian isxus tis eimi desmos oun o lian kai o megas o yuxazw o deilia an eaw wsper ge kai o aporia kakos kai pas ws eoika ostis an empodwn eimi o eimi kai poreuw outos oun fainw o kakos eimi dhlow o isxw te kai empodizw poreuw o dh yuxh otan exis kakia mestos gignomai ei de epi toioutos o kakia eimi onoma enantion outos o areth an eaw shmainw proteros
english_head: when we ought to have examined it after ἀνδρεία (courage); and I fancy we passed over a good many other words. Now the meaning of δειλία is a strong bond of the soul; for λίαν (excessively) is, in a way, expressive of strength; so δειλία would be the excessive or greatest bond (δεσμός, δεῖν) of the soul; and so, too, ἀπορία (perplexity) is an evil, as is everything, apparently, which hinders motion and progress (πορεύεσθαι). This, then, seems to be the meaning of evil motion (κακῶς ἰέναι), that advance is halting and impeded; and the soul that is infected by it becomes filled with wickedness (κακία). If these are the reasons for the name of wickedness, virtue (ἀρετή) would be the opposite of this; it would signify first ease of motion,

greek_head: μὲν εὐπορίαν ἔπειτα δὲ λελυμένην τὴν ῥοὴν τῆς ἀγαθῆς ψυχῆς εἶναι ἀεί ὥστε τὸ ἀσχέτως καὶ τὸ ἀκωλύτως
greek_tokens: men euporia epeita de luw o roh o agaqos yuxazw eimi aei wste o asxetos kai o akwlutos aei reon epwnumios lambanw ws eoika outos onoma o orqos men exis aeireitos kalew isos de aireths legw ws eimi outos o ecis airetos sugkrotew de kai kalew areth kai isos egw au fhmi plassw egw de fhmi eiper o emprosqen eipon orqos exis o kakia
english_head: and secondly that the flow of the good soul is always unimpeded, and therefore it has received this name, which designates that which always flows (ἀεὶ ῥέον) without let or hindrance. It is properly called ἀειρειτή, or perhaps also αἱρετή, indicating that this condition is especially to be chosen; but it has been compressed and is pronounced ἀρετή. Perhaps you will say this is another invention of mine; but I say if what I said just now about κακία is right,

greek_head: καὶ τοῦτο τὸ ὄνομα τὴν ἀρετὴν ὀρθῶς ἔχειν
greek_tokens: kai outos o onoma o areth orqos exw
english_head: this about the name of ἀρετή is right too.

greek_head: Τὸ δὲ δὴ κακόν δι' οὗ πολλὰ τῶν ἔμπροσθεν εἴρηκας τί ἂν νοοῖ τοὔνομα
greek_tokens: o de dh kakos dia e polus o emprosqen erw tis an noew onoma atopos tis nh dios egw dokew kai xalepos sumballw epagw oun kai outos ekeinos o mhxanh poa outos o o barbarikos tis kai outos fainw eimi kai eoika ge orqos legw alla ei dokew outos men eaw o de kalos kai o aisxros peirw eidon ph eulogos exis o men toinun aisxros kai dh katadhlos egw
english_head: But what is the meaning of the word κακόν which you used in many of your derivations? By Zeus, I think it is a strange word and hard to understand; so I apply to it that contrivance of mine. What contrivance? The claim of foreign origin, which I advance in this case as in those others. Well, probably you are right. But, if you please, let us drop these words and try to discover the reasons for the words καλόν (beautiful, noble) and αἰσχρόν (base). I think the meaning of αἰσχρόν is clear,

greek_head: φαίνεται ὃ νοεῖ καὶ τοῦτο γὰρ τοῖς ἔμπροσθεν ὁμολογεῖται τὸ γὰρ ἐμποδίζον καὶ ἴσχον τῆς ῥοῆς τὰ ὄντα λοιδορεῖν μοι
greek_tokens: fainw o noew kai outos gar o emprosqen omologew o gar empodizw kai isxw o roh o eimi loidorew egw fainw dia pas o o onoma tiqhmi kai nun o aei isxw o roos outos o onoma eqw o aeisxoroun nun de sugkrotew aisxros kalew tis de o kalos outos xalepos katanoew kaitoi legw ge autos armonia monos kai mhkos o ou paragw pws dh o dianoia tis eoika epwnumios eimi outos o onoma pws legw
english_head: and this also agrees with what has been said before. For the giver of names appears to me throughout to denounce that which hinders and restrains things from flowing, and in this instance he gave to that which always restrains the flow (ἀεὶ ἴσχει τὸν ῥοῦν) this name ἀεισχοροῦν, which is now compressed and pronounced αἰσχρόν. What about καλόν? That is harder to understand, and yet it expresses its meaning: it has been altered merely in accent and in the length of the O.

greek_head: Φέρε τί οἴει σὺ εἶναι τὸ αἴτιον κληθῆναι ἑκάστῳ τῶν ὄντων ἆρ' οὐκ ἐκεῖνο τὸ τὰ ὀνόματα θέμενον
greek_tokens: ferw tis oiomai su eimi o aitios kalew ekastos o eimi ara ou ekeinos o o onoma tiqhmi pantws pou oukoun dianoia an eaw outos htoi qea h anqrwpos h amfoteros nai oukoun o kalew o pragma kai o kalew tautos eimi outos dianoia fainw oukoun kai osos men an noos te kai dianoia ergazomai outos eimi o epainetos o de mh yektos panu ge
english_head: How is that? I think this word denotes intellect. What do you mean? Why, what do you think is the cause why anything is called by a name? Is it not the power which gave the name? Why, certainly. And is not that power the intellect either of gods or of men or both? Yes. Are not that which called things by name and that which calls them by name (τὸ καλοῦν) the same thing, namely intellect? Yes, clearly. And are not all works which are done by mind and intelligence worthy of praise, and those that are not done by them worthy of blame? Certainly.

greek_head: Τὸ οὖν ἰατρικὸν ἰατρικὰ ἐργάζεται καὶ τὸ τεκτονικὸν τεκτονικά ἢ πῶς λέγεις
greek_tokens: o oun iatrikos iatrikos ergazomai kai o tektonikos tektonikos h pws legw outws egw kai o kalew ara kalos deomai ge su eimi de ge outos os fhmi dianoia panu ge orqos ara fronhsis outos o epwnumios eimi o kalos o o toioutos apergazomai o dh kalos faskw eimi aspazomai fainw
english_head: Does not the medical power perform medical works and the power of carpentry works of carpentry? Do you agree to that? I agree. And the beautiful performs beautiful works? It must do so. And the beautiful is, we say, intellect? Certainly. Then this name, the beautiful, is rightly given to mind, since it accomplishes the works which we call beautiful and in which we delight. Evidently.

greek_head: Τί οὖν ἔτι ἡμῖν λοιπὸν τῶν τοιούτων Ταῦτα τὰ περὶ τὸ ἀγαθόν τε καὶ καλόν συμφέροντά
greek_tokens: tis oun eti egw loipos o toioutos outos o peri o agaqos te kai kalos sumferw
english_head: What further words of this sort are left for us? Those that are related to the good and the beautiful,

greek_head: τε καὶ λυσιτελοῦντα καὶ ὠφέλιμα καὶ κερδαλέα καὶ τἀναντία τούτων
greek_tokens: te kai lusitelew kai wfelimos kai kerdaleos kai enantios outos oukoun o men sumferw hdh pou kan su euriskw ek o proteros episkopew o gar episthmh adelfos tis fainw oudeis gar allos dhlow h o ama fora o yuxazw meta o pragma kai o upo o toioutos prassw sumferw te kai sumforos kalew apo o sumperiferw eoika o de ge kerdaleos apo o
english_head: such as συμφέροντα (advantageous), λυσιτελοῦντα (profitable), ὠφέλιμα (useful), κερδαλέα (gainful), and their opposites. You might by this time be able to find the meaning of συμφέροντα by yourself in the light of the previous explanations, for it appears to be own brother to ἐπιστήμη. It means nothing else but the motion (φορά) of the soul in company with the world, and naturally things which are done by such a power are called συμφέροντα and σύμφορα because they are carried round with (συμπεριφέρεσθαι) the world. But κερδαλέον is from κέρδος (gain).

greek_head: κέρδους κέρδος δὲ νῦ ἀντὶ τοῦ δέλτα ἀποδιδόντι ἐς τὸ ὄνομα δηλοῖ ὃ βούλεται τὸ γὰρ ἀγαθὸν κατ' ἄλλον τρόπον
greek_tokens: kerdos kerdos de nun anti o delta apodidwmi eis o onoma dhlow o boulomai o gar agaqos kata allos tropos onomazw ostis gar kerannumi eis pas dieceimi outos autos o dunamis eponomazw eqw onoma delta de entiqhmi anti o nun kerdos fqeggomai lusitelew de tis dh eoika eimi ermogenhs ou kaqa e kaphlos autos xraomai ean o analwma apoluw ou outos legw
english_head: If you restore nu in the word κέρδος in place of the delta, the meaning is plain; it signifies good, but in another way. Because it passes through and is mingled (κεράννυται) with all things, he who named it gave it this name which indicates that function; but he inserted a delta instead of nu and said κέρδος. And what is λυσιτελοῦν? I do not think, Hermogenes, the name-giver gives the meaning to λυσιτελοῦν which it has in the language of tradesfolk, when profit sets free (ἀπολύει) the sum invested,

greek_head: μοι δοκεῖ τὸ λυσιτελοῦν ἀλλ' ὅτι τάχιστον ὂν τοῦ ὄντος ἵστασθαι οὐκ ἐᾷ τὰ πράγματα οὐδὲ τέλος λαβοῦσαν τὴν
greek_tokens: egw dokew o lusitelew alla ostis taxistos eimi o eimi isthmi ou eaw o pragma oude telos lambanw o fora o ferw isthmi te kai pauw alla aei luw autos an tis epixeirew telos eggignomai kai paraxew apaustos kai aqanatos autos outos egw dokew epifhmizw o agaqos lusitelew o gar o fora luw o telos lusitelew kalew wfelimos de cenikos onoma os kai omhros pollaxou xraw o ofellw eimi de outos o aucanw kai poiew epwnumios
english_head: but he means that because it is the swiftest thing in the world it does not allow things to remain at rest and does not allow the motion to come to any end (τέλος) of movement or to stop or pause, but always, if any end of the motion is attempted, it sets it free, making it unceasing and immortal. It is in this sense, I think, that the good is dubbed λυσιτελοῦν, for it frees (λύει) the end (τέλος) of the motion. But the word ὠφέλιμον is a foreign one, which Homer often uses in the verbal form ὀφέλλειν. This is a synonym of increase and create.

greek_head: Τὰ δὲ δὴ τούτων ἐναντία πῶς ἔχει ἡμῖν Ὅσα μὲν ἀπόφησιν αὐτῶν ὥς γέ μοι δοκεῖ οὐδὲν
greek_tokens: o de dh outos enantios pws exis egw osos men apofhmi autos os ge egw dokew oudeis deomai outos dieceimi poios outos asumforos kai anwfelhs kai alusitelhs kai akerdhs alhqhs legw alla blaberos ge kai zhmiwdhs nai kai o men ge blaberos o blaptw o
english_head: What shall be our explanations of the opposites of these? Those of them that are mere negatives, need, I think, no discussion. Which are those? Disadvantageous, useless, unprofitable, and ungainful. True. But βλαβερόν (harmful) and ζημιῶδες (hurtful) do need it. Yes. And βλαβερόν means that which harms (βλάπτον) the flow (ῥοῦν);

greek_head: ῥοῦν εἶναι λέγει τὸ δὲ βλάπτον αὖ σημαίνει βουλόμενον ἅπτειν τὸ δὲ ἅπτειν καὶ δεῖν ταὐτόν ἐστι τοῦτο
greek_tokens: roos eimi legw o de blaptw au shmainw boulomai aptw o de aptw kai dew tautos eimi outos de pantaxou yegw o boulomai oun aptw roos orqos men an eaw boulapteroun kallwpizw de kalew egw fainw blaberos poikilos ge su eimi swkraths ekbainw o onoma kai gar nun egw dokew wsper o o aqhnaas nomos proaulion stomaulew outos o onoma proeipon o
english_head: but βλάπτον means wishing to fasten (ἅπτειν), and ἅπτειν is the same thing as δεῖν (bind), which the name-giver constantly finds fault with. Now τὸ βουλόμενον ἅπτειν ῥοῦν (that which wishes to fasten the flow) would most correctly be called βουλαπτεροῦν, but is called βλαβερόν merely, as I think, to make it prettier. Elaborate names these are, Socrates, that result from your method. Just now,

greek_head: βουλαπτεροῦν Οὐκ ἐγώ ὦ Ἑρμόγενες αἴτιος ἀλλ' οἱ θέμενοι
greek_tokens: boulapteroun ou egw eimi ermogenhs aitios alla e tiqhmi o onoma alhqhs legw alla dh o zhmiwdhs tis an eaw tis de an eaw pote zhmiwdhs qeaomai eimi ermogenhs ws egw alhqhs legos legos ostis prostiqhmi gramma kai ecairew sfodra alloiow o o onoma dianoia outws wste mikros panu parastrefw eniote enantios
english_head: when you pronounced βουλαπτεροῦν, you looked as if you had made up your mouth to whistle the flute-prelude of the hymn to Athena. Not I, Hermogenes, am responsible, but those who gave the name. True. Well, what is the origin of ζημιῶδες? What can the origin of ζημιῶδες be? See, Hermogenes, how true my words are when I say that by adding and taking away letters people alter the sense of words so that even by very slight changes they sometimes make them mean the opposite of what they meant before; as, for instance,

greek_head: ποιεῖν σημαίνειν οἷον καὶ ἐν τῷ δέοντι ἐνενόησα γὰρ αὐτὸ καὶ ἀνεμνήσθην ἄρτι ἀπὸ τοῦδε ὃ ἔμελλόν σοι ἐρεῖν ὅτι
greek_tokens: poiew shmainw oios kai en o deon ennoew gar autos kai anamimnhskw arti apo ode o mellw su erew ostis o men naus fwnh egw o kalos outos kai enantion peritrepw mhnuw o deon kai o zhmiwdhs afanizw ostis noew o de palaios amfoteros dhlow o boulomai onoma pws legw egw su eraomai oida ostis e palaios e hmeteros o iwta kai o delta eu mala xraomai kai ou hkistos
english_head: in the case of the word δέον (obligation, right), for that just occurred to me and I was reminded of it by what I was going to say to you, that this fine modern language of ours has turned δέον and also ζημιῶδες round, so that each has the opposite of its original meaning, whereas the ancient language shows clearly the real sense of both words. What do you mean? I will tell you. You know that our ancestors made good use of the sounds of iota and delta,

greek_head: αἱ γυναῖκες αἵπερ μάλιστα τὴν ἀρχαίαν φωνὴν σῴζουσι νῦν δὲ ἀντὶ μὲν τοῦ ἰῶτα ἢ εἶ ἢ ἦτα μεταστρέφουσιν ἀντὶ
greek_tokens: o gunh o malista o arxaios fwnh swzw nun de anti men o iwta h ei h hta metastrefw anti de o delta zhta ws dh megaloprephs eimi pws dh oios e men arxaios imera o hmeros kalew e de emera e de nun hmeros eimi outos oida oun ostis monos outos dhlow o arxaios onoma o dianoia o tiqhmi ostis gar asmenos o
english_head: and that is especially true of the women, who are most addicted to preserving old forms of speech. But nowadays people change iota to eta or epsilon, and delta to zeta, thinking they have a grander sound. How is that? For instance, in the earliest times they called day ἱμέρα, others said ἑμέρα, and now they say ἡμέρα. That is true. Only the ancient word discloses the intention of the name-giver, don’t you know? For day comes out of darkness to men; they welcome it and long (ἱμείρουσι) for it,

greek_head: ἀνθρώποις καὶ ἱμείρουσιν ἐκ τοῦ σκότους τὸ φῶς ἐγίγνετο ταύτῃ ὠνόμασαν ἱμέραν
greek_tokens: anqrwpos kai imeirw ek o skotos o faos gignomai outos onomazw imera fainw nun de ge tragwdew oude an katanoew ostis boulomai o hmeros kaitoi tis oiomai ws dh o hmeros hmeros poiew dia outos onomazw autos outws dokew egw kai o ge zugon oida ostis duogon e palaios kalew panu ge kai o men ge zugon oudeis dhlow o de o
english_head: and so they called it ἱμέρα. That is clear. But now ἡμέρα is masquerading so that you could not guess its meaning. Why, some people think day is called ἡμέρα because it makes things gentle (ἥμερα). I believe they do. And you know the ancients called ζυγόν (yoke) δυογόν. Certainly. And ζυγόν conveys no clear meaning,

greek_head: δυοῖν ἕνεκα τῆς δέσεως ἐς τὴν ἀγωγὴν ἐπωνόμασται δυογὸν δικαίως νῦν δὲ ζυγόν καὶ ἄλλα πάμπολλα
greek_tokens: duo eneka o desis eis o agwgh eponomazw duogon dikaios nun de zugon kai allos pampolus outws exis fainw kata tautos toinun proteros men o deon outws legw enantion shmainw pas o peri o agaqos onoma agaqos gar idea eimi o deon fainw desmos eimi kai kwluma fora wsper adelfos eimi o blaberos kai mala eimi swkraths outws fainw alla ou ean o arxaios onoma xraomai o polus
english_head: but the name δυογόν is quite properly given to that which binds two together for the purpose of draught; now, however, we say ζυγόν. There are a great many other such instances. Yes, that is plain. Similarly the word δέον (obligation) at first, when spoken in this way, denotes the opposite of all words connected with the good; for although it is a form of good, δέον seems to be a bond (δεσμός) and hindrance of motion, own brother, as it were, toβλαβερόν. Yes, Socrates, it certainly does seem so. But it does not, if you employ the ancient word,

greek_head: μᾶλλον εἰκός ἐστιν ὀρθῶς κεῖσθαι ἢ τὸ νῦν ἀλλ' ὁμολογήσει τοῖς πρόσθεν ἀγαθοῖς ἐὰν ἀντὶ τοῦ εἶ τὸ ἰῶτα ἀποδιδῷς
greek_tokens: mallon eoika eimi orqos kew h o nun alla omologhsis o prosqen agaqos ean anti o ei o iwta apodidwmi wsper o palaios dieimi gar au shmainw alla ou deon agaqos osper dh epainew kai outws ou enantioomai autos eautou o o onoma tiqhmi alla deon kai wfelimos kai lusitelew kai kerdaleos kai agaqos kai sumferw kai euporos o autos fainw eteros onoma shmainw o diakosmew kai eimi pantaxou egkwmiazw
english_head: which is more likely to be right than the present one. You will find that it agrees with the previous words for good, if instead of the epsilon you restore the iota, as it was in old times for διόν (going through), not δέον, signifies good, which the name-giver praises. And so the giver of names does not contradict himself, but δέον (obligation, right), ὠφέλιμον (useful), λυσιτελοῦν (profitable), κερδαλέον (gainful), ἀγαθόν (good), ξυμφέρον (advantageous), and εὔπορον (prosperous), are plainly identical, signifying under different names the principle of arrangement and motion which has constantly been praised,

greek_head: τὸ δὲ ἴσχον καὶ δοῦν ψεγόμενον καὶ δὴ καὶ τὸ ζημιῶδες ἐὰν κατὰ τὴν ἀρχαίαν φωνὴν ἀποδῷς ἀντὶ
greek_tokens: o de isxw kai dew yegw kai dh kai o zhmiwdhs ean kata o arxaios fwnh apodidwmi anti o zhta delta fainw su kew o onoma epi o dew o eimi eponomazw dhmiwdhs tis de dh hdona kai luph kai epiqumia kai o toioutos eimi swkraths ou panu xalepos egw fainw eimi ermogenhs o te gar hdona o pros o onhsis eoika teinw pracis outos exw o onoma o delta de egkeimai wste hdona
english_head: whereas the principle of constraint and bondage is found fault with. And likewise in the case of ζημιῶδες, if you restore the ancient delta in place of the zeta, you will see that the name, pronounced δημιῶδες, was given to that which binds motion (δοῦντι τὸ ἰόν). What of ἡδονή (pleasure) and λύπη (pain) and ἐπιθυμία (desire), and the like, Socrates? I do not think they are at all difficult, Hermogenes, for ἡδονή appears to have this name because it is the action that tends towards advantage (ἡ πρὸς τὴν ὄνησιν τείνουσα); the delta is inserted, so that we say ἡδονή instead of ἡονή.

greek_head: ἀντὶ ἡονῆς καλεῖται ἥ τε λύπη ἀπὸ τῆς διαλύσεως τοῦ σώματος ἔοικεν ἐπωνομάσθαι ἣν ἐν τούτῳ τῷ πάθει
greek_tokens: anti honh kalew o te luph apo o dialusis o swma eoika eponomazw os en outos o paqos isxw o swma kai o ge ania o empodizw o eimi o de alghdwn cenikos tis fainw egw apo o algeinos onomazw odunh de apo o endusis o luph kalew eoika axqhdwn de kai pas dhlos apeikazw o onoma o o fora baros xara de o diaxusis kai euporia o roh o yuxazw eoika kalew
english_head: Λύπη appears to have received its name from the dissolution (διάλυσις) of the body which takes place through pain. Ἀνία (sorrow) is that which hinders motion (ἰέναι). Ἀλγηδών (distress) is, I think, a foreign word, derived from ἀλγεινός (distressing). Ὀδύνη (grief) appears to be so called from the putting on of pain (τῆς ἐνδύσεως τῆς λύπης). Ἀχθηδών (vexation) has a name, as anyone can see, made in the likeness of the weight (ἄχθος, burden) which vexation imposes upon motion. Χαρά (joy) seems to have its name from the plenteous diffusion (διάχυσις) of the flow of the soul.

greek_head: τέρψις δὲ ἀπὸ τοῦ τερπνοῦ τὸ δὲ τερπνὸν ἀπὸ τῆς διὰ τῆς ψυχῆς ἕρψεως πνοῇ ἀπεικασθὲν κέκληται ἐν δίκῃ
greek_tokens: teryis de apo o terpnos o de terpnos apo o dia o yuxazw eryis pnoh apeikazw kalew en dikh men an erpnoun kalew upo xronos de terpnos paragw eufrosunos de oudeis prosdeomai o dioti erw pas gar dhlos ostis apo o eu o pragma o yuxh sumferw outos lambanw o onoma euferosunh o ge dikaios omws de autos kalew eufrosunos oude epiqumia xalepos o gar epi o qumos eimi
english_head: Τέρψις (delight) is from τερπνόν (delightful); and τερπνόν is called from the creeping (ἕρψις) of the soul, which is likened to a breath (πνοή), and would properly be called ἕρπνουν, but the name has been changed in course of time to τερπνόν. Εὐφροσύνη(mirth) needs no explanation, for it is clear to anyone that from the motion of the soul in harmony (εὖ) with the universe, it received the name εὐφεροσύνη, as it rightfully is; but we call it ευφροσύνη.

greek_head: δυνάμει δῆλον ὅτι τοῦτο ἐκλήθη τὸ ὄνομα θυμὸς δὲ ἀπὸ τῆς θύσεως καὶ ζέσεως τῆς ψυχῆς ἔχοι ἂν τοῦτο τὸ ὄνομα
greek_tokens: dunamis dhlos ostis outos kalew o onoma qumos de apo o qusis kai zesis o yuxazw exw an outos o onoma alla meis imeros ge o malista elkw o yuxh razw
english_head: Nor is there any difficulty about ἐπιθυμία (desire), for this name was evidently given to the power that goes (ἰοῦσα) into the soul (θυμός). And θυμός has its name from the raging (θύσις) and boiling of the soul. The name ἵμερος (longing) was given to the stream (ῥοῦς) which most draws the soul;

greek_head: ἐπωνομάσθη ὅτι γὰρ ἱέμενος ῥεῖ καὶ ἐφιέμενος τῶν πραγμάτων καὶ οὕτω δὴ ἐπισπᾷ σφόδρα τὴν ψυχὴν διὰ τὴν
greek_tokens: eponomazw ostis gar ihmi reomai kai efihmi o pragma kai outws dh epispaw sfodra o yuxh dia o esis o roh apo outos oun pas o dunamis imeros kalew kai meis poqos au kalew shmainw ou o pareimi eimi imeros te kai reuma alla o alloqi pou eimi kai apeimi oqen poqos eponomazw os tote otan pareimi e tis efihmi imeros kalew apogignomai de o autos outos poqos kalew eros de ostis eisrew ecwqen kai ou oikeios eimi o roh
english_head: for because it flows with a rush (ἱέμενος) and with a desire for things and thus draws the soul on through the impulse of its flowing, all this power gives it the name of ἵμερος. And the word πόθος (yearning) signifies that it pertains not to that which is present, but to that which is elsewhere (ἄλλοθί που) or absent, and therefore the same feeling which is called ἵμερος when its object is present, is called πόθος when it is absent. And ἔρως (love) is so called because it flows in (ἐσρεῖ) from without, and this flowing is not inherent in him who has it,

greek_head: αὕτη τῷ ἔχοντι ἀλλ' ἐπείσακτος διὰ τῶν ὀμμάτων διὰ ταῦτα ἀπὸ τοῦ ἐσρεῖν ἔσρος τό γε παλαιὸν ἐκαλεῖτο
greek_tokens: outos o exw alla epeisaktos dia o omma dia outos apo o eisrew esros o ge palaios kalew o gar ou anti o eimi xraomai nun de eros kalew dia o o eimi anti o ou metallagh alla tis eti su legw ostis skopaw doca kai o toioutos ph su fainw doca dh htoi o diwcis eponomazw os o yuxh diwkw o oida oph exis o pragma poreuw h o apo o tocon antibolew eoika de outos mallon o
english_head: but is introduced through the eyes; for this reason it was in ancient times called ἔσρος, from ἐσρεῖν—for we used to employ omicron instead of omega—but now it is called ἔρως through the change of omicron to omega. Well, what more is there that you want to examine? What is your view about δόξα (opinion) and the like? Δόξα is derived either from the pursuit (δίωξις) which the soul carries on as it pursues the knowledge of the nature of things, or from the shooting of the bow (τόξον); the latter is more likely; at any rate οἴησις (belief) supports this view,

greek_head: γοῦν οἴησις τούτῳ συμφωνεῖ οἶσιν γὰρ τῆς ψυχῆς ἐπὶ πᾶν πρᾶγμα οἷόν ἐστιν ἕκαστον τῶν ὄντων δηλούσῃ
greek_tokens: goun oihsis outos sumfwnew eimi gar o yuxazw epi pas pragma oios eimi ekastos o eimi dhlow proseoika wsper ge kai o boulh pws o bolh kai o boulomai o efihmi shmainw kai o bouleuw pas outos doca epomai atta fainw o antibolew apeikasma wsper au kai enantion o aboulia atuxia dokew eimi ws ou ballw oude tugxanw e su ballw kai o boulomai kai peri e bouleuw kai e efihmi
english_head: for it appears to mean the motion (οἶσις) of the soul towards the essential nature of every individual thing, just as βουλή (intention) denotes shooting (βολή) and βούλεσθαι (wish), as well as βουλεύεσθαι (plan), denotes aiming at something. All these words seem to follow δόξα and to express the idea of shooting, just as ἀβουλία (ill-advisedness), on the other hand, appears to be a failure to hit, as if a person did not shoot or hit that which he shot at or wished or planned or desired.

greek_head: Ταῦτα ἤδη μοι δοκεῖς ὦ Σώκρατες πυκνότερα ἐπάγειν
greek_tokens: outos hdh egw dokew eimi swkraths puknos epagw telos gar hdh qew anagkh de oun eti boulomai diaperaw ostis outos echs eimi kai o ekousios o men oun ekousios o eikw kai mh antitupew alla wsper legos eikw o eimi dhlow an eaw outos o onoma o kata o boulhsis gignomai o de anagkaion kai antitupos para o boulhsis eimi o peri o amartia an eaw kai amaqia apeikazw de o kata o
english_head: I think you are hurrying things a bit, Socrates. Yes, for I am running the last lap now. But I think I must still explain ἀνάγκη (compulsion) and ἑκούσιον (voluntary) because they naturally come next. Now by the word ἑκούσιον is expressed the yielding (εἶκον) and not opposing, but, as I say, yielding to the motion which is in accordance with the will; but the compulsory (τὸ ἀναγκαῖον) and resistant, being contrary to the will, is associated with error and ignorance; so it is likened to walking through ravines (ἄγκη),

greek_head: ἄγκη πορείᾳ ὅτι δύσπορα καὶ τραχέα καὶ λάσια ὄντα ἴσχει τοῦ ἰέναι ἐντεῦθεν οὖν ἴσως ἐκλήθη ἀναγκαῖον τῇ διὰ
greek_tokens: agkos poreia ostis dusporos kai traxus kai lasion eimi isxw o eimi enteuqen oun isos kalew anagkaion o dia o agkos apeikazw poreia ews de pareimi o rwmh mh aniaw autos alla kai su mh anihmi alla erwtaw
english_head: because they are hard to traverse, rough, and rugged, and retard motion; the word ἀναγκαῖον may, then, originate in a comparison with progress through a ravine. But let us not cease to use my strength, so long as it lasts and do not you cease from asking questions. I ask, then, about the greatest and noblest words,

greek_head: Ἐρωτῶ δὴ τὰ μέγιστα καὶ τὰ κάλλιστα τήν τε ἀλήθειαν καὶ τὸ ψεῦδος καὶ τὸ ὂν καὶ αὐτὸ
greek_tokens: erwtaw dh o megas kai o kallista o te alhqeia kai o yeudos kai o eimi kai autos outos peri os nun o logos egw eimi onoma dia ostis o onoma exis maiomai oun kalew tis egw o ge zhtew eoika toinun ek logos onoma sugkrotew legw ostis outos eimi eimi e tugxanw zhthma eimi o onoma mallon de an autos gignwskw en os legw o onomastos entauqa gar safhs legw outos eimi eimi e
english_head: truth (ἀλήθεια), falsehood (ψεῦδος), being (τὸ ὄν), and why name, the subject of our whole discourse, has the name ὄνομα. Does the word μαίσθαι (search) mean anything to you? Yes, it means seek. The word ὄνομα seems to be a word composed from a sentence signifying this is a being about which our search is. You can recognize that more readily in the adjective ὀνομαστόν, for that says clearly that this is

greek_head: μάσμα ἐστίν ἡ δ' ἀλήθεια καὶ τοῦτο τοῖς ἄλλοις ἔοικε συγκεκροτῆσθαι ἡ γὰρ θεία τοῦ ὄντος φορὰ ἔοικε προςειρῆσθαι
greek_tokens: masma eimi o de alhqeia kai outos o allos eoika sugkrotew o gar qeia o eimi fora eoika proserew outos o rhma o alhqeia ws qeia eimi alh o de yeudos enantion o fora palin gar au loidorew hkw o isxw kai o anagkazw hsuxazw apeikazw de o kaqeudw o yew de prosgignomai epikruptw o boulhsis o onoma o de eimi kai o ousia omologew o alhqhs o iwta apolambanw eimi gar
english_head: ὄν οὗ μάσμα ἐστίν (being of which the search is). And ἀλήθεια (truth) is like the others; for the divine motion of the universe is, I think, called by this name, ἀλήθεια, because it is a divine wandering θεία ἄλη. But ψεῦδος (falsehood) is the opposite of motion; for once more that which is held back and forced to be quiet is found fault with, and it is compared to slumberers (εὕουσι); but the addition of the psi conceals the meaning of the word. The words τὸ ὄν (being) and οὐσία (existence) agree with ἀληθής with the loss of iota, for they mean going (ἰόν). And οὐκ ὄν (not being) means οὐκ ἰόν (not going),

greek_head: σημαίνει καὶ τὸ οὐκ ὂν αὖ ὥς τινες καὶ ὀνομάζουσιν αὐτό οὐκ ἰόν
greek_tokens: shmainw kai o ou eimi au os tis kai onomazw autos ou eimi outos men egw dokew eimi swkraths andreios panu diakrotew ei de tis su eromai outos o eimi kai o reon kai o dew tis exis orqoths outos o onoma tis an autos apokrinw legw eimi gar panu men oun eis men toinun arti pou porizw wste dokew tis legw apokrinw o poios outos fainw o an mh gignwskw barbarikos tis outos
english_head: and indeed some people pronounce it so. I think you have knocked these words to pieces manfully, Socrates; but if anyone should ask you what propriety or correctness there was in these words that you have employed—ἰόν and ρἕον and δοῦν— What answer should I make? Is that your meaning? Yes, exactly. We acquired just now one way of making an answer with a semblance of sense in it. What way was that? Saying, if there is a word we do not know about, that it is of foreign origin.

greek_head: εἶναι εἴη μὲν οὖν ἴσως ἄν τι τῇ ἀληθείᾳ καὶ τοιοῦτον αὐτῶν εἴη δὲ κἂν ὑπὸ παλαιότητος τὰ πρῶτα τῶν ὀνομάτων
greek_tokens: eimi eaw men oun isos an tis o alhqeia kai toioutos autos eaw de kan upo palaioths o proteros o onoma aneuretos eimi dia gar o pantaxh strefw o onoma oudeis qaumastos an ei o palaios fwnh pros o nuni barbarikos mhdeis diaferw kai oudeis ge apo tropos legw legos gar oun eoika ou mentoi egw dokew profasis agwn dexomai alla proqumhteon outos diaskeptomai enqumeomai de ei tis aei dia os an legw o
english_head: Now this may be true of some of them, and also on account of the lapse of time it may be impossible to find out about the earliest words; for since words get twisted in all sorts of ways, it would not be in the least wonderful if the ancient Greek word should be identical with the modern foreign one. That is not unlikely. It is indeed quite probable. However, we must play the game and investigate these questions vigorously. But let us bear in mind that if a person asks

greek_head: ὄνομα ἐκεῖνα ἀνερήσεται τὰ ῥήματα καὶ αὖθις αὖ δι' ὧν ἂν τὰ ῥήματα λεχθῇ ἐκεῖνα πεύσεται καὶ τοῦτο μὴ παύσεται
greek_tokens: onoma ekeinos aneromai o rhma kai auqis au dia os an o rhma legw ekeinos punqanomai kai outos mh pauw poa ara ou anagkh teleutaw apeipon o apokrinw egw dokew
