english_head: about the words by means of which names are formed, and again about those by means of which those words were formed, and keeps on doing this indefinitely, he who answers his questions will at last give up; will he not? Yes, I think so.

greek_head: Πότε οὖν ἀπειπὼν ὁ ἀπαγορεύων δικαίως παύοιτο ἄν ἆρ' οὐκ ἐπειδὰν ἐπ' ἐκείνοις γένηται τοῖς ὀνόμασιν ἃ
greek_tokens: pote oun apeipon o apagoreuw dikaios pauw an ara ou epeidan epi ekeinos gignomai o onoma o wsper stoixeion o allos eimi kai logos kai onoma outos gar pou ouketi dikaios fainw ek allos onoma sugkeimai os outws exis oios nundi o agaqos fhmi ek o agastos kai ek o qoos sugkeimai o de qoos isos fhmi an ek eteros ekeinos de ek allos
english_head: Now at what point will he be right in giving up and stopping? Will it not be when he reaches the names which are the elements of the other names and words? For these, if they are the elements, can no longer rightly appear to be composed of other names. For instance, we said just now that ἀγαθόν was composed of ἀγαστόν and θοόν; and perhaps we might say that θοόν was composed of other words, and those of still others;

greek_head: ἀλλ' ἐάν ποτέ γε λάβωμεν ὃ οὐκέτι ἔκ τινων ἑτέρων σύγκειται ὀνομάτων δικαίως ἂν φαῖμεν ἐπὶ στοιχείῳ τε
greek_tokens: alla ean pote ge lambanw o ouketi ek tis eteros sugkeimai onoma dikaios an fhmi epi stoixeion te hdh eimi kai ouketi outos egw dew eis allos onoma anaferw egw dokew orqos legw ara oun kai nun o ge erwtaw o onoma stoixeion eimi tugxanw kai deomai autos allos tis tropos hdh o orqoths episkeptomai ostis eimi eoika ge eoika dhta eimi ermogenhs pas goun fainw o
english_head: but if we ever get hold of a word which is no longer composed of other words, we should be right ill saying that we had at last reached an element, and that we must no longer refer to other words for its derivation. I think you are right. Are, then, these words about which you are now asking elements, and must we henceforth investigate their correctness by some other method? Probably. Yes, probably, Hermogenes; at any rate, all the previous words were traced back to these.

greek_head: ἔμπροσθεν εἰς ταῦτα ἀνεληλυθέναι εἰ δὲ τοῦτο οὕτως ἔχει ὥς μοι δοκεῖ ἔχειν δεῦρο αὖ συνεπίσκεψαι μετ' ἐμοῦ μή
greek_tokens: emprosqen eis outos anerxomai ei de outos outws exis os egw dokew exw deuro au sunepiskeptomai meta egw mh tis paralhrew legos oios deomai o o proteros onoma orqoths eimi legos monos ws osos ge dunamis para egw eimi sunepiskeptomai ostis men toinun eis ge tis o orqoths pas onoma kai proteros kai ustatos kai oudeis diaferw o onoma eimi oudeis autos oimh kai sos sundokew panu ge
english_head: But if this be true, as I think it is, come to my aid again and help me in the investigation, that I may not say anything foolish in declaring what principle must underlie the correctness of the earliest names. Go on, and I will help you to the best of my ability. I think you agree with me that there is but one principle of correctness in all names, the earliest as well as the latest, and that none of them is any more a name than the rest. Certainly.

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μὴν ὧν γε νυνδὴ διεληλύθαμεν τῶν ὀνομάτων ἡ ὀρθότης τοιαύτη τις ἐβούλετο εἶναι οἵα δηλοῦν
greek_tokens: alla meis os ge nundi dierxomai o onoma o orqoths toioutos tis boulomai eimi oios dhlow oios ekastos eimi o eimi pws gar ou outos men ara oudeis hsswn kai o proteros deomai exw kai o usteron eiper onoma eimi panu ge alla o men usteron ws eoika dia o proteros oios te eimi outos apergazomai fainw eien o de dh proteros os oupw eteros upokeimai tis tropos kata o dunatos ostis malista faneros egw
english_head: Now the correctness of all the names we have discussed was based upon the intention of showing the nature of the things named. Yes, of course. And this principle of correctness must be present in all names, the earliest as well as the later ones, if they are really to be names. Certainly. But the later ones, apparently, were able to accomplish this by means of the earlier ones. Evidently. Well, then, how can the earliest names, which are not as yet based upon any others, make clear to us the nature of things, so far as that is possible,

greek_head: ποιήσει τὰ ὄντα εἴπερ μέλλει ὀνόματα εἶναι ἀπόκριναι δέ μοι τόδε εἰ φωνὴν μὴ εἴχομεν μηδὲ γλῶτταν ἐβουλόμεθα
greek_tokens: poihsis o eimi eiper mellw onoma eimi apokrinw de egw ode ei fwnh mh exw mhde glwssa boulomai de dhlow allhlwn o pragma ara ou an wsper nun e eneos epixeirew an shmainw o xeir kai kefalh kai o allos swma pws gar an allos eimi swkraths
english_head: which they must do if they are to be names at all? Answer me this question: If we had no voice or tongue, and wished to make things clear to one another, should we not try, as dumb people actually do, to make signs with our hands and head and person generally? Yes. What other method is there, Socrates?

greek_head: Εἰ μέν γ' οἶμαι τὸ ἄνω καὶ τὸ κοῦφον ἐβουλόμεθα δηλοῦν ᾔρομεν ἂν πρὸς τὸν οὐρανὸν τὴν χεῖρα μιμούμενοι
greek_tokens: ei men ge oimh o anoos kai o koufos boulomai dhlow airw an pros o ouranos o xeir mimeomai autos o fus o pragma ei de o katos kai o barus pros o gaia kai ei ippos qew h tis allos o zwion boulomai dhlow oida ostis ws omoios an o hmeteros autos swma kai sxhma epoizw ekeinos anagkh egw dokew ws legw exw outws gar an oimh dhlwma tis swma gignomai
english_head: If we wished to designate that which is above and is light, we should, I fancy, raise our hand towards heaven in imitation of the nature of the thing in question; but if the things to be designated were below or heavy, we should extend our hands towards the ground; and if we wished to mention a galloping horse or any other animal, we should, of course, make our bodily attitudes as much like theirs as possible. I think you are quite right; there is no other way. For the expression of anything, I fancy,

greek_head: μιμησαμένου ὡς ἔοικε τοῦ σώματος ἐκεῖνο ὃ ἐβούλετο δηλῶσαι
greek_tokens: mimeomai ws eoika o swma ekeinos o boulomai dhlow nai epei de fwnew te kai glwssa kai stoma boulomai dhlow ara ou tote ekastos dhlwma egw eimi o apo outos gignomai otan mimhma gignomai dia outos peri otioun anagkh egw dokew onoma ara eimi ws eoika mimhma fwnew ekeinos o mimeomai kai onomazw o mimeomai o fwnew o an mimeomai dokew egw
english_head: would be accomplished by bodily imitation of that which was to be expressed. Yes. And when we wish to express anything by voice or tongue or mouth, will not our expression by these means be accomplished in any given instance when an imitation of something is accomplished by them? I think that is inevitable. A name, then, it appears, is a vocal imitation of that which is imitated, and he who imitates with his voice names that which he imitates. I think that is correct.

greek_head: Μὰ Δί' ἀλλ' οὐκ ἐμοί πω δοκεῖ καλῶς λέγεσθαι ὦ ἑταῖρε
greek_tokens: ma diw alla ou egw pw dokew kalos legw eimi etairos tis dh o o probaton mimeomai outos kai o alektruwn kai o allos zwion anagkazw an omologew onomazw outos o mimeomai alhqhs legw kalos oun exw dokew su ou egw alla tis an eimi swkraths mimhsis eaw o onoma proteros men ws egw dokew ou ean kaqa o
english_head: By Zeus, I do not think it is quite correct, yet, my friend. Why not? We should be obliged to agree that people who imitate sheep and cocks and other animals were naming those which they imitate. Yes, so we should. And do you think that is correct? No, I do not; but, Socrates, what sort of an imitation is a name? In the first place we shall not, in my opinion, be making names, if we imitate things as we do in music,

greek_head: μουσικῇ μιμούμεθα τὰ πράγματα οὕτω μιμώμεθα καίτοι φωνῇ γε καὶ τότε μιμούμεθα ἔπειτα οὐκ ἐὰν ἅπερ ἡ μουσικὴ
greek_tokens: mousikh mimeomai o pragma outws mimeomai kaitoi fwnew ge kai tote mimeomai epeita ou ean o o mousikh mimeomai kai egw mimeomai ou egw dokew onomazw legos de su outos eimi o pragma fwnh kai sxhma ekastos kai xrwma ge polus panu ge eoika toinun ou ean tis outos mimeomai oude peri outos o mimhsis o texnh o onomastikos eimi outos men gar eimi o men mousikh o de grafikos eimi gar nai
english_head: although musical imitation also is vocal; and secondly we shall make no names by imitating that which music imitates. What I mean is this: all objects have sound and shape, and many have color, have they not? Certainly. Well then, the art of naming is not employed in the imitation of those qualities, and has nothing to do with them. The arts which are concerned with them are music and design, are they not? Yes.

greek_head: Τί δὲ δὴ τόδε οὐ καὶ οὐσία δοκεῖ σοι εἶναι ἑκάστῳ ὥσπερ καὶ χρῶμα καὶ ἃ νυνδὴ ἐλέγομεν πρῶτον αὐτῷ τῷ
greek_tokens: tis de dh ode ou kai ousia dokew su eimi ekastos wsper kai xrwma kai o nundi legw proteros autos o xrwma kai o fwnew ou eimi ousia tis ekateros autos kai o allos pas osos aciow outos o prosrhsis o eimi egw dokew tis oun ei tis autos outos mimeomai dunamai ekastos o ousia gramma te kai sullabh ara ou an dhlow ekastos o eimi h ou
english_head: Here is another point. Has not each thing an essential nature, just as it has a color and the other qualities we just mentioned? Indeed, in the first place, have not color and sound and all other things which may properly be said to exist, each and all an essential nature? I think so. Well, then, if anyone could imitate this essential nature of each thing by means of letters and syllables, he would show what each thing really is, would he not?

greek_head: Πάνυ μὲν οὖν Καὶ τί ἂν φαίης τὸν τοῦτο δυνάμενον ὥσπερ τοὺς
greek_tokens: panu men oun kai tis an fhmi o outos dunamai wsper o proteros o men mousikos fhmi o de tis grafikos outos de tis outos egw dokew eimi swkraths osper pala zhtew outos an eimi o onomastikos ei ara outos alhqhs hdh eoika episkepteos peri ekeinos o onoma os su arow peri roh te kai o eimi kai sxesis ei o gramma kai o sullabh
english_head: Certainly. And what will you call him who can do this, as you called the others musician and painter? What will you call this man? I think, Socrates, he is what we have been looking for all along, the name-maker. If that is the case, is it our next duty to consider whether in these names about which you were asking—flow, motion, and restraint—the namemaker grasps with his letters and syllables the reality

greek_head: τοῦ ὄντος ἐπιλαμβάνεται αὐτῶν ὥστε ἀπομιμεῖσθαι τὴν οὐσίαν εἴτε καὶ οὔ
greek_tokens: o eimi epilambanw autos wste apomimeomai o ousia eite kai ou panu men oun ferw dh eidon poteros ara outos monos eimi o proteros onoma h kai allos polus oimh egw kai allos eoika gar alla tis an eaw o tropos o diairesis oqen arxw mimeomai o mimeomai ara ou epei sullabh te kai gramma o mimhsis tugxanw eimi o ousia orqos eimi diairew o stoixeion proteros wsper
english_head: of the things named and imitates their essential nature, or not? Certainly. Well now, let us see whether those are the only primary names, or there are others. I think there are others. Yes, most likely there are. Now what is the method of division with which the imitator begins his imitation? Since the imitation of the essential nature is made with letters and syllables, would not the most correct way be for us to separate the letters first,

greek_head: οἱ ἐπιχειροῦντες τοῖς ῥυθμοῖς τῶν στοιχείων πρῶτον τὰς δυνάμεις διείλοντο ἔπειτα τῶν συλλαβῶν καὶ οὕτως ἤδη
greek_tokens: e epixeirew o ruqmos o stoixeion proteros o dunamis diairew epeita o sullabh kai outws hdh erxomai epi o ruqmos skeptomai proteros de ou nai ara oun kai egw outws deomai proteros men o fwnheis diairew epeita o eteros kata eidos o te afwnos kai afqoggos outwsi gar pou legw e deinos peri outos kai o au fwnheis men ou ou mentoi ge afqoggos kai autos o fwnheis osos diaforos eidos exis allhlwn kai
english_head: just as those who undertake the practice of rhythms separate first the qualities of the letters, then those of the syllables, and then, but not till then, come to the study of rhythms? Yes. Must not we, too, separate first the vowels, then in their several classes the consonants or mutes, as they are called by those who specialize in phonetics, and also the letters which are neither vowels nor mutes, as well as the various classes that exist among the vowels themselves?

greek_head: ἐπειδὰν ταῦτα διελώμεθα τὰ ὄντα εὖ πάντα αὖ οἷς δεῖ ὀνόματα ἐπιθεῖναι εἰ ἔστιν εἰς ἃ ἀναφέρεται πάντα ὥσπερ τὰ
greek_tokens: epeidan outos diairew o eimi eu pas au os deomai onoma epitiqhmi ei eimi eis o anaferw pas wsper o stoixeion ek os eimi eidon autos te kai ei en autos eneimi eidos kata o autos tropos wsper en o stoixeion outos pas kalos diaqeaomai efisthmi epiferw ekastos kata o omoioths ean eis eis deomai epiferw ean sugkerannumi polus eis wsper e zwgrafos boulomai afomoiow eniote men ostreon monos epiferw eniote de
english_head: And when we have made all these divisions properly, we must in turn give names to the things which ought to have them, if there are any names to which they can all, like the letters, be referred, from which it is possible to see what their nature is and whether there are any classes among them, as there are among letters. When we have properly examined all these points, we must know how to apply each letter with reference to its fitness, whether one letter is to be applied to one thing or many are to be combined; just as painters, when they wish to produce an imitation, sometimes use only red,

greek_head: ὁτιοῦν ἄλλο τῶν φαρμάκων ἔστι δὲ ὅτε πολλὰ συγκεράσαντες οἷον ὅταν ἀνδρείκελον σκευάζωσιν ἢ ἄλλο τι τῶν
greek_tokens: otioun allos o farmakon eimi de oste polus sugkerannumi oios otan andreikelon skeuazw h allos tis o toioutos ws an oimh dokazw ekastos o eikwn deomai ekastos farmakon outws dh kai egw o stoixeion epi o pragma epiferw kai eis epi eis e an dokazw dew kai sumpolloi poiew o dh sullabh kalew kai sullabh au suntiqhmi
english_head: sometimes some other color, and sometimes mix many colors, as when they are making a picture of a man or something of that sort, employing each color, I suppose, as they think the particular picture demands it. In just this way we, too, shall apply letters to things, using one letter for one thing, when that seems to be required, or many letters together, forming syllables, as they are called, and in turn combining syllables,

greek_head: ἐξ ὧν τά τε ὀνόματα καὶ τὰ ῥήματα συντίθενται καὶ πάλιν ἐκ τῶν ὀνομάτων καὶ ῥημάτων μέγα ἤδη τι καὶ καλὸν
greek_tokens: ek os o te onoma kai o rhma suntiqhmi kai palin ek o onoma kai rhma megas hdh tis kai kalos kai oloc sunisthmi wsper ekei o zwion o grafikos entauqa o logos o onomastikos h rhtorikos h ostis eimi o texnh mallon de ou egw alla legos ekferw suneqesan men gar outws hper sugkeimai e palaios egw de deomai eiper texnikos efisthmi skopaw autos pas
english_head: and by their combination forming nouns and verbs. And from nouns and verbs again we shall finally construct something great and fair and complete. Just as in our comparison we made the picture by the art of painting, so now we shall make language by the art of naming, or of rhetoric, or whatever it be. No, not we; I said that too hastily. For the ancients gave language its existing composite character; and we, if we are to examine all these matters with scientific ability,

greek_head: οὕτω διελομένους εἴτε κατὰ τρόπον τά τε πρῶτα ὀνόματα κεῖται καὶ τὰ ὕστερα εἴτε μή οὕτω θεᾶσθαι ἄλλως δὲ
greek_tokens: outws diairew eite kata tropos o te proteros onoma kew kai o usteron eite mh outws qeaomai allos de suneirw mh faulos eimi kai ou kaqa odos eimi filos ermogenhs isos nh diw eimi swkraths tis oun su pisteuw sautou oios su an eimi outos outws diairew egw men gar ou polus ara dew egw eaw oun h boulomai outws opws an dunamai kai an mikros tis autos oios su eimi kateidon epixeirew
english_head: must take it to pieces as they put it together and see whether the words, both the earliest and the later, are given systematically or not; for if they are strung together at haphazard, it is a poor, unmethodical performance, my dear Hermogenes. By Zeus, Socrates, may be it is. Well, do you believe you could take them to pieces in that way? I do not believe I could. Then I am sure I could not. Shall we give up then? Or shall we do the best we can and try to see if we are able to understand even a little about them,

greek_head: προειπόντες ὥσπερ ὀλίγον πρότερον τοῖς θεοῖς ὅτι οὐδὲν εἰδότες τῆς ἀληθείας τὰ τῶν ἀνθρώπων δόγματα περὶ αὐτῶν
greek_tokens: proeipon wsper oligos proteros o qeaw ostis oudeis oida o alhqeia o o anqrwpos dogma peri autos eikazw outws de kai nun au eipon egw autos eimi ostis ei men tis xraw dew autos diairew eite allos ostis eite egw outws dew autos diairew nun de o legw kata dunamis dehsis egw peri autos pragmateuomai dokew outos h pws legw panu men oun sfodra egw dokew
english_head: and, just as we said to the gods a while ago that we knew nothing about the truth but were guessing at human opinion about them, so now, before we proceed, shall we say to ourselves that if anyone, whether we or someone else, is to make any analysis of names, he will have to analyze them in the way we have described, and we shall have to study them, as the saying is, with all our might? Do you agree, or not? Yes, I agree most heartily.

greek_head: Γελοῖα μὲν οἶμαι φανεῖσθαι ὦ Ἑρμόγενες γράμμασι καὶ συλλαβαῖς τὰ πράγματα μεμιμημένα κατάδηλα γιγνόμενα
greek_tokens: geloios men oimh fainw eimi ermogenhs gramma kai sullabh o pragma mimeomai katadhlos gignomai omws de anagkh ou gar exw outos beltiwn eis ostis epanaferw peri alhqeia o proteros onoma ei mh ara boulomai wsper e tragwdopoios epeidan tis aforaw epi o mhxanh katafeugw qeos airw kai egw outws eipon apallassw ostis o proteros onoma e qeos eqesan kai dia outos orqos exis ara
english_head: It will, I imagine, seem ridiculous that things are made manifest through imitation in letters and syllables; nevertheless it cannot be otherwise. For there is no better theory upon which we can base the truth of the earliest names, unless you think we had better follow the example of the tragic poets, who, when they are in a dilemma, have recourse to the introduction of gods on machines. So we may get out of trouble by saying that the gods gave the earliest names, and therefore they are right.

greek_head: καὶ ἡμῖν κράτιστος οὗτος τῶν λόγων ἢ ἐκεῖνος ὅτι παρὰ βαρβάρων τινῶν αὐτὰ παρειλήφαμεν εἰσὶ δὲ ἡμῶν ἀρχαιότεροι
greek_tokens: kai egw kratistos outos o logos h ekeinos ostis para barbaros tis autos paralambanw eimi de egw arxaios barbaros h ostis upo palaioths adunatos autos
english_head: Is that the best theory for us? Or perhaps this one, that we got the earliest names from some foreign folk and the foreigners are more ancient than we are? Or that it is impossible to investigate them because of their antiquity, as is also the case with the foreign words?

greek_head: ἐπισκέψασθαι ὥσπερ καὶ τὰ βαρβαρικά αὗται γὰρ ἂν πᾶσαι ἐκδύσεις εἶεν καὶ μάλα κομψαὶ τῷ μὴ ἐθέλοντι λόγον
greek_tokens: episkeptomai wsper kai o barbarikos outos gar an paomai ekdusis eien kai mala komyos o mh eqelw logos didwmi peri o proteros onoma ws orqos kew kaitoi ostis tis tropos o proteros onoma o orqoths mh oida adunatos pou o ge usteron oida o ek ekeinos anagkh dhlow os tis peri mhdeis oida alla dhlos ostis o faskw peri autos texnikos eimi peri o proteros
english_head: All these are merely very clever evasions on the part of those who refuse to offer any rational theory of the correctness of the earliest names. And yet if anyone is, no matter why, ignorant of the correctness of the earliest names, he cannot know about that of the later, since they can be explained only by means of the earliest, about which he is ignorant. No, it is clear that anyone who claims to have scientific knowledge of names must be able first of all to explain the earliest names perfectly,

greek_head: ὀνομάτων μάλιστά τε καὶ καθαρώτατα δεῖ ἔχειν ἀποδεῖξαι ἢ εὖ εἰδέναι ὅτι τά γε ὕστερα ἤδη φλυαρήσει ἢ σοὶ ἄλλως
greek_tokens: onoma malista te kai kaqaros deomai exw apodeiknumi h eu oida ostis o ge usteron hdh fluarew h sos allos dokew oude opwsoun eimi swkraths allos o men toinun egw aisqanomai peri o proteros onoma panu egw dokew ubristikos eimi kai geloios outos oun su metadidwmi an boulomai su de an tis exw beltiwn poqen lambanw peiraw kai egw metadidwmi poiew outos alla qarsos legos
english_head: or he can be sure that what he says about the later will be nonsense. Or do you disagree? No, Socrates, not in the least. Now I think my notions about the earliest names are quite outrageous and ridiculous. I will impart them to you, if you like; if you can find anything better, please try to impart it to me. I will do so. Go on, and do not be afraid.

greek_head: Πρῶτον μὲν τοίνυν τὸ ῥῶ ἔμοιγε φαίνεται ὥσπερ ὄργανον εἶναι πάσης τῆς κινήσεως ἣν οὐδ' εἴπομεν δι' ὅτι
greek_tokens: proteros men toinun o razw egw fainw wsper organon eimi pas o kinhsis os oude eipon dia ostis exis outos onoma alla gar dhlos ostis esis boulomai eimi ou gar hta xraomai alla ei o palaios o de arxh apo o kiw cenikos de onoma outos de eimi eimi ei oun tis o palaios autos euriskw onoma eis o hmeteros fwnh sumbainw esis an orqos kalew nun de apo te o cenikos o kiw kai apo o o hta metaboleus kai o o nun enqesis kinhsis kalew dew
english_head: First, then, the letter rho seems to me to be an instrument expressing all motion. We have not as yet said why motion has the name κίνησις; but it evidently should be ἴεσις, for in old times we did not employ eta, but epsilon. And the beginning of κίνησις is from κίειν, a foreign word equivalent to ἰέναι (go). So we should find that the ancient word corresponding to our modern form would be ἴεσις; but now by the employment of the foreign word κίειν, change of epsilon to eta, and the insertion of nu it has become κίνησις, though it ought to be κιείνεσις or εἶσις.

greek_head: δὲ κιείνησιν καλεῖσθαι ἢ εἶσιν ἡ δὲ στάσις ἀπόφασις τοῦ ἰέναι βούλεται εἶναι διὰ δὲ τὸν καλλωπισμὸν στάσις
greek_tokens: de kieinhsis kalew h eiseimi o de stasis apofasis o eimi boulomai eimi dia de o kallwpismos stasis onomazw o de oun razw o stoixeion wsper legos kalos dokew organon eimi o kinhsis o o onoma tiqhmi pros o afomoiow o fora pollaxou goun xraomai autos eis autos proteros men en autos o rew kai roh dia outos o gramma o fora mimeomai eita en o
english_head: And στάσις (rest) signifies the negation of motion, but is called στάσις for euphony. Well, the letter rho, as I was saying, appeared to be a fine instrument expressive of motion to the name-giver who wished to imitate rapidity, and he often applies it to motion. In the first place, in the words ῥεῖν (flow) and ῥοή (current) he imitates their rapidity by this letter,

greek_head: τρόμῳ εἶτα ἐν τῷ τρέχειν ἔτι δὲ ἐν τοῖς τοιοῖσδε ῥήμασιν οἷον κρούειν θραύειν ἐρείκειν θρύπτειν
greek_tokens: tromos eita en o trexw eti de en o toiosde rhma oios krouw qrauw ereikw qruptw kermatizw rumbew pas outos o polus apeikazw dia o razw oraw gar oimh o glwssa en outos hkistos menw malista de seiw dio fainw egw outos pros outos kataxraw o de au iwta pros o lepton pas o dh malista dia pas eimi an dia outos o
english_head: then in τρόμος (trembling) and in τρέχειν (run), and also in such words as κρούειν (strike), θραύειν (break), ἐρείκειν (rend), θρύπτειν (crush),κερματίζειν (crumble), ῥυμβεῖν (whirl), he expresses the action of them all chiefly by means of the letter rho; for he observed, I suppose, that the tongue is least at rest and most agitated in pronouncing this letter, and that is probably the reason why he employed it for these words. Iota again, he employs for everything subtle, which can most readily pass through all things.

greek_head: ἰέναι καὶ τὸ ἵεσθαι διὰ τοῦ ἰῶτα ἀπομιμεῖται ὥσπερ γε διὰ τοῦ φεῖ καὶ τοῦ ψεῖ καὶ τοῦ σῖγμα καὶ τοῦ ζῆτα ὅτι
greek_tokens: eimi kai o ihmi dia o iwta apomimeomai wsper ge dia o fei kai o yew kai o sigma kai o zhta ostis pneumatwdhs o gramma pas o toioutos mimeomai autos onomazw oios o yuxros kai o zew kai o seiw kai oloc seismos kai otan pou o fuswdhs mimeomai pantaxou entauqa ws o polus o toioutos gramma epiferw fainw o o onoma tiqhmi o de au o delta sumpiesis kai o tau kai apereisis o glwssa
english_head: Therefore he imitates the nature of ἰέναι (go) and ἵεσθαι (hasten) by means of iota, just as he has imitated all such notions as ψυχρόν (cold, shivering), ζέον (seething), σείεσθαι (shake), and σεισμός (shock) by means of phi, psi, sigma, and zeta, because those letters are pronounced with much breath. Whenever he imitates that which resembles blowing, the giver of names always appears to use for the most part such letters. And again he appears to have thought that the compression and pressure of the tongue in the pronunciation of delta and tau was naturally fitted

greek_head: τὴν δύναμιν χρήσιμον φαίνεται ἡγήσασθαι πρὸς τὴν μίμησιν τοῦ δεσμοῦ καὶ τῆς στάσεως ὅτι δὲ ὀλισθάνει μάλιστα
greek_tokens: o dunamis xrhsimos fainw hgeomai pros o mimhsis o desmew kai o stasis ostis de olisqanw malista en o labda o glwssa kateidon afomoiow onomazw o te leios kai autos o olisqanw kai o liparos kai o kollwdes kai allos pas o toioutos h de olisqanw o glwssa antilambanw o o gamma dunamis o glisxros apomimeomai kai glukus kai gloiwdhs
english_head: to imitate the notion of binding and rest. And perceiving that the tongue has a gliding movement most in the pronunciation of lambda, he made the words λεῖα (level), ὀλισθάναιν (glide) itself, λιπαρόν (sleek), κολλῶδες (glutinous), and the like to conform to it. Where the gliding of the tongue is stopped by the sound of gamma he reproduced the nature of γλισχρόν (glutinous), γλυκύ (sweet), and γλοιῶδες (gluey).

greek_head: τοῦ δ' αὖ νῦ τὸ εἴσω αἰσθόμενος τῆς φωνῆς τὸ ἔνδον καὶ τὰ ἐντὸς ὠνόμασεν ὡς ἀφομοιῶν τοῖς γράμμασι τὰ
greek_tokens: o de au nun o eidomai aisqanomai o fwnew o endon kai o entos onomazw ws afomoiow o gramma o ergon o de au alfa o megas apodidwmi kai o mhkos o hta ostis megas o gramma eis de o goggulos o ou deomai shmeion outos pleistos autos eis o onoma egkerannumi kai allos outws fainw prosbibazw kai kata gramma kai kata sullabh ekastos o eimi shmeion te kai onoma poa o nomoqeths ek de outos o loipas hdh autos outos suntiqhmi apomimeomai outos
english_head: And again, perceiving that nu is an internal sound, he made the words ἔνδον (inside) and ἐντός (within), assimilating the meanings to the letters, and alpha again he assigned to greatness, and eta to length, because the letters are large. He needed the sign Ο for the expression of γόγγυλον (round), and made it the chief element of the word. And in this way the lawgiver appears to apply the other letters, making by letters and syllables a name for each and every thing, and from these names he compounds all the rest by imitation.

greek_head: μοι φαίνεται ὦ Ἑρμόγενες βούλεσθαι εἶναι ἡ τῶν ὀνομάτων ὀρθότης εἰ μή τι ἄλλο Κρατύλος ὅδε λέγει
greek_tokens: egw fainw eimi ermogenhs boulomai eimi o o onoma orqoths ei mh tis allos kratulos ode legw kai meis eimi swkraths polus ge egw pollakis pragma paraxew kratulos wsper kata arxh elegos faskw men eimi orqoths onoma ostis de eimi oudeis safhs legos wste egw mh dunamai oida poteros ekwn h akwn outws asafhs ekastote peri autos legw nun oun
english_head: This, Hermogenes, appears to me to be the theory of the correctness of names, unless, indeed, Cratylus has some other view. Truly, Socrates, as I said in the beginning, Cratylus often troubles me a good deal; he declares that there is such a thing as correctness of names, but does not say clearly what it is; and so I cannot tell whether he speaks so obscurely about it on any given occasion intentionally or unintentionally.

greek_head: μοι ὦ Κρατύλε ἐναντίον Σωκράτους εἰπὲ πότερον ἀρέσκει σοι ᾗ λέγει Σωκράτης περὶ ὀνομάτων ἢ ἔχεις πῃ ἄλλῃ
greek_tokens: egw eimi kratule enantion swkraths eipon poteros areskw su h legw swkratew peri onoma h exis ph allh kallion legw kai ei exis legos ina htoi maqh para swkraths h didaskw egw amfoteros tis de eimi ermogenhs dokew su radios eimi outws taxus manqanw te kai didaskw otioun pragma mh ostis tosoutos o dh dokew en o megas megas eimi
english_head: So now, Cratylus, tell me, in the presence of Socrates, do you like what Socrates says about names, or have you a better theory to propose? And if you have, tell us about it; then you will either learn from Socrates or instruct both him and me. But, Hermogenes, do you think it is an easy matter to learn or teach any subject so quickly, especially so important an one as this, which appears to me to be one of the most important?

greek_head: Μὰ Δί' οὐκ ἔμοιγε ἀλλὰ τὸ τοῦ Ἡσιόδου καλῶς μοι φαίνεται ἔχειν τὸ εἰ καί τις σμικρὸν ἐπὶ σμικρῷ
greek_tokens: ma diw ou egw alla o o hsiodos kalos egw fainw exw o ei kai tis mikros epi mikros katatiqhmi prourgou eimi ei oun kai mikros tis oios su ei pleos poiew mh apokamnw alla euergetew kai swkratew ode dikaios de ei kai egw kai men dh egw kai autos eimi kratule oudeis an isxurizomai os erw h de egw fainw meqo ermogenhs episkeptomai wste outos ge eneka qarsos legos ei tis
english_head: No, by Zeus, I do not. But I think Hesiod is right in saying:If you can only add little to little, it is worth while.Hes. WD 359 So now if you can make even a little progress, do not shirk the trouble, but oblige Socrates—you owe it to him—and me. For that matter, Cratylus, I would not positively affirm any of the things I have said. I merely expressed the opinions which I reached with the help of Hermogenes. So far as I am concerned, you need not hesitate,

greek_head: ἔχεις βέλτιον ὡς ἐμοῦ ἐνδεξομένου εἰ μέντοι ἔχεις τι σὺ κάλλιον τούτων λέγειν οὐκ ἂν θαυμάζοιμι δοκεῖς γάρ μοι
greek_tokens: exis beltiwn ws egw endexomai ei mentoi exis tis su kallion outos legw ou an qaumazw dokew gar egw autos te skeptomai o toioutos kai para allos manqanw ean oun legos tis kallion enos o maqhths peri orqoths onoma kai egw grafw alla men dh eimi swkraths wsper su legw melw su egw peri autos kai isos an su poiew
english_head: and if your view is better than mine, I will accept it. And I should not be at all surprised if it were better; for I think you have not only investigated such matters yourself but have been taught about them by others. So if you have any better theory to propound, put me down as one of your pupils in the course on the correctness of names. Yes, Socrates, I have, as you say, paid attention to these matters, and perhaps I might make you my pupil. However, I am afraid the opposite is the case,

greek_head: μαθητήν φοβοῦμαι μέντοι μὴ τούτου πᾶν τοὐναντίον ᾖ ὅτι μοί πως ἐπέρχεται λέγειν πρὸς σὲ τὸ τοῦ Ἀχιλλέως
greek_tokens: maqhths fobew mentoi mh outos pas enantion eimi ostis egw pws eperxomai legw pros sos o o axilleus o ekeinos en lith pros o aias legw fhmi de aia diogenhs telamwn koiranos laos pas tis egw kata qumos eidomai muqeomai kai egw su eimi swkraths epieikhs fainw kata new xrhsmwdew eite para euqufrwn epipnous gignomai eite kai allos tis mousa pala su eneimi lanqanw
english_head: and I am impelled to say to you what Achilles says in the Prayers to Ajax. He says:Ajax, descendant of Zeus, son of Telamon, chief of thy people,All thou hast uttered is good in my sight and pleases my spirit.Hom. Il. 9.644 f And so, Socrates, your oracular utterances seem to me to be much to my mind, whether you are inspired by Euthyphro or some other Muse has dwelt within you all along without our knowing it.

greek_head: Ὠγαθὲ Κρατύλε θαυμάζω καὶ αὐτὸς πάλαι τὴν ἐμαυτοῦ σοφίαν καὶ ἀπιστῶ δοκεῖ οὖν μοι χρῆναι ἐπανασκέψασθαι
greek_tokens: agaqos kratule qaumazw kai autos pala o emautou sofia kai afisthmi dokew oun egw xrh anaskeptomai tis kai legos o gar ecapataw autos ufh eautou pas xalepos otan gar mhde mikros apostatew alla aei pareimi o ecapataw pws ou deinos deomai dh ws eoika qama metastrefw epi o proerew kai peiraw o ekeinos o poihths blepw ama prossw kai opissw kai dh kai nuni egw eidon tis egw eromai
english_head: My excellent Cratylus, I myself have been marvelling at my own wisdom all along, and I cannot believe in it. So I think we ought to reexamine my utterances. For the worst of all deceptions is self-deception. How can it help being terrible, when the deceiver is always present and never stirs from the spot? So I think we must turn back repeatedly to what we have said and must try, as the poet says, to look both forwards and backwards.Hom. Il. 1.343; 3.109

greek_head: ὀνόματος φαμέν ὀρθότης ἐστὶν αὕτη ἥτις ἐνδείξεται οἷόν ἐστι τὸ πρᾶγμα τοῦτο φῶμεν ἱκανῶς εἰρῆσθαι
greek_tokens: onoma fhmi orqoths eimi outos ostis endeiknumi oios eimi o pragma outos fhmi ikanos erw egw men dokew panu sfodra eimi swkraths didaskalia ara eneka o onoma legw panu ge oukoun fhmi kai outos texnh eimi kai dhmiourgos autos panu ge tis
english_head: Then let us now see what we have said. Correctness of a name, we say, is the quality of showing the nature of the thing named. Shall we call that a satisfactory statement? I am perfectly satisfied with it, Socrates. Names, then, are given with a view to instruction? Certainly. Shall we, then; say that this instruction is an art and has its artisans? Certainly. Who are they?

greek_head: Οὕσπερ σὺ κατ' ἀρχὰς ἔλεγες τοὺς νομοθέτας Πότερον οὖν καὶ ταύτην φῶμεν τὴν τέχνην ἐν τοῖς
greek_tokens: os su kata arxh legw o nomoqeths poteros oun kai outos fhmi o texnh en o anqrwpos eggignomai wsper kai o allos h mh boulomai de legw o toiosde zwgrafos eimi pou e men xeirwn e de ameinwn panu ge oukoun e men ameinwn o eautou ergon kallias parexw o zwion e de faulos kai oikodomos wsautws e men kalliwn o oikia ergazomai e de aisxros nai
english_head: The lawgivers, as you said in the beginning. Shall we declare that this art arises in men like the other arts, or not? What I mean is this: Some painters are better, and others worse, are they not? Certainly. And the better produce better works—that is, their paintings—and the others worse works? And likewise some builders build better houses and others worse? Yes.

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν καὶ νομοθέται οἱ μὲν καλλίω τὰ ἔργα αὑτῶν παρέχονται οἱ δὲ αἰσχίω
greek_tokens: ara oun kai nomoqeths e men kallias o ergon eautou parexw e de aisxros ou egw dokew outos eti ou ara dokew su nomos e men beltiwn e de faulos eimi ou dhta oude dh onoma ws eoika dokew su kew o men xeirwn o de ameinwn ou dhta pas ara o onoma orqos kew osos ge onoma eimi tis oun o kai arti legw ermogenhs ode poteros
english_head: Then do some lawgivers produce better, and others worse works? No; at that point I cease to agree. Then you do not think that some laws are better, and some worse? No, I do not. And you do not, it appears, think that one name is better, and another worse? No, I do not. Then all names are correct? All that are really names. How about the name of our friend Hermogenes,

greek_head: μηδὲ ὄνομα τοῦτο κεῖσθαι φῶμεν εἰ μή τι αὐτῷ Ἑρμοῦ γενέσεως προσήκει ἢ κεῖσθαι μέν οὐ μέντοι ὀρθῶς γε
greek_tokens: mhde onoma outos kew fhmi ei mh tis autos ermhs genesis proshkw h kew men ou mentoi orqos ge oude kew egw dokew eimi swkraths alla dokew kew eimi de eteros outos onoma ouper kai o fusis o o onoma dhlow poteros oude yeudw otan tis fhmi ermogenhs autos eimi mh gar oude outos au eimi o outos fainw ermogenhs eimi ei mh eimi pws legw
english_head: which was mentioned a while ago? Shall we say that it is not his name at all, unless he belongs to the race of Hermes, or that it is his name, but is incorrect? I think, Socrates, that it is not his name at all; it appears to be his, but is really the name of some one else who possesses the nature that makes the name clear. And when anyone says that our friend is Hermogenes, is he not even speaking falsely? For perhaps it is not even possible to say that he is Hermogenes, if he is not. What do you mean? Do you mean to say that it is impossible to speak falsehood at all?

greek_head: Ἆρα ὅτι ψευδῆ λέγειν τὸ παράπαν οὐκ ἔστιν ἆρα τοῦτό σοι δύναται ὁ λόγος συχνοὶ γάρ τινες οἱ λέγοντες
greek_tokens: ara ostis yeudhs legw o parapan ou eimi ara outos su dunamai o logos suxnos gar tis e legw eimi filos kratule kai nun kai pala pws gar an eimi swkraths legos ge tis outos o legw mh o eimi legos h ou outos eimi o yeudhs legw o mh o eimi legw komyos men o logos h kata egw kai kata o emos hlikia eimi etairos omws mentoi eipon egw tososde
english_head: For there are, my dear Cratylus, many who do so, and who have done so in the past. Why, Socrates, how could anyone who says that which he says, say that which is not? Is not falsehood saying that which is not? Your reasoning is too clever for me at my age, my friend. However, tell me this: Do you think it is possible to speak falsehood,

greek_head: πότερον λέγειν μὲν οὐ δοκεῖ σοι εἶναι ψευδῆ φάναι δέ Οὔ μοι δοκεῖ οὐδὲ φάναι
greek_tokens: poteros legw men ou dokew su eimi yeudhs fainw de ou egw dokew oude fainw oude eipon oude proseipon oios ei tis apantaw su epi cenios lambanw o xeir eipon xairw eimi cenos aqhnaios uios smikriwnos ermogenhs outos legw an outos h fhmi an outos h eipon an outos h proseipon an outws sos men ou ermogenhs de ode h oudeis egw men dokew eimi swkraths allos an outos outos fqeggomai
english_head: but not to say it? Neither to speak nor to say it. Nor utter it or use it as a form of address? For instance, if some one should meet you in hospitable fashion, should grasp your hand and say, Well met, my friend from Athens, son of Smicrion, Hermogenes, would he be saying or speaking or uttering or addressing these words not to you, but to Hermogenes—or to nobody? I think, Socrates, the man would be producing sounds without sense. Even that reply is welcome;

greek_head: Ἀλλ' ἀγαπητὸν καὶ τοῦτο πότερον γὰρ ἀληθῆ ἂν φθέγξαιτο ταῦτα ὁ φθεγξάμενος ἢ ψευδῆ ἢ τὸ μέν τι
greek_tokens: alla agaphtos kai outos poteros gar alhqhs an fqeggomai outos o fqeggomai h yeudhs h o men tis autos alhqhs o de yeudos kai gar an kai outos ecarkew yofew egw an fhmi o toioutos math autos eautou kinew wsper an ei tis xalkion kinew krouw ferw dh ean ph diallassw eimi kratule ara ou allos men an fhmi o onoma eimi allos de ekeinos e o onoma eimi egw oukoun kai o onoma omologew mimhma tis eimi o
english_head: for I can ask whether the words he produced would be true, or false, or partly true and partly false. Even that would suffice. I should say that the man in such a case was merely making a noise, going through purposeless motions, as if he were beating a bronze pot. Let us see, Cratylus, if we cannot come to terms somehow. You would agree, would you not, that the name is one thing and the thing of which it is the name is another? Yes, I should. And you agree that the name is an imitation

greek_head: πράγματος Πάντων μάλιστα
greek_tokens: pragma pas malista oukoun kai o zwgrafhma tropos tis allos legw mimhma eimi pragma tis nai ferw dh isos gar egw ou manqanw ostis pote eimi o legw su de taxa an orqos legos eimi dianemw kai prosferw outos amfoteros o mimhma o te zwgrafhma ekeinos o onoma o pragma os mimhma eimi h ou
english_head: of the thing named? Most assuredly. And you agree that paintings also are imitations, though in a different way, of things? Yes. Well then—for perhaps I do not understand, and you may be right—can both of these imitations, the paintings and the names, be assigned and applied to the things which they imitate, or not?

greek_head: Ἔστιν Πρῶτον μὲν δὴ σκόπει τόδε ἆρ' ἄν τις τὴν μὲν
greek_tokens: eimi proteros men dh skopaw ode ara an tis o men o anhr eikwn o andris apodew o de o gunh o gunh kai allos outws panu men oun oukoun kai enantion o men o anhr o gunh o de o gunh o andris eimi kai outos ara oun outos o dianomh amfoteros orqos h o eteros o eteros o an ekastos oimh o proshkw te kai o omoios apodidwmi egw dokew
english_head: They can. First, then, consider this question: Can we assign the likeness of the man to the man and that of the woman to the woman, and so forth? Certainly. And can we conversely attribute that of the man to the woman, and the woman’s to the man? That is also possible. And are these assignments both correct, or only the former? The former. The assignment, in short, which attributes to each that which belongs to it and is like it. That is my view. To put an end to contentious argument between you and me,

greek_head: Ἵνα τοίνυν μὴ μαχώμεθα ἐν τοῖς λόγοις ἐγώ τε καὶ σὺ φίλοι ὄντες ἀπόδεξαί μου ὃ λέγω τὴν τοιαύτην γάρ
greek_tokens: ina toinun mh maxomai en o logos egw te kai su filos eimi apodexomai egw o legos o toioutos gar eimi etairos kalew egw dianomh epi amfoteros men o mimhma o te zwion kai o onoma orqos epi de o onoma pros o orqos kai alhqhs o de eteros o o anomoios dosis te kai epifora ou orqos kai yeudhs otan epi onoma eimi alla opws mh eimi swkraths en men o zwgrafhma
english_head: since we are friends, let me state my position. I call that kind of assignment in the case of both imitations paintings and names—correct, and in the case of names not only correct, but true; and the other kind, which gives and applies the unlike imitation, I call incorrect and, in the case of names, false. But it may be, Socrates, that this incorrect assignment is possible in the case of paintings, and not in the case of names,

greek_head: ᾖ τοῦτο τὸ μὴ ὀρθῶς διανέμειν ἐπὶ δὲ τοῖς ὀνόμασιν οὔ ἀλλ' ἀναγκαῖον ᾖ ἀεὶ ὀρθῶς
greek_tokens: eimi outos o mh orqos dianemw epi de o onoma ou alla anagkaion eimi aei orqos pws legw tis outos ekeinos diaferw ara ou eimi proserxomai andris tis eipon ostis outos eimi sos gramma kai deiknumi autos an men tuxh ekeinos eikwn an de tuxh gunh o de deiknumi legos eis o o ofqalmos aisqhsis kaqisthmi panu ge tis de palin autos outos proserxomai eipon ostis outos eimi sos onoma eimi de pou kai o onoma mimhma wsper o zwgrafhma outos dh legos ara ou an eaw autos
english_head: which must be always correctly assigned. What do you mean? What difference is there between the two? Can I not step up to a man and say to him, This is your portrait, and show him perhaps his own likeness or, perhaps, that of a woman? And by show I mean bring before the sense of sight. Certainly. Well, then, can I not step up to the same man again and say, This is your name? A name is an imitation, just as a picture is.

greek_head: εἰπεῖν ὅτι Τουτί ἐστι σὸν ὄνομα καὶ μετὰ τοῦτο εἰς τὴν τῆς ἀκοῆς αὖ αἴσθησιν καταστῆσαι ἂν μὲν τύχῃ τὸ ἐκείνου
greek_tokens: eipon ostis outos eimi sos onoma kai meta outos eis o o akoh au aisqhsis kaqisthmi an men tuxh o ekeinos mimhma eipon ostis anhr an de tuxh o o qhlus o anqrwpinos genos eipon ostis gunh ou dokew su outos oios su eimi kai gignomai eniote eqelw su eimi swkraths sugxwrew kai eimi outws kalos ge su poa eimi filos ei eimi outos outws oudeis gar deomai nun panu diamaxomai peri autos ei de oun
english_head: Very well; can I not say to him, This is your name, and then bring before his sense of hearing perhaps the imitation of himself, saying that it is a man, or perhaps the imitation of the female of the human species, saying that it is a woman? Do you not believe that this is possible and sometimes happens? I am willing to concede it, Socrates, and grant that you are right. That is a good thing for you to do, my friend, if I am right; for now we need no longer argue about the matter.

greek_head: ἔστι τοιαύτη τις διανομὴ καὶ ἐνταῦθα τὸ μὲν ἕτερον τούτων ἀληθεύειν βουλόμεθα καλεῖν τὸ δ' ἕτερον ψεύδεσθαι εἰ δὲ
greek_tokens: eimi toioutos tis dianomh kai entauqa o men eteros outos alhqeuw boulomai kalew o de eteros yeudw ei de outos outws exis kai eimi mh orqos dianemw o onoma mhde apodidwmi o proshkw ekastos alla eniote o mh proshkw eaw an kai rhma tautos outos poiew ei de rhma kai onoma eimi outws tiqhmi anagkh kai logos
english_head: If, then, some such assignment of names takes place, we will call one kind speaking truth, and the other speaking falsehood. But if this is accepted, and if it is possible to assign names incorrectly and to give to objects not the names that befit them, but sometimes those that are unfitting, it would be possible to treat verbs in the same way. And if verbs and nouns can be assigned in this way, the same must be true of sentences; for sentences are, I conceive, a combination of verbs and nouns.

greek_head: λόγοι γάρ που ὡς ἐγᾦμαι ἡ τούτων σύνθεσίς ἐστιν ἢ πῶς λέγεις ὦ Κρατύλε
greek_tokens: logos gar pou ws oimh o outos sunqesis eimi h pws legw eimi kratule outws kalos gar egw dokew legw oukoun ei gramma au o proteros onoma apeikazw eimi wsper en o zwgrafhma kai pas o proshkw xrwma te kai sxhma apodidwmi kai mh pas au alla enioi elleipw enioi de kai prostiqhmi kai plew kai megas h ou eimi eimi oukoun o men apodidwmi pas kalos o gramma te kai o eikwn apodidwmi o de h prostiqhmi h afairew gramma men kai eikwn ergazomai kai outos alla ponhros
english_head: What do you say to that, Cratylus? I agree; I think you are right. If, then, we compare the earliest words to sketches, it is possible in them, as in pictures, to reproduce all the colors and shapes, or not all; some may be wanting, and some may be added, and they may be too many or too large. Is not that true? Yes, it is. Then he who reproduces all, produces good sketches and pictures, and he who adds or takes away produces also sketches and pictures, but bad ones?

greek_head: Ναί Τί δὲ ὁ διὰ τῶν συλλαβῶν τε καὶ γραμμάτων τὴν
greek_tokens: nai tis de o dia o sullabh te kai gramma o ousia o pragma apomimeomai ara ou kata o autos logos an men pas apodidwmi o proshkw kalos o eikwn eimi outos de eimi onoma ean de mikros elleipw h prostiqhmi eniote eikwn men gignomai kalos de ou wste o men kalos ergazomai eimi o onoma o de kakos isos
english_head: Yes. And how about him who imitates the nature of things by means of letters and syllables? By the same principle, if he gives all that is appropriate, the image—that is to say, the name—will be good, and if he sometimes omits a little, it will be an image, but not a good one; and therefore some names are well and others badly made. Is that not true? Perhaps.

greek_head: Ἴσως ἄρα ἔσται ὁ μὲν ἀγαθὸς δημιουργὸς ὀνομάτων ὁ δὲ κακός
greek_tokens: isos ara eimi o men agaqos dhmiourgos onoma o de kakos nai oukoun outos o nomoqeths eimi onoma nai isos ara nh diw eimi wsper en o allos texnh kai nomoqeths o men agaqos o de kakos ean o emprosqen ekeinos omologew egw eimi outos alla oraw eimi swkraths otan outos o gramma o te alfa kai o bhta kai ekastos o stoixeion o onoma apodidwmi o grammatikos texnh
english_head: Perhaps, then, one artisan of names will be good, and another bad? Yes. The name of such an artisan was lawgiver? Yes. Perhaps, then, by Zeus, as is the case in the other arts, one lawgiver may be good and another bad, if we accept our previous conclusions. That is true. But you see, Socrates, when by the science of grammar we assign these letters—alpha, beta, and the rest—to names,

greek_head: ἐάν τι ἀφέλωμεν ἢ προσθῶμεν ἢ μεταθῶμέν τι οὐ γέγραπται μὲν ἡμῖν τὸ ὄνομα οὐ μέντοι ὀρθῶς ἀλλὰ τὸ
greek_tokens: ean tis afairew h prosqew h metaqew tis ou grafw men egw o onoma ou mentoi orqos alla o parapan oude grafw alla euqus eteros eimi ean tis outos paqh mh gar ou kalos skopaw outws skopaw eimi kratule pws dh isos osos ek tis ariqmew anagkaion eimi h mh eimi pasxw an outos o su legw wsper kai autos o deka h ostis boulomai allos ariqmos ean afairew tis h
english_head: if we take away or add or transpose any letter, it is not true that the name is written, but written incorrectly; it is not written at all, but immediately becomes a different word, if any such thing happens to it. Perhaps we are not considering the matter in the right way. Why not? It may be that what you say would be true of those things which must necessarily consist of a certain number or cease to exist at all, as ten, for instance, or any number you like,

greek_head: προσθῇς ἕτερος εὐθὺς γέγονε τοῦ δὲ ποιοῦ τινος καὶ συμπάσης εἰκόνος μὴ οὐχ αὕτη ᾖ ἡ ὀρθότης ἀλλὰ τὸ
greek_tokens: prosqew eteros euqus gignomai o de poiew tis kai sumpas eikwn mh ou outos eimi o orqoths alla o enantion oude o parapan deomai pas apodidwmi oios eimi os eikazw ei mellw eikwn eimi skopaw de ei tis legos ara an duo pragma eaw toiosde oios kratulos kai kratulos eikwn ei tis qea mh monos o sos xrwma kai sxhma apeikazw wsper e zwgrafos alla kai o entos pas toioutos poiew oios o sos kai malakoths
english_head: if you add or subtract anything is immediately another number; but this is not the kind of correctness which applies to quality or to images in general; on the contrary, the image must not by any means reproduce all the qualities of that which it imitates, if it is to be an image. See if I am not right. Would there be two things, Cratylus and the image of Cratylus, if some god should not merely imitate your color and form, as painters do, but should also make all the inner parts like yours, should reproduce

greek_head: καὶ θερμότητας τὰς αὐτὰς ἀποδοίη καὶ κίνησιν καὶ ψυχὴν καὶ φρόνησιν οἵαπερ ἡ παρὰ σοὶ ἐνθείη αὐτοῖς καὶ ἑνὶ λόγῳ

english_head: the same flexibility and warmth, should put into them motion, life, and intellect, such as exist in you, and in short, should place beside you a duplicate of all your qualities? Would there be in such an event Cratylus and an image of Cratylus, or two Cratyluses? I should say, Socrates, two Cratyluses. Then don’t you see, my friend, that we must look for some other principle of correctness in images and in names, of which we were speaking, and must not insist that they are no longer images

greek_head: ἀπῇ ἢ προσῇ μηκέτι αὐτὴν εἰκόνα εἶναι ἢ οὐκ αἰσθάνῃ ὅσου ἐνδέουσιν αἱ εἰκόνες τὰ αὐτὰ ἔχειν ἐκείνοις ὧν εἰκόνες
greek_tokens: apeimi h proseimi mhketi autos eikwn eimi h ou aisqanomai osos endew o eikwn o autos exw ekeinos os eikwn eimi egw geloios goun eimi kratule upo o onoma pasxw an ekeinos os onoma eimi o onoma ei pas pantaxh autos omoiow dissos gar an pou pas gignomai kai ou an exw autos eipon oudeis oudeteros opoteros eimi o men autos o de onoma alhqhs legw qarsos toinun eimi gennaios ea kai onoma o men eu
english_head: if anything be wanting or be added? Do you not perceive how far images are from possessing the same qualities as the originals which they imitate? Yes, I do. Surely, Cratylus, the effect produced by the names upon the things of which they are the names would be ridiculous, if they were to be entirely like them in every respect. For everything would be duplicated, and no one could tell in any case which was the real thing and which the name. Quite true. Then do not be faint-hearted, but have the courage to admit that one name may be correctly and another incorrectly given;

greek_head: κεῖσθαι τὸ δὲ μή καὶ μὴ ἀνάγκαζε πάντ' ἔχειν τὰ γράμματα ἵνα κομιδῇ ᾖ τοιοῦτον οἷόνπερ οὗ ὄνομά ἐστιν ἀλλ'

english_head: do not insist that it must have all the letters and be exactly the same as the thing named, but grant that an inappropriate letter may be employed. But if a letter, then grant that also a noun in a clause, and if a noun, then also a clause in a sentence may be employed which is not appropriate to the things in question, and the thing may none the less be named and described, so long as the intrinsic quality of the thing named is retained,

greek_head: τῶν στοιχείων ὀνόμασιν εἰ μέμνησαι ἃ νυνδὴ ἐγὼ καὶ Ἑρμογένης ἐλέγομεν

english_head: as is the case in the names of the letters of the alphabet, if you remember what Hermogenes and I were saying a while ago. Yes, I remember. Very well, then. So long as this intrinsic quality is present, even though the name have not all the proper letters, the thing will still be named; well, when it has all the proper letters; badly, when it has only a few of them. Let us, then, grant this, my friend, or we shall get into trouble, like the belated night wanderers in the road at Aegina, and in very truth we shall be found to have arrived too late;

greek_head: τῇ ἀληθείᾳ οὕτω πως ἐληλυθέναι ὀψιαίτερον τοῦ δέοντος ἢ ζήτει τινὰ ἄλλην ὀνόματος ὀρθότητα καὶ μὴ ὁμολόγει

english_head: otherwise you must look for some other principle of correctness in names, and must not admit that a name is the representation of a thing in syllables and letters. For if you maintain both positions, you cannot help contradicting yourself. Well, Socrates, I think what you say is reasonable, and I accept it. Then since we are agreed about this, let us consider the next point. If a name, we say, is to be a good one, it must have the proper letters? Yes.

greek_head: Προσήκει δὲ τὰ ὅμοια τοῖς πράγμασιν Πάνυ γε

english_head: And the proper letters are those which are like the things named? Yes, certainly. That is, then, the method by which wellgiven names are given. But if any name is not well given, the greater part of it may perhaps, if it is to be an image at all, be made up of proper and like letters, but it may contain some inappropriate element, and is on that account not good or well made. Is that our view? I suppose, Socrates, there is no use in keeping up my contention; but I am not satisfied that it can be a name and not be well given. Are you not satisfied that the name is

greek_head: Πότερον τοῦτο οὐκ ἀρέσκει σε τὸ εἶναι τὸ ὄνομα δήλωμα τοῦ πράγματος

english_head: the representation of a thing? Yes. And do you not think it is true that some names are composed of earlier ones and others are primary? Yes. But if the primary names are to be representations of any things, can you suggest any better way of making them representations than by making them as much as possible like the things which they are to represent?

greek_head: ἄλλον ἢ αὐτὰ ποιῆσαι ὅτι μάλιστα τοιαῦτα οἷα ἐκεῖνα ἃ δεῖ δηλοῦν αὐτά ἢ ὅδε μᾶλλόν σε ἀρέσκει ὁ τρόπος ὃν Ἑρμογένης
greek_tokens: allos h autos poiew ostis malista toioutos oios ekeinos o deomai dhlow autos h ode mallon su areskw o tropos os ermogenhs legw kai allos polus o sunqhma eimi o onoma kai dhlow o suntiqhmi prooida de o pragma kai eimi outos orqoths onoma sunqhkh diaferw de oudeis ean tis sunqew wsper nun sugkeimai ean kai enantion epi men os nun mikros megas kalew epi de os megas mikros poteros su o tropos areskw
english_head: Or do you prefer the theory advanced by Hermogenes and many others, who claim that names are conventional and represent things to those who established the convention and knew the things beforehand, and that convention is the sole principle of correctness in names, and it makes no difference whether we accept the existing convention or adopt an opposite one according to which small would be called great and great small? Which of these two theories do you prefer?

greek_head: Ὅλῳ καὶ παντὶ διαφέρει ὦ Σώκρατες τὸ ὁμοιώματι δηλοῦν ὅτι ἄν τις δηλοῖ ἀλλὰ μὴ τῷ ἐπιτυχόντι

english_head: Representing by likeness the thing represented is absolutely and entirely superior to representation by chance signs. You are right. Then if the name is like the thing, the letters of which the primary names are to be formed must be by their very nature like the things, must they not? Let me explain. Could a painting, to revert to our previous comparison, ever be made like any real thing, if there were no pigments out of which the painting is composed,

greek_head: φαρμακεῖα ὅμοια ὄντα ἐξ ὧν συντίθεται τὰ ζωγραφούμενα ἐκείνοις ἃ μιμεῖται ἡ γραφική ἢ ἀδύνατον

english_head: which were by their nature like the objects which the painter’s art imitates? Is not that impossible? Yes, it is impossible. In the same way, names can never be like anything unless those elements of which the names are composed exist in the first place and possess some kind of likeness to the things which the names imitate; and the elements of which they are composed are the letters, are they not? Yes. Then I must now ask you to consider with me the subject which Hermogenes and I discussed a while ago.

greek_head: Ἑρμογένης φέρε καλῶς σοι δοκοῦμεν λέγειν ὅτι τὸ ῥῶ τῇ φορᾷ καὶ κινήσει καὶ σκληρότητι προσέοικεν ἢ οὐ καλῶς

english_head: Do you think I am right in saying that rho is expressive of speed, motion, and hardness, or not? You are right. And lambda is like smoothness, softness, and the other qualities we mentioned? Yes. You know, of course, that we call the same thing σκληρότης (hardness) which the Eretrians call σκληρότηρ? Certainly. Have rho and sigma both a likeness to the same thing, and does the final rho mean to them just what the sigma means to us, or is there to one of us no meaning?

greek_head: Δηλοῖ μὲν οὖν ἀμφοτέροις Πότερον ᾗ ὅμοια τυγχάνει ὄντα τὸ ῥῶ καὶ τὸ σῖγμα

english_head: They mean the same to both. In so far as rho and sigma are alike, or in so far as they are not? In so far as they are alike. And are they alike in all respects? Yes; at least for the purpose of expressing motion equally. But how about the lambda in σκληρότης? Does it not express the opposite of hardness? Well, perhaps it has no right to be there, Socrates; it may be like the cases that came up in your talk with Hermogenes, when you removed or inserted letters where that was necessary. I think you did right; and in this case perhaps we ought to put a rho in place of the lambda.

greek_head: Εὖ λέγεις τί οὖν νῦν ὡς λέγομεν οὐδὲν μανθάνομεν ἀλλήλων ἐπειδάν τις φῇ σκληρόν οὐδὲ οἶσθα σὺ

english_head: Excellent. However, do we not understand one another when anyone says σκληρόν, using the present pronunciation, and do you not now know what I mean? Yes, but that is by custom, my friend. In saying custom do you think you are saying anything different from convention? Do you not mean by convention that when I speak I have a definite meaning and you recognize that I have that meaning? Is not that what you mean?

greek_head: Ναί Οὐκοῦν εἰ γιγνώσκεις ἐμοῦ φθεγγομένου δήλωμα

And even if custom is entirely distinct from convention,

greek_head: συνθήκη οὐκ ἂν καλῶς ἔτι ἔχοι λέγειν τὴν ὁμοιότητα δήλωμα εἶναι ἀλλὰ τὸ ἔθος ἐκεῖνο γάρ ὡς ἔοικε καὶ
greek_tokens: sunqhkh ou an kalos eti exw legw o omoioths dhlwma eimi alla o eqos ekeinos gar ws eoika kai omoios kai anomoios dhlow epei de outos sugxwrew eimi kratule o gar sigh su sugxwrhsis tiqhmi anagkaion pou kai sunqhkh tis kai eqos sumballw pros dhlwsis os dianoeomai legw epei eimi beltistos ei eqelw epi o ariqmos erxomai pinw oiomai exw onoma omoios eis ekastos o ariqmew epiferw ean mh eaw tis
english_head: we should henceforth be obliged to say that custom, not likeness, is the principle of indication, since custom, it appears, indicates both by the like and by the unlike. And since we grant this, Cratylus—for I take it that your silence gives consent—both convention and custom must contribute something towards the indication of our meaning when we speak. For, my friend, if you will just turn your attention to numbers, where do you think you can possibly get names to apply to each individual number on the principle of likeness,

greek_head: τὴν σὴν ὁμολογίαν καὶ συνθήκην κῦρος ἔχειν τῶν ὀνομάτων ὀρθότητος πέρι ἐμοὶ μὲν οὖν καὶ αὐτῷ ἀρέσκει μὲν κατὰ τὸ
greek_tokens: o shs omologia kai sunqhkh kuros exw o onoma orqoths peri egw men oun kai autos areskw men kata o dunatos omoios eimi o onoma o pragma alla mh ws alhqhs o o ermogenhs glisxros eimi o olkh outos o omoioths anagkaion de eimi kai o fortikos outos prosxraomai o sunqhkh eis onoma orqoths epei isos kata ge o dunatos kallista an legw otan h pas h ws pleistos omoios legw outos de eimi proshkw
english_head: unless you allow agreement and convention on your part to control the correctness of names? I myself prefer the theory that names are, so far as is possible, like the things named; but really this attractive force of likeness is, as Hermogenes says, a poor thing, and we are compelled to employ in addition this commonplace expedient, convention, to establish the correctness of names. Probably language would be, within the bounds of possibility, most excellent when all its terms, or as many as possible, were based on likeness, that is to say, were appropriate, and most deficient under opposite conditions.

greek_head: αἴσχιστα δὲ τοὐναντίον τόδε δέ μοι ἔτι εἰπὲ μετὰ ταῦτα τίνα ἡμῖν δύναμιν ἔχει τὰ ὀνόματα καὶ τί φῶμεν αὐτὰ καλὸν
greek_tokens: aisxros de enantion ode de egw eti eipon meta outos tis egw dunamis exis o onoma kai tis fhmi autos kalos apergazomai didaskw egw dokew eimi swkraths kai outos panu aploos eimi os an o onoma efisthmi efisthmi kai o pragma isos gar eimi kratule o toiosde legw ws epeidan tis oida o onoma oios eimi eimi de oionper o pragma
english_head: But now answer the next question. What is the function of names, and what good do they accomplish? I think, Socrates, their function is to instruct, and this is the simple truth, that he who knows the names knows also the things named. I suppose, Cratylus, you mean that when anyone knows the nature of the name—and its nature is that of the thing—he will know the thing also,

greek_head: εἴσεται δὴ καὶ τὸ πρᾶγμα ἐπείπερ ὅμοιον τυγχάνει ὂν τῷ ὀνόματι τέχνη δὲ μία ἄρ' ἐστὶν ἡ αὐτὴ πάντων τῶν ἀλλήλοις
