english_head: since it is like the name, and the science of all things which are like each other is one and the same. It is, I fancy, on this ground that you say whoever knows names will know things also. You are perfectly right. Now let us see what this manner of giving instruction is, to which you refer, and whether there is another method, but inferior to this, or there is no other at all. What do you think?

greek_head: Οὕτως ἔγωγε οὐ πάνυ τι εἶναι ἄλλον τοῦτον δὲ καὶ μόνον καὶ βέλτιστον
greek_tokens: outws egw ou panu tis eimi allos outos de kai monos kai beltistos poteros de kai euresis o eimi o autos outos eimi o o onoma euriskw kai ekeinos euriskw os eimi o onoma h zhtew men kai euriskw eteros dew tropos manqanw de outos pas malista kai zhtew kai euriskw o autos tropos outos kata tautos ferw dh ennoew eimi kratule ei tis zhtew o
english_head: I think there is no other at all; this is both the best and the only method. Do you think this is also the method of discovering realities, and that he who has discovered the names has discovered also the things named; or do you think inquiry and discovery demand another method, and this belongs to instruction? I most certainly think inquiry and discovery follow this same method and in the same way. Let us consider the matter, Cratylus. Do you not see that he who in his inquiry after things follows names

greek_head: πράγματα ἀκολουθοῖ τοῖς ὀνόμασι σκοπῶν οἷον ἕκαστον βούλεται εἶναι ἆρ' ἐννοεῖς ὅτι οὐ σμικρὸς κίνδυνός ἐστιν
greek_tokens: pragma akolouqew o onoma skopaw oios ekastos boulomai eimi ara ennoew ostis ou mikros kindunos eimi ecapataw pws dhlos ostis o tiqhmi proteros o onoma oios ageomai eimi o pragma toioutos tiqhmi kai o onoma os fhmi eimi gar nai ei oun ekeinos mh orqos ageomai eqw de oios ageomai tis oiomai egw o akolouqew autos pasxw allos tis h ecapataw alla mh ou outws exis eimi swkraths alla anagkaion
english_head: and examines into the meaning of each one runs great risks of being deceived? How so? Clearly he who first gave names, gave such names as agreed with his conception of the nature of things. That is our view, is it not? Yes. Then if his conception was incorrect, and he gave the names according to his conception, what do you suppose will happen to us who follow him? Can we help being deceived? But, Socrates, surely that is not the case.

greek_head: ᾖ εἰδότα τίθεσθαι τὸν τιθέμενον τὰ ὀνόματα εἰ δὲ μή ὅπερ πάλαι ἐγὼ ἔλεγον οὐδ' ἂν ὀνόματα εἴη μέγιστον δέ σοι
greek_tokens: eimi oida tiqhmi o tiqhmi o onoma ei de mh osper pala egw elegos oude an onoma eaw megas de su eimi tekmhrion ostis ou sfallw o alhqeia o tiqhmi ou gar an pote outws sumfwnos eimi autos apas h ou ennoew autos legos ws pas kata tautos kai epi tautos gignomai o onoma alla outos men agaqos kratule oudeis eimi apologhma ei gar o proteros sfallw o tiqhmi allos hdh
english_head: He who gave the names must necessarily have known; otherwise, as I have been saying all along, they would not be names at all. And there is a decisive proof that the name-giver did not miss the truth, one which you must accept; for otherwise his names would not be so universally consistent. Have you not yourself noticed in speaking that all names were formed by the same method and with the same end in view? But that, Cratylus, is no counter argument. For if the giver of names erred in the beginning

greek_head: πρὸς τοῦτ' ἐβιάζετο καὶ αὑτῷ συμφωνεῖν ἠνάγκαζεν οὐδὲν ἄτοπον ὥσπερ τῶν διαγραμμάτων ἐνίοτε τοῦ πρώτου σμικροῦ
greek_tokens: pros outos biazw kai eautou sumfwnew anagkazw oudeis atopos wsper o diagramma eniote o proteros mikros kai adhlos yeudos gignomai o loipas pampolus hdh eimi epomai omologew allhlwn deomai dh peri o arxh pas pragma pas andris o polus logos eimi kai o polus skeyis eite orqos eite mh upokeimai ekeinos de ecetazw ikanos o loipas fainw ekeinos epomai ou mentoi alla
english_head: and thenceforth forced all other names into agreement with his own initial error, there is nothing strange about that. It is just so sometimes in geometrical diagrams; the initial error is small and unnoticed, but all the numerous deductions are wrong, though consistent. Every one must therefore give great care and great attention to the beginning of any undertaking, to see whether his foundation is right or not. If that has been considered with proper care, everything else will follow.

greek_head: θαυμάζοιμ' ἂν εἰ καὶ τὰ ὀνόματα συμφωνεῖ αὐτὰ αὑτοῖς πάλιν γὰρ ἐπισκεψώμεθα ἃ τὸ πρότερον διήλθομεν ὡς
greek_tokens: qaumazw an ei kai o onoma sumfwnew autos eautou palin gar episkeptomai o o proteros dierxomai ws o pas eimi te kai ferw kai rew fhmi shmainw egw o ousia o onoma allos tis outws su dokew dhlow
english_head: However, I should be surprised if names are really consistent. Let us review our previous discussion. Names, we said, indicate nature to us, assuming that all things are in motion and flux. Do you not think they do so?

greek_head: Πάνυ σφόδρα καὶ ὀρθῶς γε σημαίνει Σκοπῶμεν δὴ ἐξ αὐτῶν ἀναλαβόντες πρῶτον μὲν
greek_tokens: panu sfodra kai orqos ge shmainw skopaw dh ek autos analambanw proteros men outos o onoma o episthmh ws amfibolos eimi kai mallon eoika shmainw ostis isthmi egw epi o pragma o yuxh h ostis sumperiferw kai orqos eimi wsper nun autos o arxh legw mallon h emballw o ei epeisthmhn alla o embolh poiew anti o en o ei en o iwta epeita o bebaios ostis basis tis eimi kai stasis mimhma alla ou fora
english_head: Yes, and they indicate it correctly. Let us first take up again the word ἐπιστήμη (knowledge) and see how ambiguous it is, seeming to indicate that it makes our soul stand still (ἵστησιν) at things, rather than that it is carried round with them, so it is better to speak the beginning of it as we now do than to insert the epsilon and say ἐπεϊστήμ; we should insert an iota rather than an epsilon. Then take βέβαιον (firm), which expresses position and rest, not motion.

greek_head: ἔπειτα ἡ ἱστορία αὐτό που σημαίνει ὅτι ἵστησι τὸν ῥοῦν καὶ τὸ πιστὸν ἱστὰν παντάπασι σημαίνει ἔπειτα
greek_tokens: epeita o istoria autos pou shmainw ostis isthmi o roos kai o piston isthmi pantapasi shmainw epeita de o mnhmh pas pou mhnuw ostis monh eimi en o yuxw alla ou fora ei de boulomai o amartia kai o sumfora ei kata o onoma tis akolouqhsis fainw tautos o suneimi outos kai episthmh kai o allos pas o peri o spoudaios onoma eti toinun o amaqia kai o akolasia paraplhsios outos fainw o men
english_head: And ἱστορία (inquiry) means much the same, that it stops (ἵστησιν) the flow. And πιστόν (faithful) most certainly means that which stops (ἱστόν) motion. Then again, anyone can see that μνήμη (memory) expresses rest (μονή) in the soul, not motion. On the other hand, ἁμαρτία (error) and ξυμφορά (misfortune), if you consider merely the form of the names, will appear to be the same as σύνεσις (intellect) and ἐπιστήμη and all the other names of good significance. Moreover, ἀμαθία (ignorance) and ἀκολασία (unrestraint) also appear to be like them; for the former, ἀμαθία,

greek_head: γὰρ τοῦ ἅμα θεῷ ἰόντος πορεία φαίνεται ἡ ἀμαθία ἡ δ' ἀκολασία παντάπασιν ἀκολουθία τοῖς πράγμασι
greek_tokens: gar o ama qeaw eimi poreia fainw o amaqia o de akolasia pantapasi akolouqia o pragma fainw kai outws o nomizw epi o kakos onoma eimi omoios an fainw o epi o kalos oimh de kai allos polus an tis euriskw ei pragmateuomai ek os oiomai an au palin o o onoma tiqhmi ou eimi oude ferw alla menw o pragma shmainw
english_head: seems to be τοῦ ἅμα θεῷ ἰόντος πορεία (the progress of one who goes with God), and ἀκολασία seems to be exactly ἀκολουθία τοῖς πράγμασιν (movement in company with things). And so names which we believe have the very worst meanings appear to be very like those which have the best. And I think we could, if we took pains, find many other words which would lead us to reverse our judgement and believe that the giver of names meant that things were not in progress or in motion, but were at rest. But, Socrates, you see that most of the names

greek_head: Ἀλλ' ὦ Σώκρατες ὁρᾷς ὅτι τὰ πολλὰ ἐκείνως ἐσήμαινεν
greek_tokens: alla eimi swkraths oraw ostis o polus ekeinos shmainw tis oun outos eimi kratule wsper yhfos diariqmew o onoma kai en outos eimi o orqoths opoteros an plew fainw o onoma shmainw outos dh eimi alhqhs oukoun eoika ge oude opwsoun eimi filos kai outos men ge autos
english_head: indicate motion. What of that, Cratylus? Are we to count names like votes, and shall correctness rest with the majority? Are those to be the true names which are found to have that one of the two meanings which is expressed by the greater number? That is not reasonable. No, not in the least, my friend.

greek_head: ἐάσωμεν ἐπανέλθωμεν δὲ πάλιν ὅθεν δεῦρο μετέβημεν ἄρτι γὰρ ἐν τοῖς πρόσθεν εἰ μέμνησαι τὸν τιθέμενον τὰ
greek_tokens: eaw epanerxomai de palin oqen deuro metabainw arti gar en o prosqen ei mimnhskw o tiqhmi o onoma anagkaion fhmi eimi oida tiqhmi os tiqhmi poteros oun eti su dokew outws h ou eti eimi kai o o proteros tiqhmi oida fhmi tiqhmi oida ek poios oun onoma h manqanw h euriskw eimi
english_head: Now let us drop this and return to the point at which we digressed. A little while ago, you may remember, you said he who gave names must have known the things to which he gave them. Do you still hold that opinion, or not? I do. And you say that he who gave the first names also knew the things which he named? Yes, he knew them. But from what names had he learned or discovered the things,

greek_head: τὰ πράγματα εἴπερ τά γε πρῶτα μήπω ἔκειτο μαθεῖν δ' αὖ φαμεν τὰ πράγματα καὶ εὑρεῖν ἀδύνατον εἶναι ἄλλως ἢ τὰ
greek_tokens: o pragma eiper o ge proteros mhpw keimai manqanw de au fhmi o pragma kai euriskw adunatos eimi allos h o onoma manqanw h autos eceuriskw oios eimi dokew tis egw legw eimi swkraths tis oun tropos fhmi autos oida tiqhmi h nomoqeths eimi prin kai otioun onoma kew te kai ekeinos oida eiper mh eimi o pragma manqanw alla h ek o onoma
english_head: if the first names had not yet been given, and if we declare that it is impossible to learn or discover things except by learning or ourselves discovering the names? I think there is something in what you say, Socrates. How can we assert that they gave names or were lawgivers with knowledge, before any name whatsoever had been given, and before they knew any names, if things cannot be learned except through their names?

greek_head: Οἶμαι μὲν ἐγὼ τὸν ἀληθέστατον λόγον περὶ τούτων εἶναι ὦ Σώκρατες μείζω τινὰ δύναμιν εἶναι ἢ ἀνθρωπείαν
greek_tokens: oimh men egw o alhqhs logos peri outos eimi eimi swkraths megas tis dunamis eimi h anqrwpeios o tiqhmi o proteros onoma o pragma wste anagkaion eimi autos orqos exw eita oiomai enantios an tiqhmi autos eautou o tiqhmi eimi daimwn tis h qeos h oudeis su dokew arti legw alla mh ou eimi outos o eteros onoma poteros eimi aristos o epi o stasis agw h o epi o fora ou gar pou kata o arti legw plhqw krinw
english_head: I think the truest theory of the matter, Socrates, is that the power which gave the first names to things is more than human, and therefore the names must necessarily be correct. Then, in your opinion, he who gave the names, though he was a spirit or a god, would have given names which made him contradict himself? Or do you think there is no sense in what we were saying just now? But, Socrates, those that make up one of the two classes are not really names. Which of the two, my excellent friend; the class of those which point towards rest or of those that point towards motion? We agreed just now that the matter is not to be determined by mere numbers.

greek_head: Οὔτοι δὴ δίκαιόν γε ὦ Σώκρατες Ὀνομάτων οὖν στασιασάντων καὶ τῶν μὲν φασκόντων
greek_tokens: outoi dh dikaios ge eimi swkraths onoma oun stasiazw kai o men faskw eautou eimi o omoios o alhqeia o de eautou tis eti diakrinw h epi tis erxomai ou gar pou epi onoma ge eteros allos outos ou gar eimi alla dhlos ostis allos atta zhthteos plhn onoma o egw emfanizw aneu onoma opoteros outos eimi alhqhs deiknumi dhlos ostis o alhqeia o eimi
english_head: No; that would not be right, Socrates. Then since the names are in conflict, and some of them claim that they are like the truth, and others that they are, how can we decide, and upon what shall we base our decision? Certainly not upon other names differing from these, for there are none. No, it is plain that we must look for something else, not names, which shall show us which of these two kinds are the true names, which of them, that is to say,

greek_head: Δοκεῖ μοι οὕτω Ἔστιν ἄρα ὡς ἔοικεν ὦ Κρατύλε δυνατὸν μαθεῖν
greek_tokens: dokew egw outws eimi ara ws eoika eimi kratule dunatos manqanw aneu onoma o eimi eiper outos outws exis fainw dia tis allos oun eti prosdokaw an outos manqanw ara dia allos tis h ouper eoika te kai dikaios dia allhlwn ge ei ph suggeneus eimi kai autos dia eautou o gar pou eteros ekeinos kai alloios eteros an tis kai alloios shmainw alla ou ekeinos alhqhs egw fainw legw
english_head: show the truth of things. That is my opinion. Then if that is true, Cratylus, it seems that things may be learned without names. So it appears. What other way is left by which you could expect to know them? What other than the natural and the straightest way, through each other, if they are akin, and through themselves? For that which is other and different from them would signify not them, but something other and different. I think that is true.

greek_head: Ἔχε δὴ πρὸς Διός τὰ δὲ ὀνόματα οὐ πολλάκις μέντοι ὡμολογήσαμεν τὰ καλῶς κείμενα ἐοικότα εἶναι ἐκείνοις
greek_tokens: exw dh pros zeus o de onoma ou pollakis mentoi omologew o kalos keimai eoika eimi ekeinos os onoma kew kai eimi eikwn o pragma nai ei oun eimi men ostis malista dia onoma o pragma manqanw eimi de kai dia autos poteros an eaw kallos kai safhs o maqhsis ek o eikwn manqanw autos te autos ei kalos eikazw kai o alhqeia eis eimi eikwn
english_head: Stop for Heaven’s sake! Did we not more than once agree that names which are rightly given are like the things named and are images of them? Yes. Then if it be really true that things can be learned either through names or through themselves which would be the better and surer way of learning? To learn from the image whether it is itself a good imitation and also to learn the truth which it imitates,

greek_head: ἢ ἐκ τῆς ἀληθείας αὐτήν τε αὐτὴν καὶ τὴν εἰκόνα αὐτῆς εἰ πρεπόντως εἴργασται
greek_tokens: h ek o alhqeia autos te autos kai o eikwn autos ei prepontws ergazomai ek o alhqeia egw dokew anagkh eimi ostis men toinun tropos deomai manqanw h euriskw o eimi megas isos eimi gignwskw h kata egw kai sos agaphtos de kai outos omologew ostis ou ek onoma alla polus mallon autos ek eautou kai maqhteos kai zhthteos h ek o onoma fainw eimi swkraths eti toinun ode skeptomai opws mh egw o
english_head: or to learn from the truth both the truth itself and whether the image is properly made? I think it is certainly better to learn from the truth. How realities are to be learned or discovered is perhaps too great a question for you or me to determine; but it is worth while to have reached even this conclusion, that they are to be learned and sought for, not from names but much better through themselves than through names. That is clear, Socrates. Then let us examine one further point to avoid being deceived by the fact that most of these names tend in the same direction.

greek_head: πολλὰ ταῦτα ὀνόματα ἐς ταὐτὸν τείνοντα ἐξαπατᾷ εἰ τῷ ὄντι μὲν οἱ θέμενοι αὐτὰ διανοηθέντες γε ἔθεντο ὡς ἰόντων
greek_tokens: polus outos onoma eis tautos teinw ecapataw ei o eimi men e tiqhmi autos dianoeomai ge tiqhmi ws eimi apas aei kai rew fainw gar egw kai autos outws dianoeomai o de ei tugxanw ou outws exis alla outos autos te wsper eis tis dinh empitnw kukaw kai egw efelkw prosemballw skeptomai gar eimi qaumasios kratule o egw pollakis oneirwssw poteros fhmi tis eimi autos kalos kai agaqos kai eis ekastos o
english_head: Suppose it should prove that although those who gave the names gave them in the belief that all things are in motion and flux—I myself think they did have that belief— still in reality that is not the case, and the namegivers themselves, having fallen into a kind of vortex, are whirled about, dragging us along with them. Consider, my worthy Cratylus, a question about which I often dream. Shall we assert that there is any absolute beauty, or good, or any other absolute existence,

greek_head: ὄντων οὕτω ἢ μή Ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ ὦ Σώκρατες εἶναι
greek_tokens: eimi outws h mh egw dokew eimi swkraths eimi autos toinun ekeinos skeptomai mh ei proswpon tis eimi kalos h tis o toioutos kai dokew outos pas rew alla autos fhmi o kalos ou toioutos aei eimi oios eimi anagkh ara oun oios te proseipon autos orqos ei aei upecerxomai proteros men ostis ekeinos eimi epeita ostis toioutos h anagkh ama egw legw allos autos euqus gignomai kai upeceimi kai mhketi outws exw anagkh
english_head: or not? I think there is, Socrates. Then let us consider the absolute, not whether a particular face, or something of that sort, is beautiful, or whether all these things are in flux. Is not, in our opinion, absolute beauty always such as it is? That is inevitable. Can we, then, if it is always passing away, correctly say that it is this, then that it is that, or must it inevitably, in the very instant while we are speaking, become something else and pass away and no longer be what it is? That is inevitable.

greek_head: Πῶς οὖν ἂν εἴη τὶ ἐκεῖνο ὃ μηδέποτε ὡσαύτως ἔχει εἰ γάρ ποτε ὡσαύτως ἴσχει ἔν γ' ἐκείνῳ τῷ χρόνῳ
greek_tokens: pws oun an eaw tis ekeinos o mhdepote wsautws exis ei gar pote wsautws isxw en ge ekeinos o xronos dhlos ostis oudeis metabainw ei de aei wsautws exis kai o autos eimi pws an outos ge metaballw h kinew mhdeis ecisthmi o eautou idea oudamos alla meis oude an gignwskw ge upo oudeis ama
english_head: How, then, can that which is never in the same state be anything? For if it is ever in the same state, then obviously at that time it is not changing; and if it is always in the same state and is always the same, how can it ever change or move without relinquishing its own form? It cannot do so at all. No, nor can it be known by anyone.

greek_head: γὰρ ἂν ἐπιόντος τοῦ γνωσομένου ἄλλο καὶ ἀλλοῖον γίγνοιτο ὥστε οὐκ ἂν γνωσθείη ἔτι ὁποῖόν γέ τί ἐστιν ἢ πῶς ἔχον
greek_tokens: gar an epeimi o gignwskw allos kai alloios gignomai wste ou an gignwskw eti opoios ge tis eimi h pws exw gnwsis de dhpou oudeis gignwskw o gignwskw mhdamos exw eimi ws legw alla oude gignwskw eimi fainw eoika eimi kratule ei metapiptw pas xrhma kai mhdeis menos ei men gar autos outos o gnwsis o gnwsis eimi mh metapiptw menw te an aei o gnwsis kai eaw gnwsis ei de kai autos o eidos
english_head: For at the moment when he who seeks to know it approaches, it becomes something else and different, so that its nature and state can no longer be known; and surely there is no knowledge which knows that which is in no state. It is as you say. But we cannot even say that there is any knowledge, if all things are changing and nothing remains fixed; for if knowledge itself does not change and cease to be knowledge, then knowledge would remain, and there would be knowledge; but if the very essence of knowledge changes,

greek_head: μεταπίπτει τῆς γνώσεως ἅμα τ' ἂν μεταπίπτοι εἰς ἄλλο εἶδος γνώσεως καὶ οὐκ ἂν εἴη γνῶσις εἰ δὲ ἀεὶ μεταπίπτει
greek_tokens: metapiptw o gnwsis ama su an metapiptw eis allos eidos gnwsis kai ou an eaw gnwsis ei de aei metapiptw aei ou an eaw gnwsis kai ek outos o logos oute o gignwskw oute o gignwskw an eaw ei de eimi men aei o gignwskw eimi de o gignwskw eimi de o kalos eimi de o agaqos eimi de eis ekastos o eimi ou egw fainw outos omoios eimi o nun egw legw roh
english_head: at the moment of the change to another essence of knowledge there would be no knowledge, and if it is always changing, there will always be no knowledge, and by this reasoning there will be neither anyone to know nor anything to be known. But if there is always that which knows and that which is known—if the beautiful, the good, and all the other verities exist—I do not see how there is any likeness between these conditions of which I am now speaking and flux or motion.

greek_head: οὐδὲν οὐδὲ φορᾷ ταῦτ' οὖν πότερόν ποτε οὕτως ἔχει ἢ ἐκείνως ὡς οἱ περὶ Ἡράκλειτόν τε λέγουσιν καὶ ἄλλοι
greek_tokens: oudeis oude fora outos oun poteros pote outws exis h ekeinos ws e peri hrakleitos te legw kai allos polus mh ou radios eimi episkeptomai oude panu new exw anqrwpos epitrepw onoma eautou kai o eautou yuxh qerapeuw pisteuw ekeinos kai o tiqhmi autos diisxurizomai os tis oida kai eautou te kai o eimi katagignwskw ws oudeis ugihs oudeis alla pas wsper keramion reomai kai atexnos wsper e katarroos nosew
english_head: Now whether this is the nature of things, or the doctrine of Heracleitus and many others is true, is another question; but surely no man of sense can put himself and his soul under the control of names, and trust in names and their makers to the point of affirming that he knows anything; nor will he condemn himself and all things and say that there is no health in them, but that all things are flowing like leaky pots,

greek_head: ἄνθρωποι οὕτως οἴεσθαι καὶ τὰ πράγματα διακεῖσθαι ὑπὸ ῥεύματός τε καὶ κατάρρου πάντα τὰ χρήματα ἔχεσθαι ἴσως
greek_tokens: anqrwpos outws oiomai kai o pragma diakeimai upo reuma te kai katarroos pas o xrhma exw isos men oun dh eimi kratule outws exis isos de kai ou skopaw oun xrh andreios te kai eu kai mh radios apodexomai eti gar neos ei kai hlikia exis skeptomai de ean euriskw metadidwmi kai egw alla poiew outos eu mentoi eimi eimi swkraths ostis oude nuni askeptos exw alla egw skopaw kai
english_head: or believe that all things are just like people afflicted with catarrh, flowing and running all the time. Perhaps, Cratylus, this theory is true, but perhaps it is not. Therefore you must consider courageously and thoroughly and not accept anything carelessly—for you are still young and in your prime; then, if after investigation you find the truth, impart it to me. I will do so. However, I assure you, Socrates, that I have already considered the matter, and after toilsome consideration

greek_head: πράγματα ἔχοντι πολὺ μᾶλλον ἐκείνως φαίνεται ἔχειν ὡς Ἡράκλειτος λέγει
greek_tokens: pragma exw polus mallon ekeinos fainw exw ws hrakleitos legw eis auqis toinun egw eimi etairos didacis epeidan hkw nun de wsper paraskeuazw poreuw eis agros propempw de su kai ermogenhs ode outos eimi eimi swkraths alla kai su peiraw eti ennoew outos hdh
english_head: I think the doctrine of Heracleitus is much more likely to be true. Some other time, then, my friend, you will teach me, when you come back; but now go into the country as you have made ready to do; and Hermogenes here will go with you a bit. Very well, Socrates, and I hope you also will continue to think of these matters.
